  • D. Baclofen
Q65

A 12-year-old boy presents to the Outpatient department with history of progressively increasing difficulty in walking and frequent falls. Physical examination reveals an ataxic gait and nystagmus. All deep tendon reflexes were observed to be absent while the plantar response was 'Extensor'. What is the most likely diagnosis?** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Friedreich's Ataxia
  • B. Subacute Combined Degeneration of Cord (SACD)
  • C. Becker's Muscular Dystrophy
  • D. Tabes Dorsalis
Q66

All of the following are features of Friedreich's Ataxia, Except:** *(AI 1992)

  • A. Progressive Ataxia is the most common presentation
  • B. Cardiomyopathy is a common association
  • C. Diabetes mellitus may be associated
  • D. Extensor plantar with brisk lower limb reflexes.
Q67

Bilateral loss of ankle jerk and extensor plantar response is seen in:** *(AIIMS Dec 98)

  • A. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
  • B. Friedreich's ataxia
  • C. Tabes dorsalis
  • D. Lead poisoning
Q68

Features of Friedreich's Ataxia include all of the following except:** *(AI 1992)

  • A. Progressive weakness
  • B. Absent lower limb reflexes
  • C. Increased tone in lower limbs
  • D. Extensor plantar Response
Q69

Pyramidal tract involvement with absent ankle jerk is seen in:** *(AIIMS May 01)

  • A. Friedreich's ataxia
  • B. Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord
  • C. Lathyrism
  • D. Tabes dorsalis
Q70

Absent ankle jerk and extensor plantar response is found in:** *(PGI June 98)

  • B. Tabes dorsalis
  • C. SACD
  • D. Vit B deficiency
Q71

Which of the following regions of the spinal cord is involved first in Friedreich's Ataxia:** *(AI 1991)

  • A. Dorsal Root ganglion
  • B. Spinal tracts
  • C. Peripheral nerve
  • D. Cerebellum
Q72

Earliest presentation of Friedreich's ataxia is:** *(AIIMS Nov 93)

  • A. Ataxia
  • B. Seizures
  • C. Optic atrophy
  • D. Stuttering
Q73

Vitamin B12 deficiency can give rise to all of the following, except:** *(AI 2005)

  • A. Myelopathy.
  • B. Optic atrophy.
  • C. Peripheral neuropathy.
  • D. Myopathy.
Q74

Subacute Combined Degeneration of Cord is caused due to deficiency of?** *(NBE)

  • A. Vitamin B1
  • B. Vitamin B5
  • C. Vitamin B6
  • D. Vitamin B12
Q75

Subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord is associated with deficiency of which of the following Vitamins?** *(NBE)

  • B. Vitamin B2
Q76

A 65-year-old man presents with anemia, posterior column dysfunction, and Plantar Extensor. Which of the following is the likely cause?** *(NBE)

  • B. Vitamin B1 deficiency
  • C. Vitamin B12 deficiency
  • D. Myopathy
Q77

65 year old man presents with anaemia and posterior column dysfunction, the likely cause is:** *(AIIMS May 95)

  • A. B1-deficit
  • B. B12-deficit
  • C. SSPE
  • D. Multiple sclerosis
Q78

Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord is a sequel of which of the following?** *(NBE)

  • A. Thiamine deficiency
  • B. Tuberculosis of spine
  • C. Cyanocobalamin deficiency
  • D. Syphilis
Q79

All are true about subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord except:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Due to deficiency of vitamin B12
  • B. Posterior column involvement
  • C. Corticospinal involvement
  • D. Absent deep tendon reflexes
Q80

Which of the following is not true about Tabes Dorsalis:** *(NBE)

  • A. Seen in Neurosyphilis
  • B. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk
  • C. Loss of vibration sense
  • D. Acute abdominal pain and visceral symptoms
Q81

A patient known case of syphilis presented with gait disturbances and frequent falls in dark. On examination, he is found to have a high steppage gait. Which Tract is typically involved in this condition?** *(AIIMS Nov 18)

  • A. Posterior column pathway
  • B. Spinocerebellar tract
  • C. Cerebellum
  • D. Vestibular apparatus
Q82

Subacute combined degeneration due to Vit B12 deficiency mainly involves:** *(PGI Dec 03)

  • A. Peripheral nerve
  • B. Corticospinal tract
  • C. Posterior column
Q83

All are features of tabes dorsalis, except:** *(AIIMS Dec 97)

  • B. Argyll Robertson's pupil
  • C. Bladder disturbances
  • D. Hyperreflexia
Q84

Features of Syringomyelia include all of the following, except:** *(PGI 09)

  • A. Dissociative sensory loss
  • B. Bilateral involvement
  • C. Segmental sensory loss
  • D. Wasting of small muscles of hand
  • E. Ascending weakness
Q85

Which of the following sensory loss occurs in syringomyelia:** *(NBE)

  • A. Pinprick & temperature
  • B. Light touch
  • C. Position sense
  • D. Vibration
Q86

Painless burn in hand is a characteristic feature of:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Syringomyelia
  • B. Thalamic syndrome
  • C. Cord compression
  • D. Systemic-lupus-erythematosus
Q87

Cerebral ischemia occurs when cerebral blood flow is less than:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. 10 ml/100g / minute
  • B. 20 ml/100g /minute
  • C. 40 ml/ 100g/minute
  • D. 50 ml/100g/minute
Q88

Most common cause of cerebrovascular accident is:** *(AIIMS 96)

  • A. Embolism
  • B. Arterial thrombosis
  • C. Venous thrombosis
  • D. Haemorrhage
Q89

Commonest cause of cerebrovascular accident is:** *(AI 98)

  • A. Arterial thrombosis
  • B. Venous thrombosis
  • C. Embolism
  • D. Tumour
Q90

Most common cause of embolic stroke is?** *(NBE)

  • A. Intra-Cardiac Thrombi
  • B. Particulate Matter From IV Drug Injections
  • C. Protein C deficiency
  • D. Antiphospholipid syndrome
Q91

Most common cause of stroke in young women in India among OCP users:** *(PGI Dec 98)

  • A. Cortical vein thrombosis
  • B. Moyamoya disease
  • D. HT
Q92

Most common cause of intracranial haemorrhage is:** *(AIIMS Nov 98)

  • A. Sub arachnoid haemorrhage
  • B. Intracerebral hemorrhage
  • C. Subdural haemorrhage
  • D. Extradural haemorrhage
Q93

The commonest cause of Intracerebral Bleed is:** *(AI 1995)

  • A. Thrombocytopenia
  • B. Diabetes
  • D. Berry aneurysm
Q95

The most common location of hypertensive intracranial hemorrhage is:** *(AI 2006)

  • A. Subarachnoid space
  • B. Basal ganglia
  • D. Brainstem
Q96

Which of the following is the most common location of hypertensive hemorrhage?** *(AI 1994, 2003)

  • A. Pons
  • B. Thalamus
  • C. Putamen/external capsule
  • D. Subcortical white matter
Q97

The most common site for hypertensive bleed is?** *(NBE)

  • B. Putamen
  • C. Frontal lobe
  • D. Thalamus
Q98

Which of the following is the most common location of hypertensive hemorrhage?** *(AIIMS Nov 02)

Q100

The most common intracranial site of hypertensive hemorrhage is:** *(AIIMS May 07)

  • A. Basal ganglia
  • B. Brainstem
  • D. Hippocampus
Q101

The common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage is:** *(AI 2006)

  • A. Arterio-venous malformation
  • B. Cavernous angioma
  • C. Aneurysm
Q102

Which of the following is the MOST frequent cause of spontaneous subarachnoid hemorrhage?

  • A. Brain tumor
  • B. Hypertension
  • C. Coagulopathy
  • D. Intracranial aneurysm
Q103

The most common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage is:** *(NBE)

  • A. Arteriovenous malformation
Q104

Most common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage is:** *(AIIMS Nov 98)

  • A. Hypertension
  • B. A.V. malformation
  • C. Berry aneurysm
  • D. Tumours
Q105

Commonest cause of subarachnoid haemorrhage is:** *(AI 1998)

  • A. Rupture of circle of Willis aneurysm
  • B. Rupture of vertebral artery aneurysm
  • C. Rupture of venocomitants of corpus striatum
  • D. Rupture of dural sinusis
Q106

Berry aneurysm is caused by:** *(AIIMS May 2011)

  • A. Degeneration of internal elastic lamina
  • B. Degeneration of tunica media
  • C. Degeneration of muscular layer
  • D. Degeneration of external elastic lamina
Q107

Which is least common site of berry aneurysm:** *(AIIMS Dec95)

  • A. Basilar artery
  • B. Vertebral artery
  • C. Anterior cerebral artery
  • D. Posterior cerebral artery
Q108

The most common site of Berry aneurysm is:** *(AI 1994)

  • A. Junction of anterior communicating artery with anterior cerebral artery
  • B. Junction of posterior communicating artery with internal carotid artery
  • C. Bifurcation of middle cerebral artery
  • D. Vertebral artery
Q109

Most common site for berry aneurysm rupture?** *(NBE)

  • A. Anterior circulation of brain
  • B. Posterior circulation of brain
  • C. Ascending aorta
  • D. Descending aorta
Q110

Rupture of berry aneurysm most commonly results in:** *(NBE)

  • A. Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
  • B. Subdural Hemorrhage
  • C. Extradural Hemorrhage
  • D. Intra-parenchymal Hemorrhage
Q111

A patient with suspected subarachnoid haemorrhage presents with blood isolated in the fourth ventricle on a CT scan. Aneurysmal rupture is likely to have resulted from:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Posterior Inferior Cerebellar Artery Aneurysm
  • B. Anterior Communicating Artery Aneurysm
  • C. Posterior Communicating Artery Aneurysm
  • D. Basilar Artery Tip Aneurysm
Q112

Which of the following is the most common cause of late neurological deterioration in a case of cerebrovascular accident:** *(AIIMS Nov, 2000)

  • A. Rebleeding
  • B. Vasospasm
  • D. Hydrocephalus
Q113

A patient known to have mitral stenosis and atrial fibrillation presents with acute onset of weakness in the left upper limb which recovered completely in two weeks. The most likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Transient Ischemic Attack
  • B. Ischemic stroke
  • C. Hemorrhagic stroke
  • D. Vasculitis
Q114

After a transient ischemic attack chances of stroke is maximum in?** *(NBE)

  • A. 48 hrs
  • B. 3 days
  • C. 3 months
  • D. 1 year
Q115

All of the following statements about Ischemic Penumbra are true, except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Reversible dysfunctional tissue
  • B. Represents the central core of infarction
  • C. Represented by Perfusion diffusion deficit
  • D. Can be salvaged by Reperfusion
Q116

Emboli in 'Hollenhorst plaque' are composed of:** *(NBE)

  • A. Cholesterol
  • B. Calcium
  • C. Platelet-fibrin debris
  • D. Bacteria
Q117

All of the following statements about Moya-Moya disease are true, except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Occlusive disease involving large intracranial arteries
  • B. Mainly seen in Asian children or young adults
  • C. "Puff of smoke" appearance on angiography
  • D. Vascular inflammation is prominent
Q118

'Duret Hemorrhages' are seen in:** *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. Brain
  • B. Kidney
  • C. Heart
  • D. Lung
Q119

A 45 years old hypertensive male presented with sudden onset severe headache, vomiting and neck stiffness. On examination he didn't have any focal neurological deficit. His CT scan showed blood in the Sylvian fissure. The probable diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS May 03)

  • A. Meningitis
  • B. Ruptured aneurysm
  • C. Hypertensive bleed.
  • D. Stroke
Q120

A 45 year old male patient presented in the casualty with two hours history of sudden onset of severe headache associated with nausea and vomiting on clinical examination the patient had neck stiffness and right sided ptosis. Rest of the neurological examination was normal. What is the clinical diagnosis:** *(AIIMS Nov 03)

  • A. Hypertensive brain haemorrhage
  • C. Aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage
  • D. Arteriovenous malformation haemorrhage
Q121

A nondiabetic, non hypertensive adult male develops sudden severe headache with altered sensorium, likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Brain tumour
  • B. Sub arachnoid haemorrhage
  • C. Encephalitis
  • D. Meningitis
Q122

A young female presents with severe headache and neck stiffness of abrupt onset. She says, she has never had such severe headache before. She also complains of associated nausea and photophobia. Likely diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS May 09)

  • A. Subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)
  • C. Viral Encephalitis
Q123

Sudden excruciating headache is most characteristic of:** *(PGI Dec 01)

  • A. SAH
  • B. Aneurysmal bleeding
  • D. Intracerebral hemorrhage
Q124

A patient presented with thunder clap headache followed by unconsciousness and progressive III cranial nerve palsy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis:** *(AIIMS Nov 2010)

  • A. Extradural Hemorrhage
  • B. Aneurysmal Subarachnoid hemorrhage
  • C. Basilar migraine
  • D. Cluster Headache
Q125

A 25-year-old male had severe headache followed by unconsciousness. CSF tap revealed red blood cells. Most likely diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS Dec94)

  • A. Subdural hematoma
  • B. Embolism
  • C. Tumour
  • D. Ruptured aneurysm
Q126

Motor aphasia refers to defect in:** *(AI 1998)

  • A. Peripheral speech apparatus
  • B. Verbal expression
  • C. Auditory comprehension
  • D. Verbal comprehension
Q127

Lesion in inferior frontal gyrus causes:** *(AIIMS Dec 97)

  • A. Defect in articulation
  • B. Incomprehension of written language
  • C. Incomprehension of spoken language
  • D. Motor aphasia
Q128

All of the following are true about Broca's aphasia except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Lesion lies in frontal lobe
  • B. Fluency is impaired
  • C. Neologisms are absent
  • D. Repetition is preserved
Q129

Broca's aphasia is?** *(NBE)

  • A. Fluent aphasia
  • B. Non fluent aphasia
  • C. Sensory aphasia
  • D. Conduction aphasia
Q130

Aphasia which affects the arcuate fibres is called?** *(NBE)

  • A. Global aphasia
  • B. Anomic aphasia
  • C. Conduction aphasia
  • D. Broca's aphasia
Q131

A patient being evaluated for aphasia is unable to repeat sentences correctly or to name objects properly. However, the patient's speech is effortless and melodic. There are frequent errors in word choice and obvious difficulties in comprehension. The remainder of the patient's neurologic examination is normal. Damage in which area of the brain would account for this type of aphasia:** *(AIIMS Nov 99)

  • A. Posterior temporal and parietal lobes, dominant hemisphere
  • B. Frontal and parietal lobes, dominant hemisphere
  • C. Prefrontal and frontal regions, dominant hemisphere
  • D. Posterior parietal and temporal lobes, nondominant hemisphere
Q132

All of the following are Fluent Aphasia's Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Anomic Aphasia
  • B. Wernicke's Aphasia
  • C. Conduction Aphasia
  • D. Broca's Aphasia
Q133

Fluent Aphasia with preserved comprehension and impaired repetition is:** *(NBE)

  • A. Broca's
  • B. Wernicke's
  • C. Anomic
  • D. Conduction
Q134

Which of the following speech indicates damage to the categorical hemisphere:** *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. Normal speech
  • B. Increased speech
  • C. Decreased speech
  • D. Senseless, fluent speech
Q135

A lesion in the paracentral lobule causes:** *(GI June 9)

  • A. Contralateral foot weakness
  • B. Seizures only
  • D. Cognitive loss
Q136

A lesion in the paracentral lobule causes:** *(PGI June 97)

  • B. Memory impairment
  • C. Brocas aphasia
Q137

A patient with injury to paracentral lobule has all of the following features except?** *(AIIMS Nov 18)

  • A. Gait apraxia
  • B. Urinary Incontinence
  • C. Saddle anesthesia
  • D. Fecal incontinence
Q138

All of the following are features of temporal lobe lesions Except:** *(AIIMS Dec 94)

  • A. Wernicke's Aphasia
  • B. Contralateral homonymous superior quadrantanopia
  • C. Auditory hallucinations
  • D. Hemineglect
Q139

Lesions of the medial temporal lobe may produce (select three options):** *(PGI June 99)

  • A. Visual Amnesia
  • B. Auditory Amnesia
  • C. Antegrade Amnesia
  • D. Apraxia
Q140

In a Right handed person, left parietal lobe injury may be associated with all of the following except: *(AI 1992)

  • A. Aphasia
  • B. Acalculia
  • C. Urinary incontinence
  • D. Inferior quadrantic Anopsia
Q141

In isolated Gerstmann's syndrome, the damage is in:** *(NBE)

  • A. Superior parietal lobule in dominant hemisphere
  • B. Inferior parietal lobule in dominant hemisphere
  • C. Superior parietal lobule in non-dominant hemisphere
  • D. Inferior parietal lobule in non-dominant hemisphere
Q142

Gerstmann's Syndrome includes all of the following except?** *(NBE)

  • A. Acalculia
  • B. Aphasia
  • C. Right-left confusion
  • D. Finger anomia
Q143

Paratonic rigidity or gegenhalten results from disease of:** *(NBE)

  • A. Frontal lobes
  • B. Temporal lobes
  • C. Parietal lobes
  • D. Occipital lobes
Q144

In a right handed person, left temporoparietal lobe injury leads to all, Except:** *(AIIMS June 98)

  • C. Acalculia
  • D. Superior quadrantic Anopsia
Q145

Anterior cerebral artery occlusion can cause:** *(AIIMS May 93)

  • A. Urinary retention
  • B. Contralateral lower leg weakness
  • C. Hemianaesthesia of opposite side of face
  • D. Hemianopia
Q146

Hemiplegia is commonly associated with infarction of the area of distribution of the:** *(AI 1994)

  • A. Anterior cerebral artery
  • B. Middle cerebral artery
  • C. Posterior cerebral artery
  • D. Anterior communicating artery
Q147

All of the following are true about anterior choroidal artery syndrome except:** *(AI 2011)

  • A. Hemiparesis
  • B. Hemisensory loss
  • C. Homonymous Hemianopia
  • D. Involvement of anterior limb of internal capsule
Q148

Which of the following is not a usual feature of right middle cerebral artery territory infarct:** *(AIIMS Nov 02)

  • A. Aphasia.
  • B. Hemiparesis.
  • C. Facial weakness.
  • D. Dysarthria.
Q150

Which of the following sites is not involved in a posterior cerebral artery infarct:** *(AI 2011)

  • A. Midbrain
  • C. Temporal lobe
  • D. Anterior Cortex
Q151

All of the following syndromes are caused by involvement of the posterior cerebral artery, except: *(JIPMER 2018)

  • A. Balint Syndrome
  • B. Weber Syndrome
  • C. Dejerine-Roussy Syndrome
  • D. Gerstmann Syndrome
Q152

What is the site of lesion in a patient with Alexia without agraphia?** *(JIPMER 2018)

  • A. Fusiform gyrus
  • B. Inferior occipital gyrus
  • C. Splenium of corpus callosum
  • D. Pons
Q153

Anton's syndrome results due to occlusion of?** *(NBE)

  • A. Bilateral occlusion of distal ACA
  • B. Bilateral occlusion of distal MCA
  • C. Bilateral occlusion of distal PCA
  • D. Bilateral occlusion of vertebral artery
Q154

In Anton's syndrome, patient is often unaware of:** *(NBE)

  • A. Hearing
  • B. Blindness
  • C. Colour vision
  • D. Memory
Q155

Memory impairment occurs in embolism of posterior cerebral artery because of damage to:** *(AIIMS Dec 94)

  • A. Hippocampal gyrus
  • B. Superior temporal gyrus
  • C. Prefrontal gyrus
  • D. Angular gyrus
Q156

A 45 years male presents with hypertension. He has sudden abnormal flinging movements in right upper and lower limbs. Most likely site of haemorrhage is:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Substantia nigra
  • B. Caudate nuclei
  • C. Pons
  • D. Subthalamic nuclei
Q157

A hypertensive individual had a sudden headache and became unconscious within a few minutes. On regaining consciousness, there was complete flaccid hemiplegia with no involvement of upper face, absence of tendon reflexes and a positive Babinski sign. Which one of the following arteries could have ruptured:** *(AIIMS Nov 03)

  • A. Lateral striate branch of middle cerebral
  • B. Medial striate branch of anterior cerebral
  • C. Posterolateral branch from posterior cerebral
  • D. Posterior choroidal branch of posterior cerebral
Q158

The site of lesion in Dejerine-Roussy syndrome is:** *(NBE)

  • A. Cervical spinal cord
  • C. Tegmentum of pons & midbrain
  • D. Lateral medulla
Q159

All of the following Neurophysiological defects are likely to result from right lobe involvement, except:** *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. Visuospatial defects
  • B. Anosognosia
  • C. Dyscalculia
  • D. Spatial Dysgraphia
Q161

All of the following are features of Millard Gubler Syndrome, Except:** *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. 5th cranial nerve palsy
  • B. 6th cranial nerve palsy
  • C. 7th cranial nerve palsy
  • D. Contralateral hemiplegia
Q162

Third nerve palsy with pupillary sparing is seen in all except:** *(AIIMS May 95)

  • B. Aneurysm of posterior communicating artery
  • C. Diabetes mellitus
  • D. Craniopharyngioma
Q163

Which of the following structure is most likely to be compressed by an aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery:** *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. Oculomotor nerve
  • B. Optic nerve
  • C. Trochlear nerve
  • D. Hypophysis cerebri
Q164

Most common nerve involved in intracranial aneurysm is:** *(AI 1998)

  • A. Trochlear
  • B. VII
  • C. VIII
  • D. Oculomotor
Q165

Which of the following artery is likely to be involved in 3rd cranial nerve lesion?** *(NBE)

  • A. Anterior communicating
  • B. Posterior communicating
  • C. Posterior cerebral
  • D. Anterior cerebral
Q166

Triad of Balint's syndrome includes all of the following, except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Opsoclonus
  • B. Optic ataxia
  • C. Simultanagnosia
  • D. Ocular apraxia
Q167

Damage to Auditory Cortex on one side causes:** *(NBE)

  • A. Hearing loss and impaired tonal discrimination on the contralateral side
  • B. Hearing loss and impaired tonal discrimination on the ipsilateral side
  • C. No noticeable hearing loss or loss of tonal discrimination
  • D. Impaired localization of sound on the ipsilateral side
Q168

Lacunar infarcts are caused by:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Lipohyalinosis of penetrating arteries
  • B. Middle carotid artery involvement
  • C. Emboli to anterior circulation
Q169

Lacunar infarcts may manifest all of the following except:** *(PGI)

  • A. Pure sensory weakness
  • B. Pure motor weakness
  • C. Ataxia paresis
  • D. Dysarthria
  • E. Quadriparesis
Q170

All of the following manifestations may be associated with lacunar infarcts, except:** *(PGI)

  • A. Pure motor loss
  • B. Pure sensory loss
  • C. Ataxia
  • E. Quadriplegia
Q171

Which is true about carotid stenosis:** *(GI June 2000)

  • A. Ipsilateral hemiplegia by embolism of MCA
  • B. Bruit indicates severity of stenosis
  • C. Common in External carotid artery
  • D. Aspirin reduces risk of TIA
Q172

Investigation of choice for screening of proximal internal carotid artery stenosis is:** *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. Doppler flow USG
  • B. CT subtraction angiography
  • C. MRI
  • D. Angiography (DSA)
Q173

Lateral Medullary Syndrome is most frequently due to:** *(NBE)

  • A. Vertebral Artery
  • B. Posterior Inferior Cerebellar Artery (PICA)
  • C. Anterior Inferior Cerebellar Artery (AICA)
  • D. Basilar Artery
Q174

Which of the following syndrome occurs due to blockage of PICA?** *(NBE)

  • A. Weber's syndrome
  • B. Parinaud syndrome
  • C. Medial medullary syndrome
  • D. Wallenberg syndrome
Q175

Lesion of posterior inferior cerebellar artery at brain involves/affects all of the following, except:

  • A. Spinal tract of trigeminal nerve
  • B. Tractus Solitarius
  • C. Spinothalamic tract
  • D. Corticospinal tract
Q176

Lateral Medullary Syndrome involves all of the following cranial nerves, Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Vth CN
  • B. IXth CN
  • C. Xth CN
  • D. XII CN
Q177

All are features of Wallenberg's syndrome, Except:** *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • A. Ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature
  • B. Ipsilateral loss of taste sensation
  • C. Ipsilateral loss of posterior column sensations
  • D. Ipsilateral involvement of lower cranial nerves
Q178

Abdul Khan presents with pain, numbness and impaired sensation over half of the face along with ataxia, nystagmus, dysphagia and hoarseness of voice. His pain and thermal sensations over opposite half are impaired. Horner's syndrome is present. Likely cause of the disease is thrombosis of which vessel?** *(AIIMS Nov 01)

  • A. AICA (Anterior inferior cerebellar artery)
  • B. PICA (Posterior inferior cerebellar artery)
  • C. Basilar
  • D. Pontine vessels
Q179

Lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome) is characterized by all, Except:** *(NBE)

  • C. Crossed hemianaesthesia
  • D. Paralysis of tongue
Q180

Clinical features of medial medullary syndrome (select two options):** *(PGI June 05)

  • A. Ipsilateral numbness of arm, trunk
  • B. Horner's syndrome
  • C. Ipsilateral 12th nerve palsy
  • D. Contralateral pyramidal tract sign
Q181

Which of the following is a features of Medial Medullary Syndrome, except:** *(PGI June 07)

  • A. Ipsilateral numbness of face and trunk
  • C. Ipsilateral ataxia
  • D. Contralateral paralysis
Q182

All of the following are true about Weber's syndrome. Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Ipsilateral oculomotor nerve palsy
  • B. Diplopia
  • C. Contralateral hemiplegia
  • D. Ipsilateral facial nerve palsy
Q183

Benedikt's syndrome, all are true except:** *(AI 2007)

  • A. Contralateral tremor
  • B. 3rd nerve palsy
  • C. Involvement of the penetrating branch of the basilar artery
  • D. Lesion at the level of the pons
Q184

Claude's syndrome results due to occlusion of?** *(NBE)

  • D. Basilar artery
Q185

In Claude's syndrome, ataxia is due to involvement of:** *(NBE)

  • A. Cerebellum
  • B. Superior cerebellar peduncle
  • C. Red nucleus
Q186

Millard Gubler syndrome includes the following except:** *(AI 2007)

  • A. 5th nerve palsy
  • B. 6th nerve palsy
  • C. 7th nerve palsy
  • D. Contralateral hemiparesis
Q187

Pontine Stroke is associated with all except:** *(AI 2007)

  • A. Bilateral pin point pupil
  • B. Pyrexia
  • C. Vagal palsy
  • D. Quadriparesis
Q188

The only thrombolytic agent approved for the treatment of acute ischemic stroke is:** *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. Alteplase
  • B. Streptokinase
  • C. Urokinase
  • D. Pro-urokinase
Q189

The only thrombolytic agent approved for the treatment of acute ischemic stroke is:** *(AIIMS Nov

Q190

Target BP before thrombolysis in ischemic stroke is below?** *(NBE)

  • A. 185/110 mm Hg
  • B. 165/100mm Hg
  • C. 145/100 Hg
  • D. 120/80 mm Hg
Q191

Dose of rTPA in ischaemic stroke is?** *(NBE)

  • A. 60 mg
  • B. 90 mg
  • C. 100 mg
  • D. 120 mg
Q192

Cerebral angiography was performed by:** *(NBE)

  • A. Sir Walter Dandy
  • B. George Moore
  • C. Seldinger
  • D. Egas Moniz
Q193

A 60 year old female presented with history left sided stroke. Onset of symptoms were reported since 1-2 hours. Her blood pressure was 170/100 mm. What is the immediate next best step in management of this patient?** *(AIIMS Nov 18)

  • A. Anti-hypertensives
  • B. IV thrombolysis
  • C. Aspirin with Clopidogrel
  • D. Supportive treatment
Q194

Which one of the following agents has been associated with hemorrhagic stroke?** *(AI 2006)

  • A. Phenylpropanolamine
  • B. Terfenadine
Q195

Central Pontine Myelinolysis (cerebral osmotic demyelination) occurs due to rapid correction of:

  • A. Hypernatremia
  • B. Hypercalcemia
  • D. Hyponatremia
Q196

A 60 year old Patient came with history of vomiting for 10 days. He was treated by large volume of fluids over 24 hours followed which patient developed quadriparesis. What is the most likely cause for patient's condition?** *(NBE)

  • A. Brain infarct
  • B. Brainstem injury
  • C. Central pontine myelinolysis
  • D. Rare cause of hypernatremia
Q197

Absence seizures are seen in:** *(AIIMS Dec 98)

  • A. Grand mal epilepsy
  • B. Myoclonic epilepsy
  • C. Petit mal epilepsy
  • D. Hyperkinetic child
Q198

Absence seizures are characterized on EEG by:** *(AI 2003)

  • A. 3 Hz spike & wave
  • B. 1-2 Hz spike & wave
  • C. Generalized poly spikes
  • D. Hypsarrythmia
Q199

A child presents with short episodes of vacant stare several times a day. The vacant episode begins abruptly and the child remains unresponsive during the episode. There is no associated history of aura or postictal confusion and the child is otherwise normal. The likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Grand mal seizures
  • B. Absence seizures
  • C. Complex partial seizures
  • D. Day dreaming
Q200

All of the following are features of absence seizures except:** *(AIIMS May 05)

  • A. Usually seen in childhood
  • B. 3-Hz spike wave in EEG
  • C. Postictal confusion
  • D. Precipitation by hyperventilation
Q201

All of the following statements about Lennox-Gastaut syndrome are true, except?** *(NBE)

  • A. Multiple seizure types
  • B. EEG shows slow (<3 Hz) spike-&-wave discharges
  • C. Impaired cognitive function
  • D. Good prognosis
Q202

All of the following are features of juvenile Myoclonic epilepsy, EXCEPT:** *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. Myoclonus on awakening
  • B. Generalized tonic-clonic seizures
  • C. Automatism
  • D. Absence seizures
Q203

All of the following are true about Juvenile Myoclonic epilepsy, except:** *(PGI Dec 03)

  • A. Focal seizures
  • B. Generalized seizures
  • C. Myoclonus
  • D. Responses to sodium valproate
Q204

Seizures in Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME) are most frequent in?** *(NBE)

  • A. Morning
  • B. Afternoon
  • C. Evening
  • D. Night
Q205

Which of the following drugs is not used in Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy (JME):** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Topiramate
  • B. Zonisamide
  • C. Carbamazepine
  • D. Valproate
Q206

Myoclonic seizure typically seen in:** *(PGI Dec 98)

  • A. SSPE
  • B. Cerebellar lesion
  • C. Pontine lesion
  • D. Thalamic lesion
Q207

Inclusion bodies seen in Progressive Myoclonic Epilepsy:** *(NBE)

  • A. Buschino bodies.
  • B. Lewy bodies.
  • C. Hirano bodies.
  • D. Lafora bodies
Q208

Most common syndrome associated with focal seizures with altered awareness or responsiveness.

  • A. Mesial temporal lobe epilepsy (MTLE)
  • B. Lennox-Gastaut syndrome
  • C. Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME)
Q209

Which of the following is a feature of focal motor seizure:** *(NBE)

  • A. Jacksonian march
  • B. Todd's paralysis
  • C. Epilepsia partialis continua
  • D. Ictal Cry
Q210

The term post traumatic epilepsy refers to seizures occurring:** *(AIIMS Nov 02)

  • A. Within moments of head injury.
  • B. Within 7 days of head injury.
  • C. Within several weeks to months after head injury.
  • D. Many years after head injury.
Q211

Commonest type of seizure in newborn:** *(AI 08)

  • A. Clonic
  • B. Tonic
  • C. Subtle
  • D. Myoclonic
Q212

All of the following factors are associated with a substantially greater risk of developing epilepsy after febrile seizures, Except:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Complex Febrile seizures
  • B. Early age of onset
  • C. Developmental abnormalities
  • D. Positive family History of Epilepsy
Q213

All of the following drugs are used for managing status epilepticus except:** *(AIIMS Nov. 04)

  • A. Phenytoin
  • B. Diazepam
  • C. Thiopentone sodium
  • D. Carbamazepine
Q214

All of the following drugs are used for managing status epilepticus except:** *(AIIMS May 06)

Q215

Ethosuximide is the drug of choice for:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Generalized Tonic clonic seizures
  • B. Complex partial seizures
  • C. Absence seizures
  • D. Myoclonic seizures
Q216

The drug of choice for absence seizure:** *(AIIMS Nov. 06)

  • A. Valproate
  • B. Gabapentin
  • D. Phenytoin
Q217

Which of the following is the drug of choice for absence seizure?** *(NBE)

  • D. Phenobarbitone
Q218

Generalized Tonic clonic status epilepticus, Rx of choice:** *(PGI Dec 03)

  • A. Ethosuximide
  • B. Sodium valproate
  • C. Lamotrigine
  • D. Lorazepam
  • E. Vigabatrin
Q219

A 15 year old boy with epilepsy on treatment with combination of valproate and phenytoin has good control of seizures. Levels of both drugs are in the therapeutic range. All of the following adverse effects can be attributed to valproate except:** *(AI 2004)

  • A. Weight gain of 5 kg
  • B. Serum alanine aminotransferase 150 IU/L
  • C. Rise in serum ammonia level by 20ug/dL
  • D. Lymphadenopathy
Q220

A patient who is being treated for temporal lobe epilepsy (complex partial seizures) and who is having recurrent seizures on his chronic regimen of carbamazepine is given phenobarbital as a second drug. However, the seizures increase in frequency. What is the probable reason for the apparent deleterious effect of adding phenobarbital:** *(AIIMS Nov 99)

  • A. Intracerebral bleeding from worsening bone marrow suppression
  • B. Decreased carbamazepine level
  • C. Decreased stability of CNS neuronal membranes
Q221

All of the following are true about delirium tremens, except:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Visual Hallucinations
  • B. Tremors
  • C. Ophthalmoplegia
  • D. Clouding of consciousness
Q222

All of the following cause subcortical dementia, Except:** *(AIIMS May 09)

  • A. Alzheimer's disease
  • B. Parkinsonism
  • C. HIV encephalopathy
  • D. Progressive Supranuclear palsy
Q223

Which of the following is predominantly involved in Alzheimer's dementia:** *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. Frontal cortex
  • B. Temporo-Parietal cortex
  • C. Fronto-Parietal cortex
  • D. Fronto-Temporal cortex
Q224

All of the following are known predisposing factors for Alzheimer's disease except:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Down-syndrome
  • B. Low education level
  • C. Smoking
  • D. Female sex
Q225

Increased risk of Alzheimer's disease is associated with which of the following APO- Enzymes:

  • A. Apo-E2
  • B. APO-E3
  • C. APO-E4
Q226

A chromosomal anomaly associated with Alzheimer's dementia is:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Trisomy 18
  • B. Patau syndrome.
  • C. Trisomy 21
  • D. Turners syndrome
Q227

All of the following neurotransmitter deficiencies are observed in Alzheimer's disease, Except:

  • A. Acetylcholine
  • B. Serotonin
  • C. Noradrenaline
  • D. Dopamine
Q228

Which of the following is the most important neurotransmitter deficient in the cortex of patients with Alzheimer's Disease:** *(NBE)

  • D. Noradrenaline
Q229

Alzheimer's Disease is associated with:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Delirium
  • B. Delusion
  • C. Dementia
  • D. Depression
Q230

Which of the following is not seen in early onset Alzheimer's Disease:** *(AIIMS Nov 2011)

  • B. Apraxia
  • D. Agnosia
Q231

Which of the following statements about the pathology in Alzheimer's disease is not true:** *(AIIMS Nov 2011)

  • A. Neuritic Plaques are formed of amyloid protein
  • B. Neurofibrillary tangles (NFT) are made of tau protein
  • C. NFTs appear extracellularly before intracellular appearance
  • D. Number of NFTs correlates with dementia
Q232

Which of the following areas of brain is most resistant to Neurofibrillary tangles in Alzheimer's disease:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Entorhinal cortex
  • B. Hippocampus /Temporal lobe
  • C. Lateral Geniculate Body
  • D. Visual Association area
Q233

The following is not a feature of Alzheimer's disease:** *(AI 2004)

  • A. Neurofibrillary tangles
  • B. Senile (neuritic) plaques
  • C. Amyloid Angiopathy
  • D. Lewy bodies
Q234

An 80 year old female presents with progressive loss of memory, difficulty in recalling names, difficulty in speech and inability to perform desired tasks. Which of the following pathological features is likely to be found along with neurofibrillary tangles:** *(AIIMS May 09)

  • A. Beta Amyloid
  • B. Lewy bodies
  • C. Ceramidase
  • D. Pick's bodies
Q235

Which of the following is the commonest neurodegenerative disease?** *(NBE)

  • A. Huntington's Disease (HD)
  • B. Parkinson's disease (PD)
  • C. Wilson's disease (WD)
  • D. Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD)
Q236

Which of the following is not included in the cardinal features of Parkinson's disease (PD):** *(NBE)

  • A. Rest tremor
  • B. Masked facies
  • C. Bradykinesia
  • D. Gait impairment
Q237

Which of the following is not seen in Parkinsonism:** *(AI 1996)

  • A. Preserved postural reflexes
  • B. Hypokinesia
  • D. Static tremors
Q238

Dinesh, a 56yr aged man presents with complaints of slowness of movements, postural instability, tremors, rigidity and memory loss. Most likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Multi infarct dementia
  • B. Alzheimer's disease
  • C. Parkinsonism
Q239

In parkinsonism what is not present:** *(AIIMS May 94)

  • A. Tremors at rest
  • B. Past pointing
  • C. Akinesia
  • D. Rigidity
Q240

Parkinsonism is characterised by all of the following characteristic features except:** *(AI 1997)

  • A. Rigidity
  • B. Akinesia
  • C. Tremors at rest
  • D. Past pointing
Q241

Wheel chair sign of Quinn is used as a typical Red Flag Sign in:** *(NBE)

  • A. Idiopathic Parkinsonism
  • B. Duchenne muscular dystrophy
  • C. Becker's dystrophy
  • D. Thomson disease
Q242

Which of the following metal ions is associated with secondary Parkinsonisms:** *(NBE)

  • A. Manganese (Mn)
  • B. Magnesium (Mg)
  • C. Selenium (Se)
  • D. Molybdenum (Mo)
Q243

Which of the following enzymes is believed to be deficient in Parkinsonism?** *(NBE)

  • A. DOPA Decarboxylase
  • B. Tyrosine Hydroxylase
  • C. Phenylalanine Hydroxylase
  • D. Dopamine oxidase
Q244

Pathological hallmark feature of Parkinson's disease include all of the following, except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in SNc
  • B. Reduced striatal dopamine
  • C. Intracytoplasmic proteinaceous inclusions (Lewy bodies)
  • D. Globose tangles
Q245

Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation of which part of the brain has been shown to reduce frequency of symptoms in Parkinsonism:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Striatus
  • B. Globus Pallidus Externus
  • C. Subthalamic nucleus
  • D. Putamen
Q246

Which of the following is the most commonly used site for Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation to reduce frequency of Parkinsonism symptoms:** *(AI 2012)

  • B. Globus Pallidus
  • C. Subthalamic Nucleus
Q247

Drug of choice in phenothiazine-induced Dystonia is:** *(AI 1996)

  • A. Diphenhydramine
  • B. Metoclopramide
  • C. Trifluperamide
  • D. Benztropine
Q248

In Huntington's disease (HD), which of the following is predominantly atrophied?** *(NBE)

  • A. Striatum
  • B. Cerebellum
  • C. Hippocampus
  • D. Mamillary bodies
Q249

Huntington's disease is due to the loss of:** *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. Nigrostriatal dopaminergic neurons
  • B. Intrastriatal cholinergic neurons
  • C. Intrastriatal GABAergic neurons
  • D. Intrastriatal cholinergic and GABAergic neurons
Q250

Huntington's disease (HD) is caused by mutations in the Huntington's gene on the?** *(NBE)

  • A. Short arm of chromosome 4
  • B. Long arm of chromosome 4
  • C. Short arm of chromosome 5
  • D. Long arm of chromosome 5
Q251

All are true about Huntington's disease except:** *(AI 2001)

  • B. Behavioral disturbance
  • C. Early onset of memory loss
  • D. Cog-wheel rigidity.
Q252

Which of the following is most characteristic feature of Huntington's disease (HD):** *(NBE)

  • A. Gait disturbance
  • C. Oculomotor abnormalities
  • D. Myoclonus
Q253

Westphal variant of Huntington's disease (HD) relates to which of the following?** *(NBE)

  • B. Parkinsonian syndrome
  • C. Orthostatic hypotension
  • D. Dementia
Q254

Which of the following is a typical MRI finding in Huntington's disease (HD)?** *(NBE)

  • A. Hypointensity of the caudate head
  • B. Progressive atrophy of caudate nuclei
  • C. Iron accumulation in striatum
  • D. Atrophy of the midbrain
Q255

A family consist of husband, wife and 2 children. Wife's father has Huntington's chorea. Which statement is true:** *(AIIMS Nov 99)

  • A. Chance of getting disease in child is 1:4
  • B. Can occur upto age of 50
  • C. If wife does not get disease till age 50 children are not affected.
  • D. Huntington's chorea is due to point mutation in gene.
Q256

A patient presents with ataxia, urinary incontinence and dementia. The likely diagnosis is:** *(AI

  • A. Alzheimer's Disease
  • B. Parkinson's disease
  • C. Steel Richardson syndrome
  • D. Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus
Q257

Triad of normal-pressure hydrocephalus includes:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Tremor, aphasia dementia
  • B. Ataxia, aphasia, gait disorder
  • C. Gait disorder, urinary incontinence, dementia
  • D. Gait disorder, urinary incontinence, lower cranial nerve palsy
Q258

Normal pressure Hydrocephalus is characterized by all except:** *(AI 1996)

  • B. Dementia
  • D. Urinary incontinence
Q259

An elderly man presents with features of dementia, ataxia, difficulty in downward gaze and a history of frequent falls. Likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Parkinsons disease
  • B. Progressive supranuclear gaze palsy
  • C. Alzheimers disease
Q260

Frequent falls and abnormality of vertical gaze is a feature of which of the following?** *(NBE)

  • A. Parkinson's disease
  • B. Corticobasal degeneration
  • C. Striatonigral degeneration
  • D. Progressive supranuclear palsy
Q261

A 76 year old male comes with a history of frequent falls and difficulty in looking downwards and laterally. The diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS May 01)

  • B. Supranuclear palsy
  • C. Amyotropic lateral sclerosis
  • D. Oculomotor nerve palsy
Q262

A 45-year-old man presents with history of frequent falls. He has difficulty in looking down also. What is the most probable diagnosis:** *(AIIMS Nov. 2000)

  • A. Normal pressure hydrocephalus
  • C. Alzheimer's disease
Q263

All the following are features of Pseudotumor Cerebri except:** *(AI 1996)

  • A. Normal-sized ventricles on CT scan
  • B. Increased protein in CSF
  • C. Papilledema
  • D. Absence of focal neurological deficit
Q264

All the following are features of Pseudotumor cerebri except:** *(AI 1994)

  • A. Increased intracranial tension
  • B. Convulsions
  • D. Normal CT scan

Pseudotumor cerebri is caused by:** *(NBE)

  • A. Acetazolamide
  • B. Vitamin A overdose
  • C. Methotrexate overdose
  • D. Excessive iron therapy
Q266

Pick's body in pick's disease is:** *(AI 2008)

  • A. Tau protein
  • B. Alpha synuclein
  • C. Beta synuclein
  • D. A B amyloid
Q267

Localised regional cerebral atrophy is seen in:** *(GI Dec 02)

  • B. Frontotemporal dementia
  • C. PML
  • D. C.J. disease
Q268

All are feature of Wernicke's encephalopathy, Except:** *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • A. Cogwheel rigidity
  • B. Alteration in mental function
Q269

Which of the following is not involved in Wernicke's Korsakoff psychosis:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Mamillary body
  • C. Periventricular Grey matter
Q270

Which of the following sites is responsible for the amnestic defect in Wernicke's Korsakoff syndrome:** *(AI 2012)

Q271

Which of the following is a typical feature of Korsakoff's Syndrome:** *(NBE)

  • A. Confabulation
  • B. Encephalopathy
  • D. Hallucinations
Q272

Neurodegeneration with iron accumulation in basal ganglia is typically seen in:** *(NBE)

  • A. Hallervorden Spatz disease
  • C. Wilson's Disease
  • D. Cystic Fibrosis
Q273

Memory impairment is most likely to occur in:** *(AIIMS Nov 99)

  • A. Downs Syndrome
  • B. Alkaptonuria
  • C. Attention deficit disorder
  • D. Conduct disorder
Q274

Which of the following is a cause of reversible dementia?** *(AI 2005)

  • A. Subacute combined degeneration.
  • B. Picks disease.
  • C. Creutzfeldt - Jakob disease.
  • D. Alzheimer's disease.
Q275

CSF glucose level is:** *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. Half the plasma glucose
  • B. 2/3 plasma glucose
  • C. 1/3 plasma glucose
  • D. Same as plasma glucose
Q276

The CSF findings in TB meningitis are all the following except:** *(AI 1995)

  • A. Raised protein
  • B. Low sugar
  • C. Low chloride
  • D. High RBC count
Q277

Which of the following is not seen in Tubercular meningitis:** *(NBE)

  • A. Low sugar
  • B. High protein
  • C. Low opening pressure
  • D. Lymphocytic pleocytosis
Q278

A ten yr old girl presents with fever convulsion, neck rigidity. CSF - Findings are protein 150 mg, Sugar 40 mg, chloride 50 ug/dl with lymphadenopathy:** *(AIIMS Dec 98)

  • A. Viral Meningitis
  • B. Pyogenic Meningitis
  • C. Cryptococcal Meningitis
  • D. Tubercular Meningitis
Q279

Which of the following is the classical CSF finding seen in TBM?** *(AI 2007)

  • A. Increased protein, decreased sugar, increased lymphocytes
  • B. Increased protein, sugar and lymphocytes
  • C. Decreased protein, increased sugar and lymphocytes
  • D. Increased sugar, protein and neutrophils
Q280

A 25 years old lady with history of fever for 1 month presents with headache and ataxia. Brain imaging shows dilated ventricles and significant basal exudates. Which of the following will be the most likely CSF finding:** *(AIIMS Nov 2011)

  • A. Lymphocytosis, Low Glucose, High protein
  • B. Lymphocytosis, Normal Glucose, High protein
  • C. Lymphocytosis, Low Glucose, Normal protein
  • D. Neutrophilia, Low glucose, Low Protein
Q281

Characteristic finding in CT in a TB case is:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Exudate seen in basal cistern
  • B. Hydrocephalus is non communicating
  • C. Calcification commonly seen in cerebellum
  • D. Ventriculitis is a common finding
Q282

Basal exudates, infarcts and hydrocephalus are likely findings observed in Brain imaging studies The most likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Tubercular meningitis
  • B. Viral meningitis
  • C. Herpes encephalitis
  • D. Cerebral Malaria
Q283

True about C.S.F. finding in pyogenic meningitis is:** *(AI 1998)

  • A. Protein ↑, sugar ↓
  • B. Protein ↓, sugar ↑
  • C. Chloride ↑, protein ↓
  • D. Protein ↓, sugar ↓
Q284

Pneumococcal meningitis is associated with the following CSF findings:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Pleocytosis with low protein and low sugar
  • B. Pleocytosis with high protein and low sugar
  • C. Lymphocytosis with low protein and low sugar
  • D. Lymphocytosis with high protein and low sugar
Q285

True about CSF finding in pyogenic meningitis is:** *(AI 1995)

  • A. CSF contains no organisms
  • B. ↓ Chloride, ↓ Glucose
  • C. ↑ Glucose, ↑ protein
  • D. ↑ Chloride, ↓ glucose
Q286

The drug of choice in Cryptococcal Meningitis is:** *(AI 1995)

  • A. Pentostatin
  • B. Amphotericin B
  • C. Clotrimazole
  • D. Zidovudine

Which of the following is the drug of choice in meningococcal meningitis?** *(NBE)

  • A. Ceftriaxone
  • B. Penicillin
  • C. Rifampicin
  • D. Streptomycin

Neurological complications of meningitis include all of the following, except:** *(AIIMS Nov 02)

  • A. Seizures.
  • B. Increased intra-cranial pressure.
  • C. Cerebral hamartoma.
  • D. Subdural effusions.
Q289

Which of the following agents is most commonly associated with recurrent meningitis due to CSF leaks:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Meningococci
  • B. Pneumococci
  • C. Hemophilus Influenza
  • D. E. Coli
Q290

Subdural empyema is most commonly caused by:** *(AI 2000)

  • A. H influenza
Q291

Which of the following viruses is not a common cause of viral encephalitis?** *(AI 2004)

  • A. Herpes simplex virus type 2
  • B. Japanese encephalitis virus
  • C. Nipah virus
  • D. Cytomegalovirus
Q292

Which of the following statements about Mollaret meningitis is true?** *(NBE)

  • A. Caused by Herpes simplex 2 in most of the cases.
  • B. Is a recurrent, benign septic meningitis,
  • C. Is also referred to as "Benign Recurrent Neutrophilic Meningitis"
  • D. Does not resolve without treatment

Which of the following is the most common cause of meningoencephalitis in children:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Mumps
  • B. Arbovirus
  • C. HSV
  • D. Enterovirus
Q294

Commonest cause of sporadic encephalitis is:** *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. Japanese B Virus
  • B. Herpes Simplex Virus
  • C. Human Immunodeficiency Virus
  • D. Rubeola Virus
Q295

A young male develops fever, followed by headache, confusional state, focal seizures and right hemiparesis. The MRI performed shows bilateral frontotemporal hyper intense lesion. The most likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2004)

  • A. Acute pyogenic meningitis
  • B. Herpes simplex encephalitis
  • C. Neurocysticercosis
  • D. Carcinomatous meningitis
Q296

A 25 year old person presents with acute onset of fever and focal seizures. MRI scan shows hyperintensity in the temporal lobe and frontal lobe with enhancement. The most likely diagnosis is:** *(AI

  • A. Meningococcal Meningitis
  • B. Herpes simplex Encephalitis
  • C. Japanese Encephalitis
Q297

Which of the following is TRUE regarding Herpes encephalitis?** *(NBE)

  • A. Seizures are uncommon.
  • B. Temporal lobe involvement is common.
  • C. Brain MRI is characteristically normal.
  • D. Cold sores of genital herpes are usually present.
Q298

The drug of choice in Herpes Simplex Encephalitis is:** *(AI 1994)

  • A. Acyclovir
  • B. Zidovudine
  • C. Amantadine
  • D. Vidarabine
Q299

Which of the following is the most common central nervous system parasitic infection?** *(AIIMS May

  • A. Echinococcosis.
  • B. Sparganosis.
  • C. Paragonimiasis.
  • D. Neurocysticercosis.
Q300

Commonest presentation of neurocysticercosis is:** *(AI 2003)

  • A. Seizures
  • B. Focal neurological deficits
  • D. Radiculopathy
Q301

Which of the following is the MOST common presentation of Neurocysticercosis?** *(NBE)

  • A. Epilepsy
  • C. Raised intracranial pressure
  • D. Signs of meningeal irritation
Q302

Which of the following is the most common location of intracranial neurocysticercosis:** *(AIIMS Nov. 05)

  • A. Brain parenchyma
  • B. Subarachnoid space
  • C. Spinal cord
  • D. Orbit
Q303

True statement about neurocysticercosis is:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Seizures due to neurocysticercosis are resistant to anti epileptic drugs
  • B. Albendazole is superior to praziquantel in the treatment of above condition
  • C. Common presentation is 6th CN palsy and hemiparesis.
  • D. Steroids are used in the management of hydrocephalus.
Q304

Treatment of Neurocysticercosis includes all of the following except:** *(AI 1998)

  • A. Praziquantel
  • B. Albendazole
  • C. Niclosamide
  • D. Flubendazole
Q305

Which of the following is the most likely cause of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy?

  • A. JC virus
  • B. Poliovirus
  • C. Measles virus
  • D. Herpes simplex virus
Q306

All of the following statements are true regarding central nervous system infections, EXCEPT: *(AIIMS Nov 04)

  • A. Measles virus is the causative agent for subacute sclerosing pan encephalitis (SSPE)
  • B. Cytomegalo virus causes bilateral temporal lobe hemorrhagic infarction
  • C. Prions infection causes spongiform encephalopathy
  • D. JC virus is the causative agent for progressive multifocal leucoencephalopathy
Q307

Which of the following statements about Prions is true:** *(AI 2008)

  • A. They are infectious proteins
  • B. They are made up of bacteria and virus
  • C. They have rich nuclear material
  • D. They can be cultured in cell free media
Q308

Prions include:** *(AIIMS Nov 07)

  • A. DNA and RNA
  • B. Only RNA
  • C. Proteins
  • D. Only DNA
Q309

Which one of the following is not a prion associated disease:** *(AI 2005)

  • A. Scrapie
  • B. Kuru
  • C. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
  • D. Alzheimer's disease
Q310

Fatal familial insomnia is associated with:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Prion disease
  • B. Degenerative disease
  • C. Neoplastic disease
  • D. Vascular disease
Q311

A 60 year old man with progressive dementia of recent onset presents with intermittent irregular jerky movements. EEG shows periodic sharp biphasic waves. The most likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2011)

  • B. Creutzfeldt Jakob disease
  • C. Lewy body dementia
  • D. Herpes Simplex Encephalitis
Q312

All of the following statements about Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease are true, EXCEPT:** *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. It is a neurodegenerative disease
  • B. It is caused by infectious proteins
  • C. Myoclonus is rarely seen
  • D. Brain biopsy is specific for diagnosis
Q313

Seizures may be the presenting feature in all of the following, Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Cryptococcus meningitis
  • B. Toxoplasmosis
  • C. CMV
  • D. Entamoeba Histolytica
Q314

Most common Intracranial neoplasm in adults is:** *(AIIMS May 93)

  • A. Meningioma
  • B. Astrocytoma
  • C. Posterior fossa tumor
  • D. Ganglioneuroma
Q315

Most common intracranial tumour is:** *(AIIMS May 94)

  • B. Glioma
  • C. Craniopharyngioma
Q316

Which of the following is the most common type of Glial tumors?** *(AI 06)

  • A. Astrocytomas
  • B. Medulloblastomas
  • C. Neurofibromas
  • D. Ependymomas
Q317

In children most common posterior fossa tumour is:** *(AIIMS Dec 95)

  • A. Meningiomas
  • C. Medulloblastoma
  • D. Glioblastoma multiforme
Q318

Which of the following statements about cerebellar astrocytomas in paediatric age group is False: *(AI 2008)

  • A. These are usually Low grade tumors
  • B. These tumors have a good prognosis
  • C. These are more commonly seen in the 1st and 2nd decades
  • D. These tumours are more common in females
Q319

Most common site of sub ependymal astrocytoma (giant cell):** *(AIIMS Nov 07)

  • A. Trigone of lateral ventricle
  • B. Foramen of Monro
  • C. Temporal horn of lateral ventricle
  • D. 4th ventricle
Q320

Tumor with best prognosis is:** *(AIIMS May 94)

  • A. Ependymoma
  • B. Medulloblastoma
  • C. Cerebellar astrocytoma
Q321

All of the following tumors may be malignant except:** *(AI 1997)

  • A. Glioma
  • C. Hemangioblastoma
  • D. Ependymoma
Q322

Most radiosensitive brain tumor is:** *(AIIMS Sept 96)

  • C. Glioblastoma multiforme
  • D. Astrocytoma
Q323

Which of the following brain tumors does not spread Via CSF?** *(AI 2004)

  • A. Germ cell tumors
  • C. CNS Lymphoma
Q324

A 6 year old boy has been complaining of headache, ignoring to see the objects on the sides for four months. On examination, he is not mentally retarded, his grades at school are good, and visual acuity is diminished in both the eyes. Visual charting showed significant field defect. CT scan of the head showed suprasellar mass with calcification. Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?** *(AIIMS Nov 04)

  • A. Astrocytoma
  • B. Craniopharyngioma
  • C. Pituitary adenoma
  • D. Meningioma
Q325

Which of the following statements about neuroblastoma is not true:** *(AI 2009)

  • A. Most common extracranial solid tumor in childhood
  • B. > 50% present with metastasis at time of diagnosis
Q326

All the following are true of Craniopharyngioma except:** *(AI 1994)

  • A. Derived from Rathke's pouch
  • B. Contains epithelial cells
  • C. Present in sella or infra-sellar location
  • D. Causes visual disturbances
Q327

A 30-year-old male complains of loss of erection; he has low testosterone and high prolactin level in blood; What is the likely diagnosis?** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Pituitary adenoma
  • B. Testicular failure
  • C. Cranio pharyngioma
  • D. Cushing's syndrome
Q328

Which of the following is the most common CNS tumor associated with HIV infection?** *(NBE)

  • C. Astrocytoma
  • D. Medulloblastoma
Q329

Which of the following is the most common false localizing neurological sign in assessing brain tumors?** *(NBE)

  • B. Unilateral papilloedema
  • C. Abnormal unilateral pupil
  • D. Diplopia
Q330

The characteristic feature of a frontal lobe tumor is:** *(AI 1994)

  • A. Abnormal gait
  • C. Distractibility
  • D. Antisocial behavior
Q331

A 40 year old female patient presented with blunted emotional response; impaired planning and judgement. The probable site of lesion would be:** *(AIIMS Nov 18)

  • A. Parietal lobe
  • B. Frontal lobe
  • C. Occipital lobe
  • D. Temporal lobe
Q332

Foster Kennedy syndrome is classically described in association with:** *(NBE)

  • A. Craniopharyngioma
  • B. Pituitary adenoma
  • C. Olfactory groove meningioma
Q333

Foster Kennedy syndrome is associated with all of the following, except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Ipsilateral Anosmia
  • B. Contralateral Optic Atrophy
  • D. Contralateral Papilloedema
Q334

A young female patient with long history of sinusitis presented with frequent fever along with personality changes and headache of recent origin. The fundus examination revealed papilledema. The most likely diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS Nov. 04)

  • A. Frontal lobe abscess
  • D. Frontal bone osteomyelitis
Q335

All of the following statements about Neurofibromatosis are true, Except:** *(AI 2009)

  • A. Autosomal Recessive Inheritance
  • B. Cutaneous neurofibromas
  • D. Scoliosis
Q336

All of the following are seen in Neurofibromatosis, Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Lisch nodules
  • B. Axillary Freckling
  • C. Shagreen Patch
  • D. Cafe-au-lait spots
Q337

The number of café au lait macules as one of the diagnostic criteria for adult neurofibromatosis is:

  • A. ≥ 1
  • B. ≥ 2
  • C. ≥ 4
  • D. ≥ 6
Q338

The number of Neurofibromas as one of the diagnostic criteria for adult neurofibromatosis type-1 is:

Q339

Which of the following is the most common tumor associated with type I neurofibromatosis? *(AIIMS May 03)

  • A. Optic nerve glioma
  • B. Meningioma
  • C. Acoustic Schwannoma
  • D. Low grade astrocytoma
Q340

Neurofibromatosis type-II is associated with all of the following, except:** *(NBE)

  • A. B/L acoustic schwannoma
  • B. Chromosome-22
  • C. Lisch nodule
  • D. Posterior subcapsular lenticular cataract
Q341

Neurofibromatosis I is most commonly associated with:** *(AIIMS Nov 07)

  • A. Brain stem gliomas
  • B. Optic pathway glioma
  • C. Sub ependymal pilocytic astrocytoma
Q342

All of the following may be associated with Von Hippel Lindau syndrome, except:** *(AI 2009)

  • A. Retinal and cerebellar hemangioblastomas
  • B. Gastric carcinoma
  • C. Pheochromocytoma
  • D. Renal cell carcinoma
Q343

Hemangioblastoma associated with VHL are most commonly seen in:** *(NBE)

  • C. Kidney
  • D. Pancreas
Q344

In Von Hippel-Lindau Syndrome, the retinal vascular tumours are often associated with intracranial hemangioblastoma. Which one of the following regions is associated with such vascular abnormalities in this syndrome?** *(AI 2005)

  • A. Optic radiation.
  • B. Optic tract.
  • C. Cerebellum.
  • D. Pulvinar.
Q345

CNS tumor seen in Von Hippel Lindau syndrome is:** *(PGI Dec 99)

  • B. Cerebellar hemangioblastoma
  • C. CNS lymphoma
  • D. Glioma
Q346

Which of the following statements about Von-Hippel Lindau syndrome is true:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Multiple tumors are rarely seen
  • B. Craniospinal hemangioblastomas are common
  • C. Supratentorial lesions are common
  • D. Tumors of Schwann cells are common
Q347

All of the following tumors are malignant except:** *(AI 1997)

Q348

A child presents to the clinic with history of seizures and mental retardation. Clinical examination reveals multiple hypopigmented macules. What is the likely diagnosis:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Tuberous Sclerosis
  • B. Neurofibromatosis
  • C. Sturge Weber Syndrome
  • D. Linear epidermal nevus syndrome
Q349

Triad of Tuberous Sclerosis includes all, except:** *(AI 2009)

  • B. Adenoma sebaceum
  • C. Low intelligence
Q350

The diagnosis of a patient presenting with Seizures, Mental retardation and Sebaceous adenoma is: *(AI 1995)

  • B. Tuberous sclerosis
  • C. Toxoplasmosis
  • D. Down syndrome
Q351

Bilateral renal angiomyolipomas are seen on CT scan in which of the following?** *(NBE)

  • A. Tuberous sclerosis
  • B. Neurofibromatosis 1
  • C. Von Hippel-Lindau disease
  • D. Beckwith- Wiedemann syndrome
Q352

Shagreen patch is a characteristic feature of:** *(NBE)

  • A. Tuberous sclerosis.
  • B. Neurofibromatosis.
  • C. Sturge weber syndrome.
  • D. Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome.
Q353

Koenen's tumor are seen in?** *(NBE)

  • A. Neurofibromatosis
  • C. Sturge weber syndrome
Q354

Calcified subependymal nodules in CT scan of brain are characteristically seen in which of the following?** *(NBE)

  • A. Parkinsonism
  • B. Tuberous Sclerosis
  • C. Neurofibromatosis 1
  • D. Von Hippel-Lindau disease
Q355

Brain tumor is associated with A/E:** *(PGI Dec 99)

  • B. Von hippel landau syndrome
  • C. Neurofibromatosis
  • D. Sturge Weber syndrome
Q356

What is NOT a feature of Sturge Weber syndrome:** *(AIIMS Dec 94)

  • A. Rail track appearance
  • B. Hemiatrophy of the brain
  • C. Convulsion
  • D. Empty sella
Q357

A middle aged female presents with prolonged history of back pain followed by slowly progressive weakness of both lower limbs, spasticity and recent onset difficulty in micturation. On neurological examination there is evidence of dorsal myelopathy. MRI scan of spine shows a well-defined mid-dorsal intradural homogenous contrast enhancing mass lesion. The likely diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS Nov 2011)

  • A. Intradural Lipoma
  • B. Dermoid cyst
  • C. Epidermoid cyst
  • D. Spinal meningioma
Q358

All are true about peripheral-neuropathy except:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. Glove and stocking anaesthesia
  • B. Proximal muscle weakness
  • C. Nerve-conduction deficit
  • D. Decreased reflexes
Q359

All can cause peripheral neuropathy, except:** *(AIIMS June 97)

  • A. Uraemia
  • B. HIV
  • C. Chronic liver disease
Q360

All of the following are classified as 'Small fiber' neuropathies' except:** *(AI 2012)

  • B. HIV Induced
  • C. Hansen's disease
  • D. Amyloidosis
Q361

Small fiber neuropathy presents as:** *(PGI June 08)

  • A. Burning pain
  • B. Tingling & Numbness
  • C. Foot drop
  • D. Twitching
Q362

All of the following are associated with Autonomic Neuropathies, Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Diabetes
  • B. Amyloid
  • D. Botulinism
Q363

All of the following are feature of autonomic neuropathy, Except:** *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. Resting Tachycardia
  • B. Silent Myocardial Infarction
  • C. Orthostatic Hypotension
  • D. Bradycardia
Q364

Which of the following types of neuropathy are seen in Diabetes:** *(NBE)

  • A. Distal symmetric sensory neuropathy
  • B. Autonomic neuropathy
  • C. Mononeuropathy
Q365

The most common form of diabetic neuropathy is:** *(NBE)

  • B. Distal symmetric sensory and motor neuropathy
  • C. Autonomic neuropathy
  • D. Distal Motor neuropathy
Q366

Which of the following drugs is used for painful diabetic neuropathy:** *(NBE)

  • A. Duloxetine
  • B. Pregabalin
Q367

First line drug used for painful diabetic neuropathy is?** *(NBE)

  • A. Carbamazepine
  • B. Duloxetine
  • C. Venlafaxine
  • D. EMLA
Q368

A Patient presents with recurrent episodes of sharp pain over his right cheek that is precipitated on chewing. Between attacks patient is otherwise normal. The most probable diagnosis is?** *(NBE)

  • A. Preherpetic neuralgia
  • B. Trigeminal neuralgia
  • C. Mumps
  • D. Thalamic syndrome
Q369

Tic Douloureux is facial pain traveling through which of the following nerves?** *(NBE)

  • A. Facial
  • B. Hypoglossal
  • C. Trigeminal
  • D. Vestibulocochlear
Q370

All of the following statements about Trigeminal Neuralgia are true Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Most commonly involves Ophthalmic Division (V3) of Maxillary Nerve
  • B. Attacks most commonly occur during the day
  • C. Affects women more than men
  • D. More common on the Right Side
Q371

Drug of choice for Trigeminal Neuralgia is:** *(NBE)

  • A. Chlorpromazine
  • B. Carbamazepine
  • C. Gabapentin
  • D. Fluoxetine
Q372

All of the following can cause neuropathies with predominant motor involvement except:** *(AI 2004)

  • A. Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy
  • B. Acute intermittent porphyria
  • C. Lead intoxication
  • D. Arsenic intoxication
Q373

Most common inherited peripheral neuropathy is?** *(NBE)

  • A. Refsum's disease
  • B. Charcot marie tooth 1
  • C. Farber's disease
  • D. Fabry disease
Q374

Most common cause of mononeuritis multiplex in India is:** *(AIIMS Nov 08)

  • A. Hansen's disease
  • D. PAN
Q375

Pure motor paralysis is seen in:** *(AI 2000)

  • A. Polio
  • B. Guillain Barre syndrome
  • D. Sub-Acute Combined Degeneration
Q376

All cause ascending motor paralysis except:** *(AI 2000)

  • A. Diabetes mellitus
  • B. Diphtheria
  • C. Guillain Barre syndrome
  • D. Porphyria
Q377

Trophic ulcers are seen in all, Except:** *(PGI Dec 2000)

  • A. Leprosy
  • B. Syringomyelia
  • C. Polio
  • D. All of the above (seen in all)
Q378

All of the following can lead to trophic ulcers in the fingers EXCEPT:** *(AIIMS Nov 2000)

  • A. Cervical disc prolapse
  • B. Subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord
  • C. Leprosy
Q379

Dying back neuropathy is seen in all except:** *(AI 2008)

  • A. Diabetic Neuropathy
  • B. Arsenic neuropathy
  • C. Porphyria
  • D. Guillain Barre syndrome (GBS)
Q380

All of the following are true about Guillain Barre Syndrome (GBS), Except:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. Ascending paralysis
  • C. Sensory level
  • D. Albumino-Cytological Dissociation
Q381

All the following are features of Guillain-Barre syndrome except:** *(AI 1996)

  • A. Predominant motor involvement
  • B. Facial nerve involvement
  • C. Absence of pleocytosis in CSF
  • D. Bladder bowel involvement
Q382

All of the following statements about Guillain-Barre Syndrome are true, Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Inflammatory
  • B. Demyelinating
  • C. Descending
  • D. Cranial Nerve Involvement
Q383

All of following are true regarding Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS):** *(NBE)

  • A. It is a predominant sensory neuropathy
  • B. It causes ascending paralysis
  • C. It is a chronic demyelinating polyneuropathy
  • D. It is an inflammatory disorder of peripheral nerves
Q384

All are true regarding G.B. syndrome, Except:** *(AI 1996)

  • B. Absence of pleocytosis in CSF
  • C. Residual disability if treatment delayed
  • D. Sparing of bladder function
Q385

Which of the following is the MOST Common antecedent infection associated with Guillain-Barre syndrome?** *(NBE)

  • A. Mycoplasma
  • B. Epstein-Barr virus
  • C. Chlamydia infection
  • D. Campylobacter jejuni
Q386

All of the following statements regarding treatment of GBS are true, Except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Corticosteroids may be used for early recovery and prevent long hospital stay
  • B. High dose intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) can be initiated
  • C. Plasmapheresis can be done
  • D. High dose intravenous immunoglobulins and plasmapheresis are equally effective
Q387

Conduction velocity of nerve is reduced in all of the following conditions, except:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Acute Motor Axonal Neuropathy (AMAN)
  • B. Acute Inflammatory Demyelinating Neuropathy (AIDP)
  • C. Hereditary Sensory Motor Neuropathy (HSMN)
  • D. Multifocal Motor Neuropathy
Q388

A child presents with ascending flaccid paralysis. There is subsequent respiratory muscle involvement. CSF examination shows albuminocytological dissociation. Treatment of choice is:** *(AIIMS May 02)

  • A. Cycloserine
  • B. Oral prednisolone
  • C. I.V. methyl prednisolone
  • D. I.V. immunoglobulins
Q389

An 18-year-old male presented with acute onset descending paralysis of 3 days duration. There is also a history of blurring of vision for the same duration. On examination, the patient has quadriparesis with areflexia. Both the pupils are non-reactive. The most probable diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS May 06)

  • B. Botulism
  • C. Diphtheria
Q390

All of the following are features of Botulism except:** *(NBE)

  • A. Cranial nerve palsies
  • B. Descending paralysis
  • D. Clear Sensorium
Q391

Acute onset of blurred vision and absent pupillary response with rapidly progressive descending muscular weakness manifesting as quadriparesis in an afebrile 20 year old male with preserved sensorium is:** *(NBE)

  • A. Porphyria
  • D. Diphtheria
Q392

A 45-year-old female presents to the outpatient clinic with symptoms of pain, fatigue and weakness in both lower limbs. She gives history of paralysis affecting both lower limbs in childhood from which she made good functional recovery. Which of the following is the most likely suspected diagnosis:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Post Polio syndrome
  • B. Polymyositis
  • C. Muscular Dystrophy
  • D. Neuropathy
Q393

Charcot's Joint includes all of the following except:** *(AIIMS Nov. 06)

  • B. Leprosy
  • C. Diabetes
  • D. Arthrogryposis Multiplex Congenita

After a minor head injury a young patient was unable to close his left eye and had drooling of saliva from left angle of mouth. He is suffering from:** *(AIIMS May 03)

  • A. VIIth nerve injury
  • B. Vth nerve injury
  • C. IIIrd nerve injury
  • D. Combined VIIth and IIIrd nerve injury
Q395

TRUE regarding upper motor neuron VIIth nerve paralysis is:** *(AIIMS Dec 95)

  • A. Ipsilateral upper face paresis
  • B. Ipsilateral lower face paresis
  • C. Contralateral upper face paresis
  • D. Contralateral lower face paresis
Q396

Schirmer's test is done for:** *(NBE)

  • C. Facial nerve
  • D. Hypoglossal nerve
Q397

Schirmer's test is used to assess:** *(NBE)

  • A. Greater superficial petrosal nerve
  • B. Stapedial branch
  • C. Chorda tympani nerve
  • D. Stylohyoid branch
Q398

Herpes zoster in geniculate ganglion causes:** *(NBE)

  • A. Bell's palsy
  • B. Ramsay Hunt syndrome
  • C. Melkersson Rosenthal syndrome
  • D. Frey's syndrome

Myasthenia gravis is associated with:** *(PGI Dec 01)

  • A. Decreased acetylcholine at nerve endings
  • B. Decreased myosin
  • C. Absent troponin C
  • D. Decreased myoneural junction transmission
Q400

All are clinical features of myasthenia gravis, EXCEPT:** *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • A. Spontaneous remission
  • B. Absent deep tendon reflexes
  • C. Proximal muscle involvement
  • D. Worsen by exertion
Q401

Which of the following is the most common initial presentation in a patient with myasthenia gravis?

  • A. Upper limb paralysis
  • B. Ptosis
  • C. Lower limb paralysis
Q402

A 45 year old woman, presenting with the history of diplopia and dysphagia worsening as the day progresses, can be diagnosed to have:** *(AIIMS Nov. 05)

  • B. Myasthenia gravis
  • C. Muscular dystrophy
  • D. Brain tumor
Q403

The most sensitive test for the diagnosis of myasthenia Gravis is:** *(AI 2005)

  • A. Elevated ACH Receptor Antibodies (ACHR Antibodies)
  • B. Repetitive Nerve Stimulation test (RNS)
  • C. Positive Edrophonium test (Tensilon test)
  • D. Single Fiber Electromyography (SFEMG)
Q404

A thirty five year old female has proximal weakness of muscles, ptosis and easy fatigability. The most sensitive test to suggest the diagnosis is:** *(AI 2011)

  • A. Muscle Biopsy
  • B. CPK levels
  • C. Edrophonium test
  • D. EMG
Q406

Which of following statements is TRUE regarding myasthenia gravis?** *(NBE)

  • A. Muscle biopsy is the 'gold standard' for diagnosis.
  • B. Non-depolarizing muscle relaxants can be used safely.
  • C. Anticholinesterase antibodies are present in 80% of patients.
  • D. Single fiber electromyography is the most sensitive diagnostic test.
Q407

Which of the following statements about Lambert Eaton Myasthenic syndrome is true:** *(AI 2009)

  • A. Tensilon test is positive
  • B. Extraocular muscles are most commonly involved
  • C. Incremental response to repeated electrical stimulation
  • D. Associated with adenocarcinoma of lung
Q408

Ice pack test is done for:** *(NBE)

  • A. Myasthenia Gravis
  • B. Multiple system atrophy
  • C. Hyperparathyroidism
  • D. Hypokalemic periodic paralysis
Q409

Which one of the following is correct regarding Eaton-Lambert syndrome:** *(AIIMS Nov 04)

  • A. It commonly affects the ocular muscle
  • B. Neostigmine is the drug of choice for this syndrome
  • C. Repeated electrical stimulation enhances muscle power in it.
  • D. It is commonly associated with adenocarcinoma of lung
Q410

All of the following may be associated with Thymoma, Except:** *(AI 2010)

  • A. SIADH
  • B. Myasthenia Gravis
  • C. Hypogammaglobulinemia
Q411

Thymomas may be associated with all of the following, Except:** *(NBE)

  • B. Hypergammaglobulinemia
  • C. Panhypopituitarism
  • D. SLE
Q412

Good's syndrome is:** *(NBE)

  • A. Thymic hyperplasia with myasthenia gravis
  • B. Thymic hypoplasia with immunodeficiency
Q413

Thymoma is associated with:** *(AI 2000)

  • A. Myasthenia gravis
  • C. Oesophageal atresia
  • D. Hyper-gammaglobulinemia
Q414

Thymoma can be associated with all, EXCEPT:** *(AIIMS Dec 95)

  • A. Superior mediastinum syndrome
  • C. Hypergammaglobulinemia
  • D. Pure red cell aplasia
Q415

Which of the following is not associated with Thymomas:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Red cell aplasia
  • D. Compression of the superior mediastinum
Q416

Thymoma is associated with:** *(AI 2008)

Q417

Myasthenia gravis is caused by dysfunction of:** *(NBE)

  • A. Pineal gland.
  • B. Thymus gland.
  • C. Pituitary gland.
  • D. Parathyroid gland.
Q418

In myasthenia gravis, correct statement regarding thymectomy is:** *(AI 2001)

  • A. Should be done in all cases
  • B. Should be done in cases with ocular involvement only
  • C. Not required if controlled by medical management
  • D. Should be done only in cases that are associated with thymoma
Q419

Drug of choice for myasthenia gravis:** *(PGI Dec 99)

  • A. Gallamine
  • B. Succinylcholine
  • C. Tubocurare
  • D. Pyridostigmine
Q420

Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy is a disease of:** *(AI 2004)

  • A. Neuromuscular junction
  • B. Sarcolemmal proteins
  • C. Muscle contractile proteins
  • D. Disuse atrophy due to muscle weakness.
Q421

Dystrophic gene mutation leads to:** *(AIIMS May 03)

  • B. Motor neuron disease
  • C. Poliomyelitis
  • D. Duchenne muscular dystrophy
Q422

Gene for myotonic dystrophy is coded on chromosome number:** *(AI 1999)

  • A. 19
  • B. 20
  • C. 21
  • D. 24
Q423

Which of the following is not a Limb Girdle Dystrophy:** *(AIIMS Nov. 06)

  • A. Sarcoglycan dystrophy
  • B. Dystrophin dystrophy
  • C. Dysferlin dystrophy
  • D. Calpain dystrophy
Q424

A young adult presents with proximal weakness of upper limbs, features of facial palsy and winging of scapula. The most likely diagnosis is:** *(AI 2012)

  • A. Facio-Scapulo-Humeral Dystrophy
  • B. Limb-Girdle Dystrophy
  • C. Scapuloperoneal Dystrophy
  • D. Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy
Q425

The term' ragged red fibers' is applied to describe the skeletal muscle fibers in:** *(NBE)

  • A. Myotonic dystrophy
  • B. Nemaline myopathy
  • C. Spinal muscular atrophy
  • D. Mitochondrial myopathy
Q426

All are congenital myopathies EXCEPT:** *(AIIMS Nov 01)

  • A. Central core myopathy
  • C. Z band myopathy
  • D. Centronuclear myopathy
Q427

Inverted Champagne bottle muscle atrophy occurs in:** *(AIIMS May 93)

  • A. Peroneal muscular atrophy
  • C. Progressive muscular atrophy
Q428

Which of the following antibodies is specific for myositis:** *(AIIMS Nov 08)

  • A. Anti – Jo-1
  • B. Anti – Scl-70
  • C. Anti – Sm
  • D. Anti- ku
Q429

Primary idiopathic polymyositis does not involve:** *(AIIMS-97)

  • A. Pelvic girdle muscles
  • B. Neck muscles
  • C. Ocular muscles
  • D. Pharyngeal muscle
Q430

Which one of the following clinical findings excludes the diagnosis of polymyositis?** *(AI 06)

  • A. Neck muscle involvement
  • B. Extraocular muscle involvement
  • C. Dysphagia
  • D. Abdominal muscle involvement
Q431

Which is NOT a feature of polymyositis?** *(AIIMS-97)

  • A. Pharyngeal muscle involvement
  • B. Gottron's rash
  • D. Pain in limbs
Q432

Presence of erythematous flat topped papules over knuckle called Gottron's sign is a characteristic finding in which of the following?** *(NBE)

  • A. Dermatomyositis
  • B. Wegener's granulomatosis
  • D. Progressive systemic sclerosis.
Q433

In idiopathic polymyositis, following are seen except:** *(PGI June 97)

  • A. Ocular muscles are not involved
  • B. Pharyngeal muscles involved
  • C. Cutaneous manifestations
  • D. Proximal limb muscles invariably involved
Q434

All of the following are feature of dermatomyositis, Except:** *(AIIMS Nov 09)

  • A. Salmon Patch
  • B. Gottron's patch
  • C. Mechanic finger
  • D. Periungual telangiectasias
Q435

True about polymyositis is:** *(PGI June 99)

  • A. Ocular muscle involvement
  • B. Pharyngeal involvement
  • C. Muscle atrophy
  • D. Brisk reflexes common
Q436

Muscular weakness due to deficiency of magnesium is enhanced by presence of:** *(PGI June 97)

  • D. Hypernatremia
Q437

Episodic generalized weakness can occur due to all of the following acute electrolyte disturbances, except:** *(AIIMS May 06)

  • B. Hypocalcemia
  • C. Hyponatremia
  • D. Hypophosphatemia

  • A. Eaton Lambert Syndrome

  • A. Channelopathy
  • B. Lambert Eaton Syndrome

SCN4A defect is seen in:** *(NBE)

  • B. Hyperkalemic periodic paralysis
  • C. Lambert Eaton syndrome
  • D. Myotonic dystrophy

  • B. Episodic ataxia type 1

  • B. Optic Neuritis

McDonald's Criteria are used for diagnosis of:** *(NBE)

  • A. No response
  • B. Inappropriate words
  • C. Incomprehensible sounds
  • D. Disoriented response

  • A. Eye opening
  • B. Motor response
  • C. Pupil size
  • D. Verbal response

  • A. 7
  • B. 9
  • C. 11
  • D. 13

Total score in Glasgow Coma Scale of a conscious person is:** *(AI 2006)

  • A. 8
  • B. 3
  • C. 15
  • D. 10

  • A. 4
  • C. 2
  • D. 1

  • D. Hypothermia

  • C. Saccular aneurysms

  • C. Injury to cortical bridging veins

Fundus examination showed papilledema. Two months ago, she also had a fall in her bathroom and struck her head against a wall. The most likely clinical diagnosis is:** *(AIIMS Nov 04)

  • B. Left parietal glioma
  • C. Left MCA territory stroke
  • D. Left chronic subdural haematoma

  • A. Progressive supranuclear palsy
  • B. Left cerebral tumor
  • C. Left sided stroke

He is unresponsive with the dilated right pupil when he reaches the emergency department. The most important step in initial management is:** *(AI 2002)

  • A. Craniotomy
  • B. CT scan of the head
  • C. X-ray of the skull and cervical spine
  • D. Doppler ultrasound examination of the neck

  • C. Intracranial hemorrhage
  • D. Intraabdominal bleed

  • B. Ipsilateral hemiplegia
  • C. Cheyne stokes respiration
  • D. Decorticate rigidity

Kernohan-Woltman sign refers to?** *(NBE)

  • D. Bilateral nystagmus

  • A. Frusemide
  • B. Mannitol
  • C. Steroid
  • D. Hyperventilation

  • D. Orbital Pseudotumor

  • A. Allodynia
  • B. Hyperalgesia
  • C. Hyperesthesia
  • D. Hyperpathia
