  • A. CMV
  • B. Varicella
  • C. HPV
  • D. EBV
Q86

All of the following antibiotics are known to cause acute interstitial nephritis, except : *(NBE)

  • A. Erythromycin
  • B. Sulphonamides
  • C. Ciprofloxacin
  • D. Aminoglycosides
Q87

Interstitial nephritis is seen with all except: *(AIIMS May 07)

  • A. Beta lactam inhibitors
  • B. INH
  • C. Diuretics
  • D. Allopurinol
Q88

Salt losing nephritis is a feature of : *(AI 2000)

  • A. Interstitial nephritis
  • B. Renal Amyloidosis *(AIIMS 95)
  • C. Lupus nephritis *(AIIMS May 94)
  • D. Post Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis
Q89

Necrotizing papillities is seen in all of the following except: *(AIIMS May 02)

  • A. Salicylate poisoning
  • B. Renal vascular thrombosis
  • C. PNH
  • D. Diabetes mellitus
Q90

Necrotizing pappillitis may be seen in all of the following conditions except : *(AI 2002)

  • A. Sickle cell disease
  • B. Tuberculous pyelonephritis
  • C. Diabetes mellitus
  • D. Analgesic nephropathy
Q91

Necrotizing pappillitis is seen in : *(AIIMS 2002)

  • B. Glomerulonephritis
  • D. Diabetes insipidus
Q92

Necrotizing pappillitis is seen in all of the following except : *(AIIMS May 02)

Q93

Renal papillary necrosis is almost always associated with one of the following conditions : *(AI 99)

  • A. Diabetes-mellitus
  • B. Analgesic-nephropathy
  • C. Chronic pyelonephritis
  • D. Post streptococcal GN
Q94

Which of the following is associated with Renal Papillary Necrosis *(NBE)

  • A. Alcohol
  • B. Heroine
  • C. Morphine
  • D. Tramadol
Q95

Ring sign on pyelogram is pathognomonic of *(NBE)

  • A. Polycystic Kidney
  • B. Papillary Necrosis
  • C. Transitional Cell Carcinoma
  • D. Medullary Sponge Kidney
Q96

All are true of Nephrotic syndrome, except : *(AI 2000)

  • A. RBC casts in urine
  • B. Hypo-proteinemia
  • C. Oedema
  • D. Hyperlipidemia
Q97

All of the following are decreased in Nephrotic syndrome except : *(AI 1997)

  • A. Transferrin
  • B. Ceruloplasmin
  • C. Albumin
  • D. Fibrinogen
Q98

All of the following are decreased in nephrotic syndrome except : *(AIIMS 1998)

  • A. Serum transferrin
  • B. Serum fibrinogen
  • C. Serum ceruloplasmin
  • D. Serum albumin
Q99

Hypercoagulation in Nephrotic syndrome is caused by: *(AI 2010)

  • A. Loss of Antithrombin III
  • B. Decreased Fibrinogen
  • C. Decreased Metabolism of Vitamin K
  • D. Increase in protein C
Q100

Which is seen in nephrotic syndrome (select two options): *(PGI June 99)

  • A. Low serum calcium
  • B. Raised AT – III
  • C. Low lipid
  • D. Platelet activation
Q101

Patient with nephrotic syndrome has decreased amount of which antibody- *(NBE)

  • A. IgG
  • B. IgE
  • C. IgM
  • D. IgA
Q102

Nephrotic syndrome may be associated with: *(PGI 2000)

  • A. ↓ Total cholesterol
  • B. ↓ LDL cholesterol
  • C. ↓ VLDL cholesterol
  • D. ↓ Triglycerides
  • E. ↓ HDL cholesterol
Q103

Most common cause of nephrotic range proteinuria in an adult is: *(AI 2007)

  • A. Diabetes Mellitus
  • B. Amyloidosis
  • C. Hypertensive nephropathy
  • D. Wegner's Granulomatosis
Q104

The Finnish type of congenital nephrotic syndrome occurs due to gene mutations the following protein: *(AI 2009, 08)

  • A. Podocin
  • B. Alpha – actinin
  • C. Nephrin
  • D. CD2 activated protein
Q105

A patient with nephrotic syndrome on longstanding corticosteroid therapy may develop all the following except: *(AI 2002)

  • A. Hyperglycemia
  • B. Hypertrophy of muscle
  • C. Neuropsychiatric symptoms
  • D. Suppression of the pituitary adrenal axis
Q106

Following are features of Acute GN except: *(PGI June 97)

  • A. Polyuria
  • B. Hematuria
  • C. Red cell cast
  • D. Oliguria
Q107

Post-infective glomerulonephritis present as: *(AI 1996)

  • A. ARF
  • B. Nephrotic syndrome
  • C. Nephritic syndrome
  • D. Asymptomatic hematuria
Q108

Cresentirc Glomerulonephritis may be seen in all of the following Except? *(AI 2008)

  • A. Post Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
  • B. Henoch Schonlein Purpura (HSP)
  • C. Anti Basement Membrane Disease
  • D. Alport syndrome
Q109

Crescent formation is characteristic of the following glomerular disease: *(AI-2002)

  • A. Minimal change disease
  • B. Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
  • C. Focal and segmental glomerulonephritis
  • D. Rapidly non progressive glomerulonephritis
Q110

All can cause R.P.G.N., except : *(AIIMS June 97)

  • A. Minimal change glomerulonephritis
  • B. Poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis
  • C. Wegener granulomatosis
  • D. S.L.E.
Q111

RPGN occurs in A/E: *(AIIMS Sep 96)

  • A. SLE
  • B. Post streptococcal glomerulonephritis
  • C. Diabetic nephropathy
  • D. Good pastures syndromes
Q112

Which of the following conditions are associated with pauci – immune crescenteric glomerulonephritis: *(AI 2009)

  • A. Henoch – Schonlein Nephritis
  • B. Lupus Nephritis (SLE)
  • C. Microscopic polyangitis
  • D. Nephritis in Alport's syndrome
Q113

All are features of RPGN except *(AIIMS June 1997)

  • A. Rapid recovery
  • B. Crescent formation
  • C. Hypertension
  • D. Non-selective proteinuria
Q114

The prognosis of rapidly proliferating glomerulonephritis (Crescentic GN) depends upon – *(AIIMS

  • A. Number of crescents
  • B. Size of crescents
  • C. Shape of crescents
  • D. Cellularity of crescents
Q115

Non –proliferative Glomerulonephritis include all of the following, Except: *(AI 2008)

  • A. Focal Segmental Glomerulonephritis (FSGS)
  • B. Mesangiocapillary Glomerulonephritis
  • C. Membranous Glomerulonephritis
  • D. Amyloidosis
Q116

A 30-year-old man presents with generalized edema and hypertension. Urine examination shows subnephrotic proteinuria (< 2gm) and microscopic hematuria. Serum complement levels are decreased and he is positive for anti-hepatitis C antibodies. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AI 2012)

  • A. Post streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
  • B. Cryoglobulinemia
  • C. Membranoproliferative Glomerulonephritis (MPGN)
  • D. Focal Segmental Glomerular Sclerosis (FSGS)
Q117

A 60-year-old woman presents with generalized edema, skin ulceration and hypertension. Urine examination shows subnephrotic proteinuria (<2gm) and microscopic haematuria. Serum complement levels are decreased and she is positive for anti-hepatitis C antibodies. The likely diagnosis is: *(AI 2012)

  • A. Post-streptococcal Glomerulonephritis
  • B. Essential Mixed Cryoglobulinemia
  • D. Focal SegmentalGlomerulosclerosis (FSGS)
Q118

Which of the following is not true about Berger's disease? *(AI / AIIMS)

  • A. The pathological changes are proliferative and usually confined to mesangial cells; usually focal and
  • B. Hematuria may be gross or microscopic
  • C. On immunofluorescence deposits contain both IgA and IgG
  • D. Absence of associated proteinuria is pathognomonic
Q119

IgA-nephropathy is seen in: *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. Membranous glomerulonephritis
  • B. Mesangioproliferative glomerulonephritis
  • C. Focal glomerulonephritis
  • D. Cresentic glomerulonephritis
Q120

Which of the following is not true about IgA nephropathy- *(NBE)

  • A. Episodic gross hematuria
  • B. Deposition of IgA in mesangium
  • C. Nephrotic syndrome is a common presentation
  • D. Persistent proteinuria for 6 months or longer points towards bad clinical outcomes
Q121

All of the following statements about IgA Glomerulonephritis are true, except: *(NBE)

  • A. Immune complex–mediated glomerulonephritis
  • B. Diffuse mesangial IgA deposits
  • C. Co-distribution of IgM, IgG, C3, or immunoglobulin light chains with IgA.
  • D. IgA deposits are monomeric and of the IgA2 subclass
Q122

Increased IgA deposits are seen in: *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. Henoch Schonlein Purpura
  • B. Minimal Change Glomemlonephritis
  • C. Chronic Pyelonephritis
  • D. Haemolytic Uremic Syndrome
Q123

A 6 yr child presents with recurrent episodes of gross hematuria for 2 yrs. He is likely to have: *(AI

  • A. IgA nephropathy
  • B. Wilm's tumour
  • C. Henoch Schonlein Purpura (HSP)
  • D. Neuroblastoma
Q124

The most common presentation of IgA nephropathy is: *(NBE)

  • A. Nephritic syndrome
  • B. Nephritic syndrome
  • C. Microscopic hematuria
  • D. Repeated gross hematuria
Q125

A feature of Renal vaculitis in children is - *(AIIMS- Sep- 96)

  • A. IgA raised
  • B. Antinuclear antibody in serum
  • C. Low complement level
  • D. Cytoplasmic antinuclear Ab in serum
Q126

Marker for the renal vasculitis in children is – *(AI 98)

  • A. Increased IgA level
  • B. Low complement level
  • C. ↑ Antineutrophilic cytoplasmic antibody titre
  • D. Increase antinuclear antibody
Q127

12 years old Shyam presented with gross hematuria with 80% dysmorphic RBC's 2 days after a attack of upper respiratory tract infection. Diagnosis is – *(AIIMS Nov- 01)

  • A. Microangiopathic thrombotic anaemia
  • B. IgA Nephropathy
  • C. PSGN
  • D. H.S. purpura
Q128

A six year old male baby presents to a hospital with recurrent gross hematuria for 2 years. There is no h/o burning micturition or pyuria. Urine routine examination demonstrated no pus cells and urine culture was sterile. Serum C3 levels were normal. What is the most probable diagnosis – *(AIPGMEE-08)

  • A. Wilm's tumour
  • C. Post –streptococcal glomerulonephritis
  • D. Urinary tract infection
Q129

A young man develops gross haematuria 3 days after an attack of URTI; likely renal pathology is: *(AI 2001)

  • A. Acute glomerulonephritis
  • B. Minimal change disease
  • C. IgA nephropathy
  • D. Membranous glomerulonephritis.
Q130

A female patient Nandini presents with upper respiratory tract infection. After 15 days, she develops hematuria. Probable diagnosis is: *(AIIMS June 99)

  • B. Wegener's granulomatosis
  • C. Henoch scholein purpura
  • D. Post streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
Q131

True about Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis is: *(AI 2000)

  • A. 50% of cases occur after pharyngitis
  • B. Early treatment of Pharyngitis eliminates the risk of P.S.G.N.
  • C. Glomerulonephritis, secondary to skin infection, is more common in summer
  • D. Recurrence is seen
Q132

A patient presents with hemoptysis and hematuria few weeks after a respiratory tract infection. ANCA antibodies are present. Likely diagnosis is: *(AI 1995)

  • A. Goodpasture's syndrome
  • C. Nephrotic syndrome
  • D. PSGN
Q133

Rama, a 20 year old smoker, presents with hemoptysis and hematuria. Likely diagnosis is: *(AI 1997)

  • A. Good pasture's syndrome
  • C. Gullian Barre syndrome
  • D. IgA Nephropathy
Q134

A 22 year old presents to his physician with hemoptysis and hematuria with basement membrane antibodies. Most likely diagnosis is: *(NBE)

  • A. Wagner's Granulomatosis
  • B. Goodpasture's syndrome
  • C. PAN
  • D. Churg-Strauss syndrome
Q135

Good pasture's syndrome is charachterized by: *(PGI June 99)

  • A. Anti-GBM antibodies
  • B. Crescentic glomerulonephritis
  • C. Diffuse alveolar haemorrhage
Q136

Good pasture's syndrome is characterized by all of the following, except: *(AIIMS May 08)

  • A. Glomerulonephritis
  • B. Leucocytoclastic Vasculitis
  • D. Presence of antibodies to Basement Membrane
Q137

Association of deafness & nephritis is seen in – *(AIIMS May 93)

  • A. Pickwickian syndrome
  • B. Alport's syndrome
  • C. Fabry's disease
  • D. Lawrence Moon Biedl syndrome
Q138

A 25 year old boy presents with renal failure. His uncle died of renal failure three years ago. Slit lamp examination reveals Lenticonus / Keratoconus. The likely diagnosis is: *(AIIMS Nov 2010)

  • A. Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney (ADPCKD)
  • B. Autosomal Recessive polycystic kidney (ARPCKD)
  • C. Alport's syndrome
  • D. Denysh-Drash Syndrome
Q139

Mutation in alpha 5 chain of collagen 4. The diagnosis – *(AIIMS Nov- 06)

  • A. Alport's syndrome
  • B. Thin membrane disease
  • C. Nodular glomerulosclerosis
  • D. Good pasture syndrome
Q140

Presenting manifestation of Alport syndrome is : *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • A. Haematuria
  • B. Proteinuria
  • C. Oliguria
  • D. Sensorineural deafness
Q141

Disease, does not recur in the kidney after renal transplant is : *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • A. Alport syndrome
  • C. Good Pasteur's syndrome
  • D. Diabetic nephropathy
Q142

A 7 year old boy presented with generalized edema. Urine examination revealed marked albuminuria. Serum biochemical examinations showed hypoalbuminaemia with hyperlipidemia. Kidney biopsy was undertaken. On light microscopic examination, the kidney appeared normal. Electron microscopic examination is most likely to reveal – *(AIIMS Nov-03)

  • A. Fusion of foot processes of the glomerular epithelial cells
  • B. Rarefaction of glomerular basement membrane
  • C. Deposition of electron dense material in the basement membrane
  • D. Thin basement membrane
Q143

A 7 year old girl is brought with complaints of generalized swelling of the body. Urinary examination reveals Grade 3 proteinuria and the presence of hyaline and fatty casts. She has no history of Hematuria. Which of the following statements about her condition is true: *(AI 2009)

  • A. No IgG deposits or C3 deposition on Renal biopsy
  • B. Her C3 levels will be low
  • C. IgA Nephropathy is the likely diagnosis
  • D. Alport's syndrome is the likely diagnosis
Q144

True about light microscopy in minimal change disease is: *(AIIMS 2001)

  • A. Loss of foot process seen
  • B. Anti GBM Abs seen
  • C. IgA deposits seen
  • D. No change seen
Q145

True about Light microscopic changes in Minimal Change Glomerulonephritis is: *(AI 95)

  • A. No abnormality
  • B. Fusion of foot process
  • C. Absence of Immunoglobulins
  • D. Absence of complement
Q146

True about Minimal change disease is : *(AI)

  • A. Appears normal under light microscopy but electron microscope shows loss of foot processes.
  • B. Mesangial deposits
  • C. Tram Track appearance
  • D. Gross haematuria
Q147

A child presents with hematuria and nephrotic syndrome. A diagnosis of minimal change disease was made. Which of the following statements about the diagnosis is true: *(All India)

  • A. Glomerular function is lost due to loss of polyanions around the foot processes
  • B. Foot processes of podocytes in the Glomerular membrane are normal
  • C. Glomerular function is lost due to deposition of IgA on the glomerular membrane
  • D. Focal segmental changes are observed
Q148

Typical features of Lipoid nephrosis include: *(PGI 2009)

  • A. Normal light microscopy
  • B. FSGS
  • C. Glomerular tuft sclerosis
  • D. Effacement of foot processes
  • E. Tubular sclerosis
Q149

Clinical features of minimal change glomerulonephritis are all except *(AI 1996)

  • A. Hypertension
  • B. Edema
  • C. Selective proteinuria
  • D. Fever
Q150

Minimal change glomerulopathy may be seen in association with all of the following except : *(AIIMS Nov. 05)

  • A. Hepatitis B
  • B. HIV
  • C. Drug - induced interstitial nephritis
  • D. Hodgkin's disease
Q151

All are steroid resistant except: *(AI 96)

  • A. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
  • B. Minimal change glomerulonephritis
  • C. RPGN
  • D. Recurrent hematuria
Q152

Most common pathological features in diabetes mellitus is : *(AI 1998)

  • A. Papillary necrosis
  • B. Diffuse glomerulosclerosing
  • C. Renal atherosclerosis
  • D. Chronic pyelonephritis
Q153

Which of the following is the first clinically detectable sign of diabetic nephropathy *(NBE)

  • B. Creatinine clearance
  • C. Microalbuminuria
  • D. Macroalbuminuria
Q154

The earliest clinical feature of diabetic nephropathy is: *(NBE)

  • A. Microalbuminuria
  • B. Increased serum creatinine
  • D. Hematuria
Q155

Preferred method for determining microalbuminuria is: *(NBE)

  • A. Urinary dipsticks
  • B. 24 hour urinary protein collection
  • C. Urinary A/C Ratio in a spot voided sample
  • D. Urinary A/C Ratio in a 24 hour collection
Q156

Pathological changes of diabetic nephropathy are all except: *(AI 1995)

  • A. Fibrin caps and capsular drops
  • B. Intercapillary glomerulosclerosis
  • C. Focal sclerosis
  • D. Capillary basement membrane thickening
Q157

The Recommended treatment of early diabetic nephropathy manifested by microalbuminuria is *(AI 1998)

  • A. Strict glycemic control
  • B. Low protein Diet
  • C. Strict glycemic control and Low Protein Diet
  • D. Strict glycemic control, Low protein Diet and ACE Inhibitors
Q158

The term 'HIVAN' is used for *(NBE)

  • A. FSGS
  • B. Acute diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis
  • D. Membranous glomerulopathy
Q159

Minimal change disease patients with steroid resistance can develop *(NBE)

  • C. microscopic polyangitis
  • D. Membranous Glomerulonephritis
Q160

Lobar glomerulonephritis is also called as *(NBE)

  • B. Membranous glomerulonephritis
  • C. MPGN
  • D. Minimal Change Dsisease
Q161

Dense deposit disease includes *(NBE)

  • A. MPGN type I
  • B. MPGN type II
  • C. MPGN type III
  • D. all of the above
Q162

Which cause of nephrotic syndrome have highest incidence of thrombotic complications *(NBE)

  • A. Minimal Change Disease
  • D. Diabetic Nephropathy
Q163

Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in elderly is due to *(NBE)

  • D. MPGN
Q164

Nephrotic range proteinuria indicated by protein: creatinine ratio of *(NBE)

  • A. >100-150 mg/mmol
  • B. >200-250 mg/mmol
  • C. >250-300 mg/mmol
  • D. >300-350 mg/mmol
Q165

In FSGS Pathological Changes Are Most Prominent In Glomeruli Located at *(NBE)

  • A. Corticomedullary Junction
  • B. Outer Cortex
  • C. Middle Cortex
  • D. Medulla
Q166

Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesion are eosinophilic, PAS nodule and relates best with following histopathological finding *(NBE)

  • A. Thickening Of GBM
  • B. Expansion Of Mesangial Matrix
  • C. Nodular Glomerulosclerosis
  • D. Hyaline Arteriosclerosis
Q167

Most characteristic GN in HIV is: *(PGI Dec99)

  • A. FSGN
  • B. MPGN
  • C. MCD
  • D. RPGN
Q168

HIV renal specific nephropathy is: *(NBE)

  • A. Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis
  • B. Membranoproliferative Glomerulonephritis
  • C. Mesangioproliferative Glomerulonephritis
Q169

HIV associated nephropathy is a type of: *(AIIMS Nov 04)

  • B. Immunotaetoid glomerulopathy
  • C. Collapsing glomerulopathy
  • D. Fibrillary glomerulopathy
Q170

Collapsing glomerulopathy, features: *(NBE)

  • A. Tuft necrosis
  • B. Mesangiolysis
  • C. Proliferation of parietal epithelium cells
  • D. Hypertrophy of visceral epithelium cells
Q171

In Lupus nephritis, characteristic kidney lesion is: *(PGI Dec 98)

  • A. Mesangial proliferation
  • B. Tubular fibrin deposits
  • C. Wire loop lesions
  • D. IgG deposits
Q172

Wire loop lesions are often characteristic for the following class of lupus nephritis: *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. Mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis(WHO class II)
  • B. Focal proliferative glomerulonephritis (WHO class III)
  • C. Diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis (WHO class IV)
  • D. Membranous glomerulonephritis (WHO class V)
Q173

All of the following factors are associated with adverse prognosis and high risk of Renal progression in Lupus Nephritis, Except: *(PGI Dec 03)

  • A. High levels of Anti-ds DNA
  • B. Persistant proteinuria (Nephrotic range > 3gm/day)
  • C. Hypocomplementenemia
  • D. Anti LA (SSB)
Q174

A patient who has been diagnosed with bronchiectasis 5 years ago presents with edema on legs and proteinuria. The most likely finding in his kidney will be: *(AI 2012)

  • A. Minimal Change Disease
  • B. Amyloid Nephropathy
  • C. Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN)
  • D. Crescentic Glomerulonephritis
Q175

In Leprosy most common renal lesion seen is: *(AI 1999)

  • A. MGN
  • B. MPGN
  • C. Focal glomerulosclerosis
  • D. Diffuse glomerulosclerosis
Q176

Reflux Nephropathy with proteinuria in the nephrotic range may be seen in patients with : *(AIIMS Nov 06)

  • A. Membranous glomerulonephritis
  • B. Focal segmental Glomerulosclerosis
  • C. Nodular glomerulosclerosis
  • D. Crescentic glomerulonephritis
Q177

A 30 year old man presents with generalized edema and hypertension. Urine examination shows is positive for antihepatitis C antibodies. The most likely diagnosis is : *(AI 2007)

  • A. Post streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
  • B. Mixed cryoglobulinemia
  • C. Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN)
  • D. Focal symentat Glomerular sclerosis (FSGS)
Q178

A 60 year old woman presents with generalized edema, skin ulceration and hypertension. Urine are decreased and she is positive for anti-hepatitis C antibodies. The likely diagnosis is : *(AI 2007)

  • A. PSGN
  • B. Essential mixed cryoglobulinemia
  • C. Membrano proliferative glomerulonephritis
  • D. Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis
Q179

Serum C3 is persistently low in the following except: *(AI / AIIMS)

  • A. Post streptococcal glomerulonephritis
  • B. Membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis
  • C. Lupus nephritis
  • D. Glomerulonephritis related to bacterial endocarditis
Q180

All of the following are associated with low complement levels except: *(All India)

  • A. Lupus nephritis
  • B. Mesangio capillary glomerulonephritis
  • C. Diarrhea-associated hemolytic uremic syndrome
  • D. Post-infections glomerulonephritis
Q181

Hypocomplementemia is seen (select three options): *(PGI June 2008)

  • B. Membranous GN
  • C. Focal segmental DN
  • D. MPGN
  • E. Infective endocarditis.
Q182

Proliferative glomerular deposits in kidney are found in *(AI-2000)

  • A. Amyloidosis
  • C. IgA nephropathy
  • D. Membranous glomerulonephritis
Q183

Type II RTA is associated with all of the following, EXCEPT *(AI-1990)

  • A. Normal Anion Gap Acidosis
  • B. Hyper calciuria
  • C. Decreased urinary citrate
  • D. Minimum urinary pH < 5.5
Q184

Type I RTA is associated with all of the following, Except: *(AI 1993)

  • A. Urinary PH > 5.5
  • B. Normal Anion Gap Acidosis
  • C. ↑ urinary calcium
  • D. ↑ urinary citrate
Q185

Which of the statements about Renal Tubular Acidosis type I (Type I RTA) is not true: *(AI 2012)

  • A. Failure to acidify urine to a pH < 5
  • B. Associated with increased risk of urinary stones
  • C. Associated with Hyperkalemia
  • D. Treatment involves alkali replacement as bicarbonate
Q186

All are feature of type 1 RTA except *(NBE)

  • A. Nephrocalcinosis
  • B. Growth Failure
  • C. Fanconi's Syndrome
  • D. Rickets Or Osteomalacia
Q187

In type 1 RTA patients fail to acidify urine to pH *(NBE)

  • A. <5.5
  • B. <6.5
  • C. <7.0
  • D. <7.5
Q188

Distal renal tubular acidosis is associated with: *(NBE)

  • A. Oxalate stones
  • B. Citrate
  • C. Calcium stones
  • D. Uric acid stones
Q189

Following type of stone is formed in type I RTA *(NBE)

  • A. Uric Acid
  • B. Cystine
  • C. Calcium Phosphate
  • D. Calcium Oxalate
Q190

Proximal & distal renal tubular acidosis is differentiated by all, except: *(AIIMS June 98)

  • A. Stones in kidney
  • C. Daily acid secretion
  • D. Presence of Fanconi syndrome
Q191

Which of the following statements about Type II Renal Tubular Acidosis is TRUE - *(NBE)

  • A. Increased Anion Gap
  • B. >15% excretion of filtered bicarbonate
  • C. Renal stone formation seen
  • D. High serum potassium
Q192

All are features of renal tubular acidosis type I, except : *(AIIMS Sept 96)

  • A. Stone in kidney
  • B. No anion gap
  • D. Fanconi syndrome
Q193

Serum potassium is high in which type of RTA *(NBE)

  • A. 1
  • B. 2
  • C. 4
Q194

Positive Urinary Anion Gap helps to establish the diagnosis of : *(AI 2009)

  • A. Alcoholic ketoacidosis
  • B. Diabetic ketoacidosis
  • C. Renal tubular Acidosis
  • D. Acidosis in Diarrhea
Q195

Dent's disease is characterised by all except: *(NBE)

  • A. Chloride channel defect
  • B. Affects males more than females
  • C. Low Molecular Weight proteinuria
  • D. Distal Renal Tubule defect
Q196

An 8 yrs old boy presents to casualty with history of diarrhoea, followed by decreased urine output. Blood examination shows thrombocytes 90,000/cm³. Diagnosis is : *(AI 2000)

  • A. Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome.
  • B. Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
  • C. Hemophilia
  • D. Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura
Q197

Which of the following statements about Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome is least correct: *(AI 2012)

  • A. Usually follows Haemorrhagic colitis
  • B. Often self-limited
  • C. Fever is usually mild or absent
  • D. Serotoxin has no role in pathogenesis
Q198

All are features of haemolytic uremic syndrome, except: *(AIIMS Dec 95)

  • B. Anaemia
  • C. Renal microthrombi
  • D. Neuropsychiatric disturbances
Q199

All of the following statements are true about Hemolytic uremic syndrome except : *(AI 1997)

  • B. Hypofibrinogenemia
  • C. Thrombocytopenia
  • D. Positive coomb's test
Q200

A 20 year old male presents with features of acute renal failure 5 days after an episode of diarrhea. Blood examination shows thrombocytopenia and Hb-10 gm%. Likely cause is: *(AIIMS June 99)

  • A. Haemolytic uremic syndrome
  • B. Hereditary spherocytosis
  • C. Haemolytic crises
  • D. Chronic glomerulonephritis
Q201

Which of the following changes does not occur in malignant hypertension : *(AI 2008)

  • A. Peticheal Haemorrhages on cortical surface
  • B. Fibrinoid necrosis of arterioles
  • C. Intimal concentric thickening
  • D. Hyaline arteriosclerosis
Q202

Commonest histoloical finding in Benign Hypertension is: *(AI 2009)

  • A. Proliferative endarteritis
  • B. Necrotizing arteriolitis
  • C. Hyaline arteriosclerosis
  • D. Cystic Medial Necrosis
Q203

Which characteristic feature is seen in the kidney in Malignant hypertension: *(AI 1994)

  • A. Hyaline necrosis
  • B. Fibrinoid necrosis
  • C. Medial wall hyperplasia
  • D. Micro-aneurysm
Q204

Common pathological changes seen in kidney in benign hypertension are: *(AI 97)

  • A. Fibrinoid necrosis
  • B. Microaneurysm
  • D. Thining of walls
Q205

Increased renin activity is seen in all of the following, except: *(NBE)

  • A. Benign nephrosclerosis
  • B. Malignant nephrosclerosis
  • C. Haemolytic Uremic Syndrome
  • D. Juxtaglomerular cell tumour
Q206

Low Renin Hypertension is seen in all of the following, Except: *(NBE)

  • A. Essential hypertension
  • B. Conn's syndrome
  • C. Renovascular hypertension
  • D. Liddle's syndrome
Q208

Which of the following is the most specific and sensitive screening test for Renovascular Hypertension. *(AIIMS May 01)

  • C. Captopril enhanced radionucleatide scan
Q209

Which of the following is the most specific screening test for renovascular hypertension: *(PGI

Q210

Most common cause of renal artery stenosis in young adults in India is: *(AIIMS Dec 97)

Q211

Renal artery stenosis may occur in all of the following except: *(AI 2006)

Q212

A 20 year old female presents with a blood pressure of 160/110 mm Hg. Clinical examination reveals a bruit in both flanks. Which of the following statements about this patient is not true (select one option) *(PGI 09)

Q213

  • A. 100 cm/s
  • B. 125 cm/s
  • C. 150 cm/s
  • D. 200 cm/s
Q214

Renal vein thrombosis is most commonly associated with : *(AI 2002)

  • A. Diabetic nephropathy
  • B. Membranous glomerulopathy
  • C. Minimal change disease
  • D. Mesangio-proliferative glomerulonephritis
Q215

Renal vein thrombosis (RVT) may be seen in : *(PGI 2009)

  • A. Trauma
  • B. Renal Cell Ca
  • C. Pregnancy
  • D. Nephrotic Syndrome
  • E. Dehydration
Q216

Renal vein thrombosis is most commonly associated with: *(AI 2001)

  • D. Membrano-proliferative glomerulonephritis
Q217

All of the following are causes of Renal Vein Thrombosis, Except: *(PGI Dec 01)

  • A. Membranous Nephropathy
  • C. Lupus Nephritis
  • D. Renal Amyloidosis
  • E. Post streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
Q218

A 10 year old child develops hematuria after 2 days of diarrhoea. Blood film shows fragmented RBCs & thrombocytopenia. Ultrasound shows marked enlargement of both kidneys. The likely diagnosis is: *(AIIMS June 99)

  • A. Acute pyelonephritis
  • B. Disseminated intravascular coagulopathy
  • C. Haemolytic uremic syndrome
  • D. Renal vein thrombosis
Q219

Which of the following is the most common type of Kidney stones *(NBE)

  • A. Calcium Oxalate
  • B. Calcium Phosphate
  • C. Uric Acid
  • D. Struvite
Q220

All of the following statement about Renal Calculi are true, Except: *(AI 1993)

  • A. Cysine stones form in acidic urine
  • B. Struvite stones form in alkaline urine
  • C. Oxalate stones are radiopaque
  • D. Uric acid stones are resistant to ESWL
Q221

Stone which is resistant to lithotripsy: *(AIIMS May 07)

  • A. Calcium oxalate
  • B. Triple phosphate stone
  • C. Cystine stone
  • D. Uric acid stone
Q222

Which of the following stones is hard to break by ESWL: *(AI 2010)

  • A. Calcium Oxalate Monohydrate
  • B. Calcium Oxalate Dihydrate
  • C. Uric acid
Q223

All of the following types of Renal Stones are Radiopaque, Except: *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. Oxalate
  • B. Uric Acid
  • C. Cystine
  • D. Mixed
Q224

Renal Calculi associated with proteus infection: *(AI 2009)

  • B. Triple Phosphate
  • C. Calcium oxalate
  • D. Xanthine
Q225

Commonest type of Renal stone seen in cases of UTI is: *(AIIMS Nov 1997)

  • A. Phosphate
  • B. Oxalate
  • C. Urate
  • D. Cysteine
Q226

Which of the following element is typical of struvite stones *(NBE)

  • A. Calcium
  • B. Aluminium
  • C. Magnesium
  • D. Zinc
Q227

Ureteric colic due to stone is caused by: *(AI 2008)

  • A. Stretching of renal capsule due to back pressure
  • B. Increased peristalsis of ureter to overcome the obstruction
  • C. Irritation of intramural ureter
  • D. Extravasation of urine.
Q228

Locate the renal stone with pain radiating to medial side of thigh and perineum due to slipping of stone in males: *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. At pelvic brim
  • B. Intramural opening of ureter
  • C. Junction of ureter and renal pelvis
  • D. At crossing of gonadal vessels and ureter
Q229

Referred pain from ureteric colic is felt in the groin due to involvement of the following nerve: *(AI 2003)

  • A. Subcostal
  • B. Iliohypogastric
  • C. Ilioinguinal
  • D. Genitofemoral
Q230

A 10-mm calculus in the right lower ureter associated with proximal hydroureteronephrosis is best treated with: *(AI 2003)

  • A. Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy
  • B. Antegrade percutaneous access
  • C. Open ureterolithotomy
  • D. Ureteroscopic retrieval
Q231

A child presents with abdominal colic and hematuria. On ultrasonography a stone 2.0 cm in diameter is seen in the renal pelvis. The next step in management of this case is - *(AIIMS Nov 2000)

  • A. Pyelolithotomy
  • B. Nephroureterostomy
  • C. Conservative
  • D. ESWL
Q232

Tx used for lower ureteric stone is: *(AIIMS June 98)

  • A. Endoscopic removal
  • B. Diuretics
  • C. Drug dissolution
  • D. Laser
Q233

Chandu, a 45 yrs male shows calcification on the Rt side of his abdomen in an AP view. In lateral view the calcification is seen to overlie the spine. Most likely diagnosis is: *(AI 2001)

  • A. Gallstones
  • B. Calcified mesenteric nodes
  • C. Renal stones
  • D. Calcified rib
Q234

Nephrocalcinosis is seen in all except- *(AIIMS May 07)

  • B. Distal RTA
  • C. Milk alkali syndrome
  • D. Medullary cystic kidney

Nephrocalcinosis is a feature of A/E: *(PGI June 2000)

  • A. Primary hyperparathyroidism
  • B. Medullary sponge kidney
  • C. Vitamin D intoxication
  • D. Pseudo hypoparathyroidism
Q236

Nephrocalcinosis is seen in (select two options): *(PGI Dec 05)

  • A. Medullary sponge disease
  • B. Acute pyelonephritis

A patient is known to have calcium nephrocalcinosis for the post 10 years. All of the following dietary recommendations should be suggested, Except: *(AIIMS Nov 2010)

  • A. Protein Restriction
  • B. Calcium Restriction
  • C. Salt Restriction
Q238

Adult polycystic kidney is inherited as : *(AI 1997)

  • A. Autosomal-co-dominant
  • B. Autosomal dominant
  • C. Autosomal recessive
  • D. X-linked dominant
Q239

Chromosomal location of Adult Polycystic Kidney Disease I and II respectively is: *(NBE)

  • A. Chromosome 16 and 1
  • B. Chromosome 16 and 4
  • C. Chromosome 11 and 5
  • D. Chromosome 21 and 18
Q240

Diagnostic criteria for ADPKD in young adults include *(NBE)

  • A. ≥2 in one and 1 cysts in other kidney
  • B. ≥3 in one and 1 cysts in other kidney
  • C. ≥2 in one and 2 cysts in other kidney
  • D. ≥3 in one and 2cysts in other kidney
Q241

True about adult polycystic kidney disease is all, except: *(AIIMS 2001)

  • A. Autosomal dominant inheritance
  • B. Hypertension is rare
  • C. Can be associated with cysts in liver, lungs and pancreas
  • D. Pyelonephritis is common
Q242

Which of the following is associated with adult polycystic kidney disease? *(AIIMS 2001)

  • A. Berry Aneurysm in Circle of Willis
  • B. Saccular aneurysms of aorta
  • C. Fusiform aneurysms of aorta
  • D. Leutic aneurysms
Q243

Polycystic disease of the kidney may have cysts in all of the following organs except : *(AI 2004)

  • C. Pancreas
  • D. Spleen
Q244

Which of the following is the common extrarenal involvement in autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease – *(AIIMS Nov 04)

  • A. Mitral valve prolapse
  • B. Hepatic cysts
  • C. Splenic cysts
  • D. Colonic diverticulosis
Q245

Which one of the following statements is wrong regarding adult polycystic kidney disease *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. Kidneys are enlarged in size
  • B. The presentation is unilateral
  • C. Intracranial aneurysms may be associated
  • D. Typically manifests in the 3rd decade
Q246

All of the following are true about Childhood Polycystic Kidney Disease, Except: *(AI 2009)

  • A. Autosomal Dominant
  • B. Pulmonary Hypoplasia may be seen
  • C. Renal cysts are present at birth
  • D. Congenital Hepatic fibrosis may be seen
Q247

All of the following about Polycystic kidney disease are true, except: *(NBE)

  • A. Defective Gene for adult type I disease is on Chromosome 16
  • B. Defective protein in Autosomal Recessive type is Polycystin
  • C. Adult type 1 disease is more common than Adult type 2
  • D. All of the above are true
Q248

Synthesis of which protein is affected in patients with autosomal recessive polycystic kidney Disease? *(NBE)

  • A. Polycystin 1
  • B. Fibrocystin
  • C. Polycystin 2
  • D. Nephrocystin
Q249

Which of the following is the most common renal cystic disease in infants is? *(AI 2005)

  • A. Polycystic kidney.
  • B. Simple renal cyst.
  • C. Unilateral renal dysplasia.
  • D. Calyceal cyst.
Q250

A 12- year –old boy is referred for evaluation of nocturnal enuresis and short stature. The blood pressure is normal. The blood urea is 112 mg/dl, creatinine 6 mg/dl, sodium 119 mEq/1, potassium 4 mEq/1, calcium 7mg/dl, phosphate 6mg/dl and alkaline phosphatase 400 U/l. Urinalysis shows trace proteinuria with hyaline casts; no red and white cells are seen. Ultrasound shows bilateral small kidneys and the micturating cystourethrogram is normal. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AIIMS Nov 03)

  • A. Alport's syndrome
  • C. Chronic glomerulonephritis
  • D. Nephronophthisis
Q251

An 8 year old child suffering from recurrent attacks of polyurea since childhood presents to the paediatrics OPD. On examination the child is short statured. Vitals and B.P. are normal. Serum Creatinine – 6 mg%, HCO3 – 16 meg, Na-134, K-4.2. On USG bilateral small kidneys are seen. Diagnosis is – *(AIIMS May 01)

  • A. Reflux Nephropathy
  • B. Nephronophthisis
  • C. Polycystic kidney disease
  • D. Medullary cystic kidney disease
Q252

Senior Loken syndrome besides juvenile nephronophthisis best relates to *(NBE)

  • A. Intracranial aneurysm
  • B. Retinitis pigmentosa
  • C. Colonic diverticula
  • D. Polycystic Kidney
Q253

Medullary cystic disease of the kidney is best diagnosed by: *(AI 2002)

  • A. Ultrasound
  • B. Nuclear scan
  • C. Urography
  • D. Biopsy

The most common cause of renal scarring in a 3 year old child is: *(AI 2005)

  • A. Trauma.
  • B. Tuberculosis.
  • C. Vesicoureteral reflux induced pyelonephritis.
  • D. Interstitial nephritis.
Q255

The neonatal kidney achieves concentrating ability equivalent to adult’s kidney by: *(AI 2004)

  • A. One year of age
  • B. Eighteen months of age
  • C. Three to six months of age
  • D. Just before puberty
Q256

The most common histological variant of renal cell carcinoma is: *(AIIMS Nov 2005)

  • A. Clear cell type
  • B. Chromophobe type
  • C. Papillary type
  • D. Tubular type

Chromophobe variant of Renal cell carcinoma is associated with : *(AI 2010)

  • A. VHL gene mutations
  • B. Trisomy of 7 and 17 (+7, +17)
  • C. 3 p deletions (3p-)
  • D. Monosomy of 1 and Y (-1, -Y)
Q258

Subsequently right nephrectomy was done and mass was seen to be composed of cells with clear cytoplasm. Areas of hemorrhage and necrosis were frequent. Cytogenic analysis of this mass is likely to reveal an abnormality of: *(AI 2004)

  • A. Chromosome 1
  • B. Chromosome 3
  • C. Chromosome 11
  • D. Chromosome 17
Q259

Which of the following statement about Renal Cell Carcinoma (Hypernephroma) is false: *(AIIMS Nov 93)

  • B. Histologically are usually Adenocarcinomas

  • D. Radiosensitive

  • C. More common in female
  • D. Arises from proximal convoluted tubule

  • A. Hematuria
  • C. Flank mass
  • D. Abdominal Pain

All can be seen in hypernephroma, Except: *(AIIMS Nov 93)

  • A. Polycythemia
  • B. Renal vein thrombosis
  • D. Hematuria

Paraneoplastic syndrome associated with RCC are all of the following except: *(AI 2009)

  • C. Malignant hypertension
  • D. Cushing syndrome

Most common site for secondary metastasis in a case of Hypernephroma: *(AIIMS Feb 1997)

  • A. Adrenal
  • B. Lungs

  • B. IVC involvement indicates inoperability
  • C. Pre op radiotherapy is not essential

  • C. Arthrogryposis multiplex congenital
  • D. Hemihypertrophy

  • B. Abdominal lump
  • C. Hydronephrosis
  • D. Pain in abdomen
Q268

Which of the following statements about Wilm’s tumor is true: *(AIIMS Nov 92)

  • A. Always Unilateral
  • B. Associated with poor prognosis
  • C. Most common site of metastasis is bone
Q269

  • B. Loss of heterozygosity of chromosome 1p
  • C. Histology
  • D. Age less than one year at presentation

  • B. International Society of Pediatric Oncology (SIOP)
  • C. AJCC TNM
  • D. Chadwick
Q271

Features of Hepatorenal syndrome are (select two options): *(PGI June 06)

  • A. Urine sodium < 10 meq/l
  • B. Normal renal histology
  • C. Renal function abnormal even after liver become normal
  • D. Proteinuria < 500 mg/day
Q272

Which of the following statements is incorrect with regard to Hepatorenal syndrome in a patient with cirrhosis *(AI 2003)

  • B. Urinary sodium < 10mq/L
  • C. Urine osmolality lower than plasma osmolality
  • D. No sustained improvement in renal function after volume expansion.

  • A. The creatinine clearance is > 40 ml/min
  • B. The urinary sodium is less than 10 mmol/L
  • C. The urine osmolality is lower than the plasma osmolality
  • D. There is poor response to volume expansion

  • A. Acute Renal Failure
  • B. Hypophosphatemia
  • D. Acute Myocardial Infarction

  • A. Cocaine Intoxication
  • B. Hyperphosphatemia
  • D. Crush injury

  • B. Viper snake venom
  • D. Multiple Hornet Stings
Q277

All of the following are true about Rhabdomyolysis, Except: *(PGI Dec 04)

  • A. Hyperuricemia
  • D. ↑ Creatine kinase
Q278

Features of Rhabdomyolysis include all of the following, Except: *(PGI Dec 04)

  • A. Acute muscular weakness
  • B. Myoglobinuria
  • C. Hemoglobinuria
  • D. Acute Renal Failure

Tumor lysis syndrome is associated with all of the following laboratory feature except: *(NBE)

  • C. Hyperuricemia
  • D. Hyperphosphatemia

All of the following are seen in Tumor Lysis Syndrome, except: *(NBE)

  • A. Serum creatinine
  • B. Serum phosphate
  • C. Serum potassium
  • D. Serum Uric acid
Q282

In Barter’s syndrome, what is seen- *(NBE)

  • D. Decrease in urinary calcium
Q283

All of the following statements about Bartter Syndrome and Gitelman’s Syndrome are true, except:

  • B. Bartter Syndrome Presents earlier in life than Gitelman’s Syndrome
  • C. Genetic defect in Bartter Syndrome involves the transport proteins in the distal tubule
  • D. Hypercalciuria is more common in Bartter Syndrome

Gitelman’s syndrome resembles the effects of which of the following drugs? *(NBE)

  • A. Thiazide
  • B. Furosemide
  • D. Amiloride

An adolescent male presents with Hypokalemia, Metabolic Alkalosis, Hypercalciuria and Nephrocalcinosis. His Blood Pressure is normal. The likely diagnosis is: *(NBE)

  • B. Gitelman’s Syndrome
  • C. Liddle’s Syndrome
  • D. Renal Tubular Acidosis

Gitelman’s syndrome is distinguished from Bartter’s syndrome by *(NBE)

  • D. Hypomagnesemia and hypocalciuria
Q287

All of the following are features of Bartter’s syndrome, Except: *(NBE)

  • C. Hyperprostaglaridine
  • D. Hyper calciurea
Q288

All of the following statements about Bartter’s Syndrome are true, except: *(NBE)

  • A. Genetic defect in transport protein of distal tubule
  • D. Increase in urinary calcium
Q289

Which type of Bartter’s syndrome is associated with mutations in barttin? *(NBE)

  • A. Type 1
  • B. Type 2
  • C. Type 3
  • D. Type 4
Q290

Causes of hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with hypertension: *(NBE)

  • C. Gitelman’s syndrome
Q291

Liddle’s syndrome includes all except: *(NBE)

  • C. High Aldosterone Levels
  • D. Metabolic Alkalosis
Q292

Which of the following is the most important strategy to prevent contrast nephropathy: *(NBE)

  • A. Fenoldopam
  • B. N-Acetylcysteine
  • C. Hydration with normal saline
Q293

All of the following pharmacological agents may be used for reducing risk of contrast nephropathy, except: *(NBE)

  • C. Prostaglandins
  • D. Furosemide
Q294

All of the following are strategies to prevent contrast nephropathy, except: *(NBE)

  • A. Intravenous Hydration
  • B. Use of High Osmolar Contrast Agents
  • C. Theophylline administration

Gastroenterology

254 Questions

▼
Q1

(AI 2008) A 30-year-old female patient presents with non-progressive dysphagia for both solids and liquids. The characteristic finding on barium swallow that will confirm the probable diagnosis is:

  • A. Dilated esophagus with narrow lower end (Rat tail esophagus)
  • B. Multiple sacculations and pseudodiverticulae (Corkscrew esophagus)
  • C. Narrow and irregular esophageal lumen
  • D. Stricture ulcer in the esophagus
Q2

(AIIMS Nov 2003) Dysphagia lusoria is due to:

  • A. Oesophageal diverticulum
  • B. Aneurysm of aorta
  • C. Oesophageal web
  • D. Compression by aberrant blood vessel
Q3

(AI 2009) Non-progressive contractions of the esophagus are:

  • A. Primary
  • B. Secondary
  • C. Tertiary
  • D. Quaternary
Q4

A lady presented with progressive dysphagia only for solids. Barium study showed proximal esophageal dilatation with distal constriction. The most likely diagnosis is:

  • A. Peptic stricture
  • B. Carcinoma esophagus
  • C. Achalasia cardia
  • D. Lower esophageal ring (Schatzki ring)
Q5

(AIIMS May 2006) A 40-year-old female patient presents with dysphagia to both liquids and solids and regurgitation for 3 months. The dysphagia was non-progressive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

  • A. Carcinoma of the esophagus
  • B. Lower oesophageal mucosal ring
  • D. Reflux esophagitis with esophageal stricture
Q6

(GI June 2004) Intermittent dysphagia is caused by (select two best options):

  • A. Stricture
  • B. Achalasia cardia
  • C. Pharyngeal diverticulum
  • D. Diffuse esophageal spasm
Q7

Oesophageal motility disorders are best diagnosed by:

  • A. Barium studies
  • B. Endoscopy
  • C. 24-hour pH monitoring
  • D. Esophageal manometry
Q8

(PGI Dec 2003) Investigation of choice for dysphagia for solids:

  • A. Barium swallow
  • C. X-ray chest
  • D. CT scan
Q9

A patient presents with dysphagia of 4 weeks duration. He is now able to swallow liquid food only. Which of the following is the one investigation to be done?

  • A. Barium studies are the best to be done
  • B. Upper GI endoscopy is to be done
  • C. CT scan is needed
Q10

(PG June 2002) True about achalasia cardia (select two best options):

  • A. Dysphagia is a presenting symptom
  • B. Esophagectomy is the treatment
  • C. Motility-improving agents are used in treatment
  • D. Barium swallow shows irregular filling defect in lower esophagus
Q11

(PGI June 2000) In achalasia cardia, true is:

  • A. Pressure at distal end increased with no peristalsis
  • B. Low pressure at LES with no peristalsis
  • C. Pressure > 50 mmHg with peristalsis
  • D. Pressure at the distal end increased with normal relaxation
Q12

(AIIMS May 1996) A young patient with dysphagia more for liquids than solids regurgitates food often at night. Radiography shows a rat-tailed appearance. Likely diagnosis is:

  • A. Achalasia cardia
  • B. Carcinoma oesophagus
  • C. Zenker's diverticulum
Q13

(AIIMS June 2003) A young patient presents with history of dysphagia more to liquids than solids. The first investigation you will do is:

  • B. Esophagoscopy
  • C. Ultrasound of the chest
  • D. CT scan of the chest

(AIIMS Nov 1993) Heller's operation is done for:

  • B. Pyloric stenosis
  • C. Peptic ulcer
  • D. Achalasia cardia

In which of the following conditions is Heller's operation done?

  • C. Strictures of esophagus
  • D. Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
Q16

Diffuse esophageal spasm is best diagnosed by:

  • A. Endoscopy
  • B. Manometry
  • C. Barium swallow
Q17

Corkscrew esophagus is seen in which of the following conditions?

  • A. Carcinoma esophagus
Q18

A young patient has dysphagia and symptoms of GERD. The barium swallow showed a corkscrew esophagus. What would be the MOST likely diagnosis?

  • B. Esophageal stricture
  • C. Carcinoma esophagus
Q19

On barium swallow, 'corkscrew esophagus' is seen in which of the following?

  • A. Esophagitis
Q20

All of the following statements about Zenker's diverticulum are true EXCEPT:

  • A. Acquired diverticulum
  • B. Pulsion diverticulum
  • C. False diverticulum
  • D. Out-pouching of the anterior pharyngeal wall

(AI 2001) A male aged 60 years has foul breath. He regurgitates food that was eaten 3 days ago. A gurgling sound is often heard on swallowing. Likely diagnosis is:

  • A. Zenker's diverticulum
  • B. Meckel's diverticulum
  • C. Scleroderma

Q23

  • A. Dysphonia
  • B. Gastroesophageal reflux
  • C. Lung abscess
  • D. Perforation
Q24

Which of the following is TRUE about Zenker's diverticulum?

  • A. It is asymptomatic
  • B. Occurs in the mid-esophagus
  • C. Treatment is simple excision
  • D. It occurs in children

(PGI Dec) Schatzki's ring is:

  • A. Mucosal ring at squamocolumnar junction
  • B. Muscular ring
  • C. Dysphagia is the presenting symptom
  • D. Inflammatory stricture
Q26

All are true regarding Plummer-Vinson syndrome, EXCEPT:

  • A. Oesophageal web
  • B. Predisposes to malignancy
  • C. Koilonychia
  • D. Common in elderly males
Q27

(AIIMS Nov) Barrett's esophagus is diagnosed by:

  • A. Squamous metaplasia
  • B. Intestinal metaplasia
  • C. Squamous dysplasia
  • D. Intestinal dysplasia
Q28

All except one are true regarding Barrett's oesophagus:

  • A. More incidence of squamous cell carcinoma
  • B. Metaplasia
  • C. Columnar epithelium
  • D. Involves lower oesophagus
Q29

(AIIMS June) Barrett's esophagus can lead to:

  • B. Reflux esophagitis
  • D. Achalasia
Q30

(PG Dec 1998) All of the following are true about Barrett's esophagus, EXCEPT:

  • A. Consequence of prolonged gastroesophageal reflux
  • B. It is premalignant
  • C. Lower oesophageal mucosa is replaced by intestinal type of epithelium
  • D. Associated with oesophageal varices
Q31

(AI) False about Barrett's esophagus is:

  • A. Premalignant condition
  • B. Can be diagnosed by endoscopy
  • C. Biopsy is necessary to confirm diagnosis
  • D. Stricture may be present in distal esophagus
Q32

(AIIMS Nov 1994) What is true regarding Barrett's esophagus?

  • A. Seen in females
  • B. Premalignant
  • C. Responds to conservative management
  • D. Squamous metaplasia is seen
Q33

Barrett's esophagus is commonly associated with:

  • A. Adenocarcinoma
  • D. Gastrointestinal stromal tumor
Q34

(PGI 2009) Which of the following statements about Mallory-Weiss syndrome are true?

  • A. Presents with hemoptysis
  • B. Shows transmural rupture
  • C. Chest pain and shortness of breath
  • D. Shows mucosal tear
Q35

The most prevalent esophageal cancer worldwide is:

  • D. Adenoid cystic carcinoma
Q36

(AIIMS Feb 1997) Most common site of carcinoma esophagus is:

  • A. Middle 1/3rd
  • B. Upper 1/3rd
  • C. Lower 1/3rd
  • D. Lower end of esophagus
Q37

Most common site for squamous cell carcinoma esophagus is:

  • A. Upper third
  • B. Middle third
  • C. Lower third
  • D. Gastroesophageal junction
Q38

(AIIMS Nov 1996, June 2000) The most common site of esophageal adenocarcinoma is:

  • A. Upper 1/3rd
  • B. Middle 1/3rd
  • D. Upper 2/3rd
Q39

The commonest site of carcinoma esophagus in India is:

  • D. GE junction
Q40

(AI 1996) The following are predisposing factors for esophageal carcinoma, EXCEPT:

  • A. Plummer-Vinson syndrome
  • B. Tylosis palmaris
  • C. Chronic achalasia
  • D. Benzene therapy
Q41

(AIIMS Dec 1997) Hyperkeratosis of palm and sole is seen in:

  • A. Carcinoma colon
  • B. Hepatoma
  • C. Adenocarcinoma lung
  • D. Carcinoma oesophagus
Q42

(PGI June 1999) Esophageal adenocarcinoma is predisposed by (select two best options):

  • A. Achalasia
  • C. Corrosive intake
  • D. Barrett's esophagus
Q43

Adenocarcinoma of the esophagus develops in:

  • A. Barrett's esophagus
  • B. Long-standing achalasia
  • C. Corrosive stricture
  • D. Alcohol abuse
Q44

(AI 1998) Adenocarcinoma of esophagus is commonly found in:

  • B. Barrett's oesophagus
  • C. Plummer-Vinson syndrome
  • D. Chronic smoking
Q45

(PGI June 1998, Dec 2003) All of the following statements about esophageal carcinoma are true, EXCEPT:

  • A. More common in men
  • B. Adenocarcinoma is on the rise
  • C. Most common in the elderly
  • D. Dysphagia is the most common symptom
  • E. Pernicious anemia is a risk factor
Q46

Which neoadjuvant chemotherapy is used in esophageal carcinoma?

  • A. Cisplatin
  • B. Cyclophosphamide
  • C. Doxorubicin
  • D. Methotrexate
Q47

(AIIMS May 2001) The commonest side effect of cisplatin in a patient using it for esophageal carcinoma is:

  • A. Acute tubular necrosis
  • B. Thrombocytopenia
  • C. Hepatic failure
  • D. Cardiomyopathy
Q48

Best substitute of esophagus after esophagectomy is:

  • A. Stomach
  • B. Jejunum
  • C. Left colon
  • D. Right colon
Q49

Peptic ulcer is associated with all EXCEPT:

  • B. Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
  • C. Primary hyperparathyroidism
  • D. Pernicious anemia
Q50

(AIIMS Nov 1995) Which one is NOT associated with peptic ulcer?

  • A. Smoking cigarette
  • D. Cirrhosis
Q51

All of the following are associated with gastric ulcer, EXCEPT:

  • A. Ingestion of food does not relieve pain
  • B. High acid output
  • C. Vomiting is common
  • D. Less frequent than duodenal ulcers
Q52

(AI 1992)

  • A. H. pylori infection
  • D. Bile reflux
Q53

(PGI Dec 2000) Stress ulcers seen in burns are:

  • A. Curling's ulcer
  • B. Cushing's ulcer
  • C. Meleney's ulcer
  • D. Rodent ulcer
Q54

Helicobacter pylori is NOT associated with:

  • A. Gastrointestinal lymphoma
  • B. Gastric cancer
  • C. Gastric leiomyoma
  • D. Peptic ulcer
Q55

(AIIMS Nov 2004) Endoscopic biopsy from a case of H. pylori related duodenal ulcer is most likely to reveal:

  • A. Antral predominant gastritis
  • B. Multifocal atrophic gastritis
  • C. Acute erosive gastritis
  • D. Gastric atrophy
Q56

(AI 1999) H. pylori is known to cause all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A. Gastric ulcer
  • B. Duodenal ulcer
  • C. Gastric lymphoma
  • D. Fundal atrophic gastritis
Q57

Diagnostic tests for H. pylori include all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A. Urea-breath test
  • B. Rapid urease test
  • C. Gastric biopsy and Warthin-Starry stain
  • D. SAFA test
Q58

Epidemiological studies of H. pylori are done by using:

  • B. Serological markers
  • C. Culture
  • D. Gastric biopsy urease test
Q59

(AIIMS June 2000) Which of the following is FALSE regarding H. pylori infection?

  • A. With chronic infection, urease breath test becomes negative
  • B. H. pylori infection remains lifelong if untreated
  • C. Endoscopy is diagnostic
  • D. Toxigenic strains usually cause ulcer
Q60

A patient with H. pylori infection is treated with drugs. The best method to detect presence of H. pylori in this person is:

  • A. Rapid urease test
  • B. Urea breath test
  • C. Endoscopy and biopsy
  • D. Serum anti-H. pylori titre
Q61

The preferred test for confirming H. pylori eradication is:

  • A. Urease breath test
  • B. Culture
  • C. Serological test
  • D. Biopsy urease test
Q62

All are true regarding Helicobacter pylori EXCEPT:

  • A. Less prevalent in developing countries
  • B. Urea breath test is positive
  • C. Toxicogenic strains usually cause ulcers
  • D. Gram-negative organism
Q63

(AIIMS May 2018) Which of the following statements about peptic ulcer disease is true?

  • A. Helicobacter pylori eradication increases the likelihood of complications
  • B. The incidence of complications has remained unchanged
  • C. The incidence of Helicobacter pylori reinfection in India is very low
  • D. Helicobacter pylori eradication does not affect recurrence ratio
Q64

(AI 1995) Which drug is NOT effective against H. pylori?

  • A. Colloidal bismuth
  • B. Metronidazole
  • C. Amoxicillin
Q65

All of the following are used for treatment of H. pylori, EXCEPT:

  • A. Gentamycin
  • B. Clarithromycin
  • C. Metronidazole
  • D. Amoxicillin
Q66

(AI 2000) All of the following drugs are commonly used in regimens against H. pylori EXCEPT:

  • A. Oxytetracycline
  • B. Amoxicillin
  • C. Bismuth subcitrate
  • D. Omeprazole
Q67

All are used in treatment of Helicobacter pylori, EXCEPT:

  • B. Cisapride
  • C. Clarithromycin
  • D. Metronidazole
Q68

(AI 1990) Common sites for Cushing's ulcers include all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A. Esophagus
  • B. Stomach
  • C. 1st part of duodenum
  • D. Distal duodenum
Q69

Commonest site of peptic ulcer is:

  • A. 1st part of duodenum
  • B. 2nd part of duodenum
  • C. Distal 1/3 of stomach
  • D. Pylorus of the stomach
Q70

Which of the following types of chronic gastric ulcer is associated with duodenal ulcers?

  • A. Type I
  • B. Type II
  • C. Type III
  • D. Type IV
Q71

Which of the following types of chronic gastric ulcer is associated with acid hypersecretion?

  • A. Type I and Type II
  • B. Type II and Type III
  • C. Type III and Type IV
  • D. Type IV and Type V
Q72

The most common type of chronic gastric ulcer is:

Q73

Most common site of chronic gastric ulcer:

  • A. Pyloric antrum
  • B. Lesser curvature at interface between body and antrum
  • C. Greater curvature
  • D. Fundus
Q74

All of the following are true regarding a patient with acid peptic disease EXCEPT:

  • A. Misoprostol is the drug of choice in patients on NSAIDs
  • B. DU is preventable by the use of single nighttime H2 blockers
  • C. Omeprazole may help ulcers refractory to H2 blockers
  • D. Surgery is the drug of choice in pregnant patients
Q75

(AIIMS June 1993) Surgery of choice for chronic duodenal ulcer is:

  • A. Vagotomy + antrectomy
  • B. Total gastrectomy
  • C. Truncal vagotomy + pyloroplasty
  • D. Highly selective vagotomy
Q76

Patient presents with recurrent duodenal ulcer of 2.5 cm size; procedure of choice:

  • A. Truncal vagotomy and antrectomy
  • B. Truncal vagotomy and gastrojejunostomy
  • C. Highly selective vagotomy
  • D. Laparoscopic vagotomy and gastrojejunostomy
Q77

The lowest recurrence of peptic ulcer is associated with:

  • A. Gastric resection
  • B. Vagotomy + drainage
  • C. Vagotomy + antrectomy
Q78

Dumping syndrome is due to:

  • A. Diarrhoea
  • B. Rapid gastric emptying
  • C. Hypermotility
  • D. Dietary indiscretion
Q79

All are true regarding Early Post-cibal syndrome (Dumping), EXCEPT:

  • A. Distension of abdomen
  • B. Managed conservatively
  • C. Hypermotility of intestine is common
  • D. Surgery is usually indicated
Q80

Most common complication of chronic gastric ulcer is:

  • A. Tea pot stomach
  • B. Scirrhous carcinoma (adenocarcinoma)
  • C. Perforation
Q81

Artery to bleed in duodenal ulcer haemorrhage:

  • A. Splenic artery
  • B. Gastroduodenal artery
  • C. Left gastric artery
  • D. Superior mesenteric artery
Q82

In gastric outlet obstruction in a peptic ulcer patient, the site of obstruction is most likely to be:

  • A. Antrum
  • B. Duodenum (1st part)
  • C. Pylorus
  • D. Pyloric canal
Q83

A posteriorly perforating ulcer in the pyloric antrum of the stomach is most likely to produce initial localized peritonitis or abscess formation in the:

  • A. Omental bursa (lesser sac)
  • B. Greater sac
  • C. Right subphrenic space
  • D. Hepatorenal pouch
Q84

(AI 2003) What is the most characteristic feature of congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?

  • A. Affects the first-born male child
  • B. The pyloric tumour is best felt during feeding
  • C. The patient is commonly marasmic
  • D. Loss of appetite occurs early
Q85

What is true regarding congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?

  • A. More common in girls
  • B. Hypochloremic alkalosis
  • C. Heller's myotomy is the procedure of choice
  • D. Most often manifests at birth
Q86

In a case of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, the metabolic disturbance is:

  • C. Metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria
  • D. Metabolic alkalosis with alkaline urine
Q87

Metabolic changes associated with excessive vomiting include the following:

  • B. Hypernatremia
  • D. Decreased bicarbonates
Q88

A patient with pyloric stenosis secondary to peptic ulcer complains of profuse vomiting. Na⁺ = 125 meq/L, K⁺ = 2.3 meq/L, Cl⁻ = 85 meq/L, BE = -5 meq/L. Patient should be given:

  • A. Half normal saline
  • B. Normal saline with KCl
  • C. K⁺ bolus
  • D. Hypertonic saline
Q89

For which of the following is the Ramstedt's operation done?

  • A. Ileal atresia
  • C. Hirschsprung's disease
  • D. Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis of infancy
Q90

The MOST frequent indication for Nissen fundoplication operation is:

  • A. GERD
  • B. Barrett's esophagus
  • C. Mallory-Weiss syndrome
  • D. Achalasia of esophagus
Q91

All of the following statements about a Dieulafoy's lesion are true EXCEPT:

  • A. Large caliber arteriole beneath gastrointestinal mucosa
  • B. Bleeds through a pinpoint mucosal erosion
  • C. Also called persistent caliber artery
  • D. Common in young patients
Q92

Gastric antral vascular ectasia is the cause of:

  • A. Washerman stomach
  • B. Watermelon stomach
  • C. Windmill stomach
  • D. Windshield stomach
Q93

(AIIMS Dec 1994) All are true regarding Menetrier's disease, EXCEPT:

  • A. Protein loss
  • B. Exophytic growth
  • C. Increased acid secretion
  • D. May be confused with Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
Q94

All of the following statements about Menetrier's disease are true EXCEPT:

  • A. It is a premalignant condition
  • B. Usually increased acid secretion
  • C. May be confused with Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
  • D. Associated with protein-losing enteropathy
Q95

Risk factor for development of gastric carcinoma:

  • A. Blood group O
  • C. Intestinal hyperplasia
  • D. Intestinal metaplasia type III
Q96

Premalignant conditions of stomach include all, EXCEPT:

  • B. Pernicious anemia and achlorhydria
  • C. Hiatus hernia
  • D. Atrophic gastritis
Q97

(PGI Dec 1997) Predisposing causes for carcinoma stomach, EXCEPT:

  • A. Chronic gastric atrophy
  • B. Hyperplastic polyp
  • C. Intestinal metaplasia grade III
Q98

Risk factors for carcinoma stomach include all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Blood group A
  • B. Atrophic gastritis
  • C. Duodenal peptic ulcer
  • D. Partial gastrectomy
Q99

Which one of the following is the most significant factor for development of gastric carcinoma?

  • A. Paneth cell metaplasia
  • B. Pyloric metaplasia
  • C. Intestinal metaplasia
  • D. Ciliated metaplasia
Q100

Most common site for carcinoma stomach is:

  • B. Fundus
  • C. Lesser curvature
  • D. Greater curvature
Q101

When carcinoma of stomach develops secondarily to pernicious anemia, it is usually situated in the:

  • A. Prepyloric region
  • B. Pylorus
  • C. Body
Q102

Early gastric cancer generally indicates:

  • A. Gastric adenocarcinoma detected early
  • B. Gastric adenocarcinoma confined to the mucosa
  • C. Gastric adenocarcinoma confined to the mucosa and submucosa
  • D. Gastric adenocarcinoma less than 1 cm in size
Q103

All the following indicate early gastric cancer EXCEPT:

  • A. Involvement of mucosa
  • B. Involvement of mucosa and submucosa
  • C. Involvement of mucosa, submucosa and muscularis
  • D. Involvement of mucosa, submucosa and adjacent lymph nodes
Q104

The best prognosis in carcinoma stomach is with:

  • A. Superficial spreading type
  • B. Ulcerative type
  • C. Linitis plastica type
  • D. Polypoid fungating type
Q105

An adult presented with hematemesis and upper abdominal pain. Endoscopy revealed a growth at the pyloric antrum. CT showed no invasion. At laparotomy, the posterior wall of stomach and pancreas up to the tail were involved. What is the most appropriate surgical management?

  • A. Closure of the abdomen
  • B. Antrectomy and vagotomy
  • C. Partial gastrectomy + distal pancreatectomy
  • D. Partial gastrectomy + distal pancreatectomy + splenectomy
Q106

(AI 1994) Amongst the following, the most common site for leiomyoma is:

  • B. Small intestine
  • C. Duodenum
  • D. Colon
Q107

Which of the following is NOT a malabsorption syndrome?

  • A. Whipple's disease
  • B. Coeliac disease
  • C. Tropical sprue
  • D. Tangier's disease
Q108

All of the following malabsorption syndromes are characterized by diffuse and specific lesions on histology of small bowel biopsy, EXCEPT:

  • B. Agammaglobulinemia
  • C. Abetalipoproteinemia
  • D. Intestinal lymphangiectasia
Q109

Non-absorption of fat-soluble vitamins is due to:

  • A. Steatorrhea
  • B. Pancreatic endocrine insufficiency
  • C. Both
  • D. None
Q110

Test for assessment of mucosal function of GIT:

  • A. D-xylose test
  • B. Small bowel study
  • C. Biopsy
  • D. Schilling test
Q111

Positive D-xylose test indicates all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Pancreatic insufficiency
  • B. Small intestinal mucosal disease
  • C. Impaired carbohydrate absorption in small intestine
  • D. Malabsorption
Q112

(AIIMS Nov 2002) A 41-year-old patient presented with chronic diarrhea for 3 months. A D-xylose absorption test was ordered to look for:

  • A. Carbohydrate malabsorption due to mucosal disease
  • B. Carbohydrate malabsorption due to chronic pancreatitis
  • C. Fat malabsorption due to mucosal disease
  • D. Fat malabsorption due to chronic pancreatitis
Q113

(AI 1997) Best test for small intestine malabsorption of carbohydrates is:

  • A. Lund meal test
  • B. Schilling test
  • C. D-Xylose test
  • D. Follacin test
Q114

A D-xylose test was requested on a patient with long-standing steatorrhea. 5-hour urine sample showed <4.0 gm excretion after giving 25 gm of D-xylose. The most likely diagnosis is:

  • A. Chronic pancreatitis
  • B. Bacterial overgrowth syndrome
  • C. Ileal disease
  • D. Celiac sprue
  • E. Intestinal lymphangiectasia
Q115

(PGI 2001) Cause of false positive D-xylose test include all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Bacterial overgrowth
  • B. Renal failure
  • E. Blind loop syndrome
Q116

(PGI June 2001) False positive D-xylose test may be seen in:

  • A. Blind loop syndrome
  • B. Ascites
  • C. Antibiotic therapy
  • D. Pyloric stenosis
Q117

(GI 2009) Which of the following statements about Schilling's test are true?

  • A. Abnormal in pernicious anemia
  • B. Normal in bacterial overgrowth syndrome
  • C. Abnormal in ileal disease
  • D. Normal in chronic pancreatitis
Q118

Which of the following is NOT used for investigation of fat malabsorption?

  • A. 13C Trioctanoin
  • B. 13C Triolein
  • C. 13C Tripalmitin
  • D. 13C Triclosan
Q119

Schilling test is abnormal in:

  • A. Intrinsic factor deficiency
  • B. Amylase deficiency
  • C. Lipase deficiency
  • D. Pancreatic endocrine insufficiency
Q120

Which of the following statements regarding the Schilling test for vitamin B12 malabsorption is most accurate?

  • A. The Schilling test results are abnormal in patients with dietary vitamin B12 deficiency
  • B. In pernicious anemia, the results normalize after oral administration of intrinsic factor
  • C. In ileal disease, the results normalize after oral administration of intrinsic factor
  • D. Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency does not cause Schilling test results to be abnormal
Q121

(AI India/AIIMS) A 12-year-old girl has history of recurrent bulky stools and abdominal pain since 3 years of age. She has moderate pallor, weight and height below the 3rd percentile. Most appropriate investigation:

  • A. Small intestinal biopsy
  • B. Barium studies
  • C. 24-hr fecal fat estimation
  • D. Urinary D-xylose test
Q122

(AIIMS Nov 2005) A 30-year-old lady presents with features of malabsorption and iron deficiency anemia. Duodenal biopsy shows complete villous atrophy. Which of the following antibodies is likely to be present?

  • A. Anti-endomysial antibodies
  • B. Anti-goblet cell antibodies
  • C. Anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibodies
  • D. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies
Q123

Cause of thick pancreatic secretions in cystic fibrosis:

  • A. Overproduction of mucin
  • B. Failure to clear mucin due to epithelial dysfunction
  • C. Defect in chloride channel leading to water reabsorption
  • D. Defect in sodium channel leading to water reabsorption
Q124

(AIIMS May) A 30-year-old male with chronic diarrhea, anemia, raised liver enzymes. Most likely associated with:

  • A. Antimitochondrial antibody
  • B. Anti-endomysial antibody
  • C. Anti-smooth muscle antibody
  • D. Antinuclear antibody
Q125

(AI 2011) A patient presents with chronic small bowel diarrhea. Duodenal biopsy shows villous atrophy. Anti-endomysial and anti-tTG antibodies are positive. Treatment of choice:

  • A. Gluten-free diet
  • B. Antibiotics
  • C. Loperamide
  • D. 5-ASA
Q126

Which of the following circulating antibodies has the best sensitivity and specificity for the diagnosis of celiac disease?

  • A. Anti-endomysial antibody
  • B. Anti-tissue transglutaminase antibody
  • C. Anti-gliadin antibody
  • D. Anti-reticulin antibody
Q127

The histological features of coeliac disease include all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Crypt hyperplasia
  • B. Increase in thickness of the mucosa
  • C. Increase in intraepithelial lymphocytes
  • D. Increase in inflammatory cells in lamina propria
Q128

Biopsy findings of celiac disease, all of the following EXCEPT (select two best options):

  • B. Villous atrophy
  • C. Mucosal atrophy
  • D. Intraepithelial lymphocytes
  • E. Presence of Giardia lamblia
Q129

Proved association of celiac sprue is with:

  • A. Dermatitis herpetiformis
  • C. Pemphigus
  • D. Pemphigoid
Q130

(PGI Dec 2006) Celiac disease is associated with:

  • B. Type I DM
Q131

(PGI Dec 2002) Celiac sprue is diagnosed by (select two options):

  • A. Intestinal biopsy
  • B. Unequivocal response to gluten restriction
  • C. Finding of organism
  • D. Improvement on dapsone treatment
  • E. History of fat malabsorption
Q132

(PGI Dec 2001) Which of the following grains can be used safely in patients with celiac sprue?

  • A. Rice
  • B. Wheat
  • C. Rye
  • D. Barley
Q133

What can a patient with gluten hypersensitivity consume (select two best options)?

  • B. Barley
  • C. Oat
  • D. Corn
Q134

Which of the following vitamin deficiencies is uncommon in celiac disease?

  • A. Vitamin D
  • B. Folic acid
  • C. Vitamin A
Q135

(AIIMS May 2005) In which of the following conditions of malabsorption is an intestinal biopsy diagnostic?

  • A. Celiac disease
  • B. Tropical sprue
  • C. Whipple's disease
  • D. Lactose intolerance
Q136

  • B. Amyloidosis
  • C. Immunoproliferative small intestinal disease
  • D. Vibrio cholerae infection
Q137

Histopathological findings in Whipple's disease include all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A. Marked increase in macrophages in the mucosa
  • B. Marked increase in intraepithelial lymphocytes
  • C. Dilatation of lymphatics in the mucosa
  • D. Lipid deposition in the mucosa
Q138

Most common CNS manifestation of Whipple's disease is:

  • A. Cerebellar ataxia
  • B. Supranuclear ophthalmoplegia
  • C. Seizure
Q139

Which of the following conditions causing malabsorption has no effect on villus architecture and no inflammatory reaction?

  • A. Giardiasis
  • B. Crohn's disease
  • D. Abetalipoproteinemia
Q141

Which of the following parasitic infestations can lead to malabsorption syndrome?

  • A. Amoebiasis
  • B. Ascariasis
  • C. Hookworm infestation
  • D. Giardiasis
Q142

(AI 2004) The short bowel syndrome is characterized by all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A. Diarrhea
  • B. Hypogastrinemia
  • D. Steatorrhea
Q143

(AIIMS June 1997) All are complications of ileal resection, EXCEPT:

  • A. Megaloblastic anemia
  • C. Gastric hypersecretion
  • D. Malabsorption syndrome
Q144

(AI 2012) Deficiency of which of the following vitamins is most commonly seen in short bowel syndrome with ileal resection?

  • A. Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamine)
  • B. Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)
  • C. Folic acid
  • D. Vitamin K
Q145

Which of the following vitamin deficiencies is most commonly seen in short bowel syndrome?

  • A. Vitamin B12
  • B. Biotin
  • C. Vitamin B1
Q146

'Intestinal angina' is a symptom complex of:

  • A. Postprandial abdominal pain, weight loss, acute mesenteric vessel occlusion
  • B. Postprandial abdominal pain, weight loss, chronic mesenteric vessel occlusion
  • C. Pre-prandial abdominal pain, weight loss, chronic mesenteric vessel occlusion
  • D. Pre-prandial abdominal pain, weight gain, acute mesenteric vessel occlusion
Q147

A 20-year-old male presents with mucus and repeated gastrointestinal bleeding. Patient is positive for ANCA. The most likely diagnosis is:

  • A. Ulcerative colitis
  • C. Radiation colitis
  • D. Ischemic bowel disease
Q148

A 25-year-old male presents with a history of chronic diarrhea. Pathological examination reveals cryptitis and crypt abscesses. Likely diagnosis is:

  • A. Crohn's disease
  • B. Ulcerative colitis
  • C. Giardiasis
  • D. Microscopic colitis
Q149

Toxic megacolon is most commonly associated with:

  • D. Reiter's disease
Q150

Pseudopolyps are features of:

  • C. Celiac sprue
  • D. Whipple's disease
Q151

Pseudopolyps are typically seen in:

  • C. Celiac disease
  • D. Tropical sprue
Q152

Which of the following is MOST commonly affected by ulcerative colitis?

  • A. Cecum
  • B. Rectum
  • C. Sigmoid colon
  • D. Terminal ileum
Q153

Backwash ileitis is seen in:

  • C. Colonic carcinoma
  • D. Ileal polyp
Q154

(AIIMS May 1995) What is the drug of choice for ulcerative colitis?

  • A. Salazopyrine
  • B. Prednisolone
  • C. Mercaptopurine
  • D. 5-amino salicylic acid
Q155

All of the following drugs may be used in the treatment of ulcerative colitis EXCEPT:

  • A. Corticosteroids
  • B. Azathioprine
  • C. Sulfasalazine
Q156
