  • A. Methotrexate
  • C. Cyclosporine
  • D. Cyclophosphamide
Q157

(AIIMS Feb 1991) Drug effective in ulcerative colitis is:

  • A. Steroids
  • B. 5-amino salicylic acid (5-ASA)
  • C. Salazopyrin
  • D. Antibiotics
Q158

Skip granulomatous lesions are seen in:

Q159

Which of the following is the earliest change in intestine which occurs in Crohn's disease?

  • A. Aphthous ulcer
  • B. Longitudinal ulcer
  • C. Cobblestone
  • D. Transverse ulcer
Q160

A patient gives chronic history of diarrhea and blood in stool with multiple fistulae in the perineum and multiple strictures in small intestine. The diagnosis is:

  • B. Radiation enteritis
  • C. Ulcerative colitis
Q161

Which of the following is MOST commonly affected by Crohn's disease?

Q162

The presence of anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibody is a surrogate marker of:

Q163

Antibody shown to be useful for Crohn's disease:

  • B. Palivizumab
  • C. Natalizumab
Q164

(GI June 2005) Extraintestinal manifestations of inflammatory bowel disease include all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Uveitis
  • B. Sclerosing cholangitis
  • C. Osteoarthritis
  • D. Skin nodules
Q165

A 25-year-old woman presents with bloody diarrhea and is diagnosed as a case of ulcerative colitis. Which of the following conditions is NOT associated?

  • A. Sclerosing cholangitis
  • B. Iritis
  • C. Ankylosing spondylitis
  • D. Pancreatitis
Q166

Which one of the following is NOT a feature of irritable bowel syndrome?

  • A. Abdominal pain
  • B. Constipation
  • C. Rectal bleeding
  • D. Bloating
Q167

Which of the following features are associated with irritable bowel syndrome?

  • A. Weight loss
  • B. Anorexia
  • C. Abdominal distension
  • D. Blood in stool
Q168

A young girl presents with abdominal pain and a recent change in bowel habit, with passage of mucus in stool. No associated blood in stool. Symptoms increased with stress. The most likely diagnosis is:

  • A. Irritable bowel syndrome
  • C. Crohn's disease
  • D. Amebiasis
Q169

All of the following statements about irritable bowel syndrome are true, EXCEPT:

  • A. Associated with anxiety
  • B. Stress predisposes
  • C. Constipation
  • D. Diarrhea
  • E. Predisposes to malignancy in long term
Q170

Which of the following drugs is used for irritable bowel syndrome of the constipating type?

  • A. Lubiprostone
  • B. Cholestyramine
  • C. Alosetron
  • D. Rifaximin
Q171

Which of the following organisms does NOT cause invasive diarrhea?

  • A. Bacillus cereus
  • B. Aeromonas sp
  • C. Rota virus
  • D. Shigella
Q172

Drugs and toxins that increase the cAMP content of the intestinal mucosa cause diarrhea because they:

  • A. Increase K⁺ secretion in the colon
  • B. Increase Cl⁻ secretion into the intestine
  • C. Increase Na⁺ absorption in the small intestine
  • D. Increase Na⁺/K⁺ cotransport in the small intestine
Q173

(PGI June 2007) Secretory diarrhea is caused by all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Cholera
  • B. Laxatives
  • C. Excess phenolphthalein intake
  • D. Clostridium difficile
Q174

Secretory diarrhea is NOT seen in:

  • A. Phenolphthalein
  • B. Celiac disease
  • C. Cholera
  • D. Addison's disease
Q175

All of the following are true about osmotic diarrhea, EXCEPT:

  • A. Stool volume < 1 L/day
  • B. Diarrhea stops after fasting
  • C. Osmolar gap < 50 mOsm/kg
  • D. All of the following
Q176

Large bowel colonic diarrhoea is associated with all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Large volume stool
  • B. Urgency
  • C. Tenesmus
  • D. Mucus
Q177

Diarrhea with acanthocytosis is seen in:

  • B. Celiac sprue
  • D. Ulcerative colitis
Q179

Features of secretory diarrhea include all of the following, EXCEPT:

  • A. Stool volume > 1 L/day
  • B. Normal osmotic anion gap
  • C. Reduces with fasting
  • D. Painless
Q180

Indications for use of antibiotics in acute diarrhea include (select three options):

  • A. Febrile dysentery with fever ≥ 38.5°C
  • B. Immunocompromised patient
  • C. Elderly patient
  • D. Dehydration
Q181

A patient presents with severe colitis associated with overgrowth of Clostridium difficile. The most likely cause is:

  • A. Stomach ulcer
  • B. Food poisoning
  • D. Immunosuppression
Q182

Pseudomembranous colitis is caused by:

  • A. Clostridium welchii
  • B. Clostridium difficile
  • C. Yersinia enterocolitica
Q183

The first antibiotic linked to antibiotic-associated pseudomembranous colitis is:

  • A. Clindamycin
  • B. Cephalosporins
  • C. Vancomycin
Q184

Which of the following best describes the clinical criteria for C. difficile associated diarrhea with no other recognized cause?

  • A. ≥ 3 unformed stools per 12h for ≥ 2 days
  • B. ≥ 3 unformed stools per 24h for ≥ 2 days
  • C. ≥ 2 unformed stools per 24h for ≥ 3 days
  • D. ≥ 2 unformed stools per 24h for ≥ 3 days
Q185

What is the MOST common cause of Traveler's diarrhea?

  • A. Enteroinvasive E. coli
  • B. Enterotoxigenic E. coli
  • C. Enteropathogenic E. coli
  • D. Enterohemorrhagic E. coli
Q186

A 14-year-old girl with history of prolonged fever and abdominal discomfort has splenomegaly and leukopenia. She develops an acute abdominal event and dies. Likely finding on autopsy:

  • A. Transverse ulcers
  • B. Longitudinal ulcers
  • C. Pinpoint ulcers
  • D. Pseudopolyps
Q187

A patient presents with lower GI bleed. Sigmoidoscopy shows ulcers in the sigmoid. Biopsy shows flask-shaped ulcers. Most appropriate treatment:

  • A. Intravenous ceftriaxone
  • B. Intravenous metronidazole
  • C. Intravenous steroids and sulphasalazine
  • D. Hydrocortisone enemas
Q188

Investigation of choice for invasive amebiasis is:

  • A. Indirect hemagglutination
  • B. ELISA
  • C. Counter immune electrophoresis
  • D. Microscopy
Q189

Features of typhoid ulcers include all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A. Bleeding
  • B. Perforation
  • C. Stricture and obstruction
  • D. Longitudinal orientation
Q190

A patient with leukemia on chemotherapy develops acute right lower abdominal pain associated with anemia, thrombocytopenia and leukopenia. Clinical diagnosis:

  • A. Appendicitis
  • B. Leukemic colitis
  • C. Perforation peritonitis
  • D. Neutropenic colitis
Q191

The most pre-cancerous condition for carcinoma colon is:

  • A. Familial polyposis
  • B. Hamartomatous polyps
  • C. Juvenile polyps
  • D. Hyperplastic polyps
Q192

Strong correlation with colorectal cancer is seen in:

  • A. Peutz-Jeghers polyp
  • B. Familial polyposis coli
  • C. Juvenile polyposis
  • D. Hyperplastic polyp
Q194

Which of the following colonic polyps is NOT pre-malignant?

  • A. Juvenile polyps
  • B. Hamartomatous polyps associated with Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
  • C. Villous adenomas
  • D. Tubular adenomas
Q195

(AI 2005) Which one of the following conditions commonly predisposes to colonic carcinoma?

  • C. Diverticular disease
  • D. Ischaemic colitis
Q196

A 25-year-old male had pigmented macules over the palm, sole and oral mucosa. He also had anemia and abdominal pain. The most probable diagnosis is:

  • A. Albright's syndrome
  • B. Cushing's syndrome
  • C. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
  • D. Incontinentia pigmenti
Q197

A patient presents with melena, hyperpigmentation over lips, oral mucosa and skin. His sister also has similar complaints. The diagnosis is:

  • A. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
  • B. Familial adenomatous polyposis
  • C. Gardner's syndrome
  • D. Villous adenoma
Q198

In Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, polyps are seen in:

  • A. Colon
  • C. Small bowel
  • D. Stomach
Q199

(AIIMS Nov 2000) A girl presents with melena. Pigmented lesions involving mouth and lips. Two sisters also had similar complaints. Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

  • A. Cronkhite-Canada syndrome
  • B. Peutz-Jeghers syndrome
  • D. Turcot's syndrome
Q200

A 25-year-old man presents with rectal bleeding. He has several epidermoid cysts and palpable bone osteomas of the mandible. Which syndrome BEST describes the findings?

  • A. Cowden's syndrome
  • B. Gardner's syndrome
  • C. Muir-Torre syndrome
Q201

Most important prognostic factor for colorectal carcinoma is:

  • A. Site of lesion
  • B. Stage of lesion
  • C. Age of patient
  • D. Lymph node status
Q202

After surgery for carcinoma of colon, a patient developed a single liver metastasis of 2 cm. What do you do next?

  • A. Resection
  • B. Chemoradiation
  • C. Acetic acid injection
  • D. Radiofrequency ablation
Q203

A patient was operated for colonic carcinoma and later a solitary nodule was found in the liver. Treatment of choice is:

  • A. Surgery
  • B. Radiation
  • C. Chemotherapy
  • D. Conservative treatment

  • A. Abdominoperineal resection
  • B. Combined surgery and radiotherapy
  • C. Combined chemotherapy and radiotherapy
  • D. Chemotherapy alone
Q205

All of the following genetic syndromes associated with colorectal cancer are caused by mutations in the APC gene, EXCEPT:

  • A. Lynch syndrome
  • B. FAP

Lynch syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder with an increased risk of colorectal cancer that is specifically caused by defects in:

  • A. RB gene
  • B. P53 gene
  • C. WT1 gene
  • D. Mismatch repair genes
Q207

Based on epidemiological studies, which of the following has been found to be most protective against carcinoma colon?

  • A. High fiber diet
  • B. Low fat diet
  • C. Low selenium diet
  • D. Low protein diet

Which of the following is the most common cause of acute pancreatitis?

  • A. Gall stones
  • B. Alcohol
  • C. Hypertriglyceridemia
  • D. Drugs

Which of the following is the LEAST common cause of acute pancreatitis?

What percentage of acute pancreatitis is drug related?

  • A. 0.1 to 2%
  • B. 2 to 5%
  • C. 6 to 12%
  • D. 15 to 20%
Q211

All the following are causes of acute pancreatitis EXCEPT:

  • C. Hemochromatosis
Q212

Which of the following is NOT a cause of acute pancreatitis?

  • A. Hypercalcemia
  • B. Pancreas divisum
  • C. Renal failure
Q213

Most common cause of chronic pancreatitis amongst adults is:

  • B. Cystic fibrosis
  • C. Gall stone disease
Q214

Most common cause of chronic pancreatitis amongst children is:

Q215

What level of hypertriglyceridemia is typically associated with a risk of acute pancreatitis?

  • A. > 250 mg/dl
  • B. > 500 mg/dl
  • C. > 750 mg/dl
  • D. > 1000 mg/dl
Q216

Increased amylase may be seen in all of the following:

  • A. Pancreatic pseudocyst
  • B. Appendicitis
  • C. Perforated peptic ulcer
  • D. Ruptured ectopic pregnancy
Q217

  • A. Autoimmune disease
  • B. Degenerative diseases
  • C. Acute cholecystitis
  • D. Acute pancreatitis

Ranson's criteria for prognosis in acute pancreatitis include all EXCEPT:

  • B. Age over 55 years
  • C. AST > 250 U/100 ml
  • D. Serum amylase > 350 I.U.
Q219

(AIIMS Nov 2006) Which of the following is NOT a prognostic factor for acute pancreatitis?

  • A. Serum amylase
  • B. Serum calcium
  • C. Serum glucose
  • D. Serum AST
Q220

  • A. TLC > 16,000
  • B. Calcium less than 8 mmol/L
  • C. Glucose > 200 mg%
  • D. Prothrombin > 2 times the control

  • C. Raised LDH level in blood
  • D. Hyperamylasemia
Q222

  • A. Low serum lipase
  • B. Increased LDH
  • D. Increased serum amylase
Q223

(AI 1993) Definitive laboratory diagnosis of acute pancreatitis is done by:

  • B. Serum lipase
  • C. Hyperglycemia
Q224

The most specific laboratory test to establish a diagnosis of acute pancreatitis is:

  • C. Serum alkaline phosphatase
  • D. Serum calcium
  • E. Serum glucose
Q225

Most specific test for diagnosis of acute pancreatitis is:

  • C. Alkaline phosphatase
  • D. Gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase
Q226

  • A. Purtscher's retinopathy
  • B. Sudden alcohol withdrawal
  • C. Acute congestive glaucoma
  • D. CRAO

  • C. Intestinal ischemia
  • D. DIC

  • C. Retroperitoneal quinsy
  • D. Retroperitoneal burn

  • B. Transverse colon
  • C. Descending colon
  • D. Sigmoid colon

  • C. Ileum

Which of the following types of pancreatitis has the best prognosis?

  • A. Alcoholic pancreatitis
  • B. Gall stone pancreatitis
  • C. Post-operative pancreatitis

Most common complication of acute pancreatitis is:

  • A. Pancreatic abscess

  • B. Pancreatitis

  • D. Presents as an epigastric mass

  • A. VIP
  • B. Insulin

  • B. Recurrence after operation

  • A. Insulin
  • B. VIP

  • A. Calcium infusion test
  • B. Secretin injection test
  • C. ACTH stimulation test
  • D. Steroid assay

Q245

Drug of choice in Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is:

  • A. Ranitidine
  • B. Omeprazole
  • C. Antacids
  • D. Beta-blocker

(AI 2007) Treatment of choice for Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (ZES):

  • A. PPI
  • B. Somatostatin analogues
  • C. Streptozocin
  • D. Sucralfate

Gold standard test for diagnosis of insulinoma is:

  • A. 72-hour fast test
  • B. Plasma glucose levels < 3 mmol/l
  • C. Plasma insulin levels > 6 uU/ml
  • D. C-peptide levels < 50 pmol/l
Q248

Gold standard test for insulinoma is:

  • A. Fast up to 72 h with serum glucose, C-peptide, proinsulin, and insulin measurements every 4-8 h
  • B. Fast up to 48 h with serum glucose, C-peptide, proinsulin, and insulin measurements every 4-8 h
  • C. Fast up to 72 h with serum glucose, C-peptide, proinsulin, and insulin measurements every 8-12 h
  • D. Fast up to 48 h with serum glucose, C-peptide, proinsulin, and insulin measurements every 8-12 h

(AI/AIIMS) All of the following statements about insulinoma are true EXCEPT:

  • A. Most commonly found in pancreas
  • B. Usually benign
  • C. Usually multiple
  • D. Derived from beta cells

(AIIMS) Which of the following statements about insulinoma is true?

  • A. Present in pancreas
  • B. Mostly malignant
  • C. Surgery is usually not required
  • D. Usually multiple
Q251

Weight loss may be seen in all of the following EXCEPT:

  • A. Uremia
  • B. Pheochromacytoma
  • C. Adrenal insufficiency
  • D. Insulinoma
Q252

Necrolytic migratory erythema is typically seen in:

  • A. Glucagonoma
  • B. Insulinoma
  • C. VIPoma
  • D. Zollinger-Ellison syndrome
Q253

The most classical symptom of VIPoma is:

  • B. Secretory diarrhea
  • D. Flushing
Q254

Pancreatic cholera is characterized by all EXCEPT:

  • A. Hypochlorhydria
  • C. Glucose intolerance
Q255

A 7-year-old child with recurrent chest infections and exocrine pancreatic insufficiency is suspected of having cystic fibrosis. Sweat chloride levels have been borderline on two occasions. Which of the following tests should be performed next?

  • A. Repeat sweat chloride levels on a different day
  • B. Demonstrate an abnormal nasal potential difference
  • C. Demonstrate an abnormal F508 mutation by DNA analysis
  • D. Demonstrate an abnormal 72-hour fecal fat collection
Q256

Which of the following statements about cystic fibrosis (CF) is NOT true?

  • A. Autosomal recessive disorder
  • B. Abnormality in CFTR which leads to defective calcium transport
  • C. Predisposition to pulmonary infection with Pseudomonas
  • D. Cirrhosis is an established complication of CF
Q257

All of the following statements about fibrocystic disease of pancreas are true, EXCEPT:

  • A. Sweat chloride levels are increased
  • B. Serum chloride levels are usually normal
  • C. Immunoreactive trypsinogen is used for screening

Hepatology

240 Questions

▼
Q1

  • A. Foul smelling stools
  • B. Large stools
  • C. Steatorrhea
  • D. Clay colored stools
Q2

Hyperbilirubinemia is seen in: *(AIIMS May 2006)

  • A. Crigler Najjar syndrome
  • B. Dubin Johnson syndrome
  • C. Rotor syndrome
  • D. Gilbert's syndrome
Q3

Dubin Johnson occurs because it is: *(AIIMS Nov 99)

  • A. Defective excretion of conjugated bilirubin
  • B. Defective excretion of unconjugated bilirubin
  • C. Defective uptake of bilirubin
  • D. Defective conjugation of bilirubin
Q4

Jaundice is seen with elevated bilirubin levels. Indication of some shows presence of bilirubin in urine: *(AI 1991)

  • A. Hemolytic jaundice
  • B. Hepatocellular jaundice
  • C. Obstructive jaundice
  • D. Gilbert syndrome
Q5

Most patients with elevated bilirubin levels. Urine bilirubin and urine Urobilinogen is present: *(NBE)

Q6

Gilbert's syndrome is: *(NBE)

  • A. Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia
  • B. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
Q7

A patient presents with icterus, but there is no evidence of bilirubin in urine. What is the most likely cause of jaundice in this patient? *(NBE)

  • A. Hepatitis
  • B. Cirrhosis
  • C. Carcinoma head of pancreas
  • D. Sclerosing cholangitis
Q8

A patient presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Presence of urobilinogen in urine. Which amongst the following is the least likely cause: *(AI 2010)

  • B. Dubin-Johnson syndrome
  • C. Gilbert's syndrome
  • D. Rotor syndrome
Q9

A patient presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia and elevated urobilinogen levels. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AI 2010)

  • A. Hemolytic Jaundice
  • B. Crigler-Najjar syndrome
  • D. Dubin-Johnson syndrome
Q10

A 50-year-old man presents with jaundice, pruritus, and a palpable gallbladder. The most likely diagnosis is: *(NBE)

  • A. Carcinoma head of pancreas
  • B. Chronic pancreatitis
  • C. Cholecystitis
  • D. Choledocholithiasis
Q11

A 35-year-old man presents with jaundice. Blood examination shows total bilirubin 10 mg/dl, direct bilirubin 2 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 180 KA units. Diagnosis is: *(AI 2001)

  • B. Obstructive jaundice
  • C. Hepatocellular jaundice
  • D. Physiological jaundice
Q12

A 25-year-old man presents with jaundice. Total bilirubin is 10 mg/dl, alkaline Ph 8.4 units. Diagnosis is: *(AI 2001)

  • C. Chronic active hepatitis
  • D. Dubin Johnson syndrome

True statement about unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia: *(NBE)

  • A. It is always pathological
  • B. All of the total should be indirect
  • C. Seen in hepatocellular jaundice
  • D. Seen in biliary obstruction if bilirubin production

  • B. Does not respond to vitamin K
  • C. Responds to fresh frozen plasma
  • D. Responds to platelet transfusion

  • A. Increased PT
  • B. Increased ALT

  • D. Increased gamma GT

Increased Br. level is seen in all, except: *(AI 2005)

  • A. Acute Hepatitis A
  • B. Acute Hepatitis B
  • C. Chronic Hepatitis B

  • B. Acute cholangitis

  • A. Acute hepatitis B
  • B. Chronic hepatitis B
  • C. Vaccinated individual
  • D. Occult hepatitis B
Q38

A 25-year-old male is found to be positive for HBsAg, HBeAg, and anti-HBc. The biochemical profile shows elevated ALT and AST. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AIIMS May 06)

  • A. Acute Hepatitis B
  • B. Chronic Hepatitis B
  • C. Occult Hepatitis B
  • D. Vaccinated individual
Q39

After one year post-exposure to hepatitis B virus, a patient is found to have anti-HBs positive, anti- HBc positive, and HBsAg negative. This indicates: *(AIIMS May 06)

  • C. Resolved Hepatitis B infection
Q40

Chronic hepatitis B infection with low infectivity is characterized by: *(AIIMS May 06)

  • A. HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive
  • B. HBsAg positive, anti-HBe positive
  • C. HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive
  • D. HBsAg positive, anti-HBc positive
Q41

A 30-year-old male with HBsAg positive for more than 6 months. ALT is elevated. HBV DNA is 100,000 copies/ml. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AIIMS May 06)

  • D. Resolved Hepatitis B
Q42

Active infection of hepatitis B virus is diagnosed by: *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • A. HBsAg positive
  • B. Anti-HBs positive
  • C. Anti-HBc positive
  • D. HBeAg positive
Q43

Acute Hepatitis B infection can be best differentiated from chronic Hepatitis B by: *(NBE)

Hepatitis B can be transmitted through all of the following except: *(NBE)

  • A. Blood
  • B. Semen
  • C. Breast milk
  • D. Fecal-oral route
Q45

The risk of vertical transmission of hepatitis B may be as high as: *(NBE)

  • A. 10%
  • B. 30%
  • C. 50%
  • D. 90%
Q46

The best virological marker following acute infection is: *(AI 1999)

  • A. Anti-HBs Ag
  • B. HBsAg
Q47

The presence of anti-HBeAg in hepatitis B infection is indicative of: *(AIIMS Dec 04)

  • A. Active viral replication
  • B. Recovery from infection
  • C. Chronic infection
  • D. High infectivity
Q48

The presence of HBeAg in serum indicates: *(AIIMS June 98)

  • C. Low infectivity
  • D. Vaccination status
Q49

Which of the following is a marker for hepatitis B virus replication: *(NBE)

  • B. HBeAg
  • C. Anti-HBs
  • D. Anti-HBc
Q50

The following are true except: *(NBE)

  • A. HBsAg indicates current infection
  • B. Anti-HBs indicates immunity
  • C. Anti-HBc IgM indicates acute infection
  • D. HBeAg indicates low infectivity
Q51

A patient is found to be positive for HBsAg. All other hepatitis B markers are negative. The patient is asymptomatic. What is the most likely diagnosis: *(AI 2010)

Q52

The following are markers of acute hepatitis B infection: *(AIIMS Nov 07)

Q53

HBsAg is a marker of: *(AIIMS Nov 07)

  • A. Acute infection
  • B. Chronic infection
  • C. Immunity
  • D. Vaccination
Q54

HBsAg is positive in: *(NBE)

  • A. Acute hepatitis
  • B. Chronic hepatitis
  • C. Carriers
Q55

Most common cause of acute liver failure is: *(NBE)

Q56

Anti-HBc IgM is seen in: *(NBE)

  • D. Vaccinated individuals
Q58

A patient is found to be positive only for Anti-HBc. This indicates: *(AIIMS Dec 04)

  • A. Window period
  • B. Occult hepatitis B
  • C. Resolved infection
Q59

The marker for determining efficacy of hepatitis B vaccination is: *(NBE)

Q60

Which of the following serum markers is indicative of HBV vaccination: *(AIIMS May 06)

Q61

The only serological marker present in the "window period" of hepatitis B infection is: *(NBE)

  • A. Anti-HBs
Q62

All of the following should be included during prophylaxis of a case of occupational exposure to HBV

  • B. HBV vaccine
  • C. Anti-HCV
  • D. Anti-HDV
Q63

A 30-year-old patient presented with history of fever, jaundice, and malaise. HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive, HBV DNA 100,000 copies/ml. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AIIMS May 06)

Q64

Hepatitis B infectivity is indicated by: *(AI 1994)

  • D. Anti-HBe
Q65

Anti-HBcAg is indicated by all of the following, except: *(AIIMS May 06)

  • A. HBV DNA
  • C. HBeAg
Q66

Hepatitis B virus is: *(NBE)

  • C. Retrovirus
Q67

The following are true about hepatitis B virus except: *(NBE)

  • A. Enveloped virus
  • B. Double stranded DNA
  • C. Hepadnavirus family
  • D. Single stranded RNA
Q68

Most common extrahepatic manifestation of hepatitis B is: *(NBE)

  • A. Polyarteritis nodosa
  • C. Cryoglobulinemia
  • D. Thyroiditis
Q69

The incubation period of hepatitis B is: *(NBE)

  • A. 2-6 weeks
  • B. 6 weeks to 6 months
  • C. 2-6 months
  • D. 6-12 months
Q70

Chronic hepatitis B is defined as persistence of HBsAg for more than: *(NBE)

  • A. 3 months
  • B. 6 months
  • C. 12 months
  • D. 24 months
Q71

The most common mode of transmission of hepatitis B in India is: *(NBE)

Q72

Hepatitis B vaccine is: *(NBE)

  • A. Live attenuated vaccine
  • B. Killed vaccine
  • C. Recombinant vaccine
  • D. Toxoid
Q73

The following are contraindications to hepatitis B vaccination except: *(NBE)

  • A. Pregnancy
  • B. Immunodeficiency
  • C. Previous allergic reaction
Q74

Post-exposure prophylaxis for hepatitis B includes: *(NBE)

  • A. HBV vaccine
  • B. HBIG
  • C. Both A and B

  • A. Interferon
  • B. Lamivudine

  • D. Ribavirin

  • A. Polymerase gene
  • B. Surface gene
  • C. Core gene
  • D. X gene

  • C. Genotype A

Hepatitis D virus is: *(NBE)

  • D. Defective RNA virus
Q79

  • A. Requires HBV for replication

Hepatic C is associated with all except: *(PGI June 08) PAN Dermatomyositis like syndrome Psoriasis

The most appropriate approach: *(PGI June 04)* Reassure the patient Repeat titre every three years Repeat enzymes every year Do liver biopsy & start antiviral drugs accordingly.

Q89

A 30 year old patient with H/O persistent antibodies to HCV for 6 months duration and his AST/ALT is normal. There is no symptom or stigmata of liver disease. The most appropriate approach: *(PGI June 04)* Determine HCV RNA levels

Which of the following would be the next most appropriate choice? *(AIIMS Nov 03)* Ribavirin and interferon Lamivudine and interferon Nevirapine and ribavirin Indinavir and ribavirin.

Q91

A 55 year old male patient was diagnosed to have chronic hepatitis C. He responded to treatment

Q92

Sustained Response to antiviral therapy (IFN + Ribavirin) in hepatitis C is indicated by *(PGI Dec 04) High HCV-RNA Cirrhosis Age > 40 years Genotype I Female sex

Q93

True regarding hepatitis E virus is: *(AIIMS Dec 98) Seen in Post transfusion cases Associated with increased mortality in pregnant female Associated with hepatitis B virus Acquired by feco-oral route

Q94

The most common route of spread in hepatitis E is: *(AI 1995) Blood transfusion Feco-oral Intercourse IV injection

Q95

During an epidemic of hepatitis E, Fatality is maximum in: *(AI 2000) Pregnant women Infants Malnourished male Adolescents

Q96

Fulminant Hepatitis E is typically seen in: *(NBE) Malnourished males

Q97

Which hepatitis is most dangerous in pregnancy: *(AIIMS May 95)

Q98

Which hepatitis virus has worst prognosis in pregnancy: *(AIIMS Feb 97) HBV HCV HEV HAV

Q99

Most common type of hepatitis responsible for epidemics in India is: *(AI 1997)

Q100

Most common cause of acute sporadic hepatitis in India is *(NBE) Hepatitis D

Q101

Characteristic Auto antibodies of Autoimmune Hepatitis include all of the following, Except: *(PGI Dec 06)* Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) Anti SLA Anti LKM1 ANCA

Q102

Type 2 autoimmune hepatitis is MOST STRONGLY associated with which antibodies *(NBE) ANA Anti-actin antibodies Anti-LKM1 Atypical P-ANCA

Q103

Type 3 autoimmune hepatitis is most strongly associated with which antibodies *(NBE) Anti-SLA All of the above

Q104

Mainstay of management in autoimmune hepatitis is *(NBE) Azathiopurine Glucocorticoid Cyclosporine Mycophenolate mofetil

Q105

Therapy for autoimmune hepatitis should continue for *(AI 1996) 3-6 months 6-12 months 12-18 months 18-24 months

Q106

Granulomatous hepatitis may be seen with: *(AI 1996) Carbamazepine Allopurinol Phenylbutazone

Q107

Alcohol consumption associated with an increased rate of alcoholic liver disease in Men *(NBE) >10 -25 gm/day >20 -35 gm/day >30 - 45 gm/day None of the above

Q108

Features of Alcoholic hepatitis include all of the following except: *(AI 1991) Elevated bilirubin Prolonged prothrombin time Elevated serum albumin Anemia

Q109

Ratio of AST/ALT > 1 is present in *(AIIMS May 07) Non alcoholic steatohepatitis Alcoholic hepatitis Wilson's disease

Q110

Which of the following is the most reliable marker of alcoholic hepatitis? *(AIIMS Nov 18) Raised GGT Raised LDH AST/ALT Ratio < 1 AST/ALT RATIO > 2

Q111

Which is not true about alcoholic hepatitis : *(AIIMS May 95) Gamma glutamyl transferase is raised SGOT is raised > SGOT SGOT is raised > SGPT Alkaline phosphatase is raised

Q112

Which of the following enzyme assay is useful to diagnose alcoholism *(AIIMS Dec 94) SGOT SGPT Alkaline phosphatase Glutamyl aminotransferase

Q113

Discriminant factor (DF) formula predicting the outcome of severe aloholic hepatitis is named after Nathan Cushin Maddrey George

Q114

  • B. Johnson cirrhosis
  • C. Gilbert cirrhosis
  • D. Laennecs cirrhosis

Zieve syndrome is characterized by all except: *(NBE) Alcohol abuse Hemolysis Hyperlipidemia Pancreatic lipase deficiency

Which of the following is advocated as the mainstay treatment for severe alcoholic hepatitis *(NBE) Glucocorticoids Thiamine Proton pump inhibitors Monoclonal Antibodies that neutralize TNF ALPHA

Q119

Which is useful to decrease mortality and renal failure in acute liver disease due to alcoholism: *(AIIMS May 07)* Pentoxifylline Orlistat S-Adenosyl methionine Syriamysin

Q120

Non Alcoholic steatohepatitis is seen in all, except: *(PGI June 05) Diabetes Obesity Hypertriglyceridemia Total Parenteral Nutrition Gall stone disease

Q121

All of the following statements about Non Alcoholic Fatty Liver disease are true, except: *(PGI June 01)* Common in Diabetics Clofibrate provides effective treatment Commonest cause of cryptogenic cirrhosis Associated with elevated transaminases

Q122

SAAG stands for *(NBE) Serum ascites to albumin gradient Serum albumin to ascites gradient Serum anion to anion gradient Serum anion to albumin gradient

Q123

SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL with an ascitic protein level of >2.5 g/dL is seen in *(NBE) Tuberculosis Nephrotic Syndrome Cardiac Ascitis

Q124

Ascitic fluid SAAG < 1.1 what is the disease associated with *(NBE) Hepatic failure Idiopathic portal fibrosis Constrictive pericarditis Peritoneal carcinomatosis

Q125

Chylous ascities is caused by all of the following except: *(AIIMS Nov 02) Colloid carcinoma of stomach. Tuberculosis. Trauma. Nephrotic syndrome.

Q126

Endemic ascites is caused due to: *(NBE) Field fungae Sanguinarine Fusarium toxins Pyrrolizidine alkaloids

Q127

Endemic ascites is associated with: *(NBE) Pyrrolizidine Aflatoxin BOAA Sanguinine

Q128

Diagnosis of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis based on ascitic fluid PMN count *(NBE) >100/ mm³ >150/ mm³ >200/ mm³ >250/ mm³

Q129

Q130

First line of treatment in Ascitis is: *(PGI June 96) Salt Restriction Diuretics Paracentesis Shunt

Q131

Recommended amount of sodium per day in the management of cirrhotic ascites is *(NBE) <2 gram <4 gram <6 gram <8 gram

Q132

Hepatic hydrothorax is more common on which side *(NBE) Right side Left side Bilateral May occur on any side

Q133

Micronodular cirrhosis is commonly seen in all except: *(AIIMS Nov 07) Chronic hepatitis B Alcoholic liver disease Hemochromatosis Chronic extrahepatic biliary obstruction

Q134

In hepatic cirrhosis, which of the following is increased: *(PGI June 97) Alpha 1 globulin Alpha 2 globulin Gamma globulin

Which of the following is associated with chronic liver disease? *(NBE) Chronic Malaria Infectious Mononucleosis Alpha-1 Antitrypsin deficiency

Q146

All of the following statements about primary sclerosing cholangitis are true, Except: *(PGI Dec 03) Increased incidence in females Associated with Inflammatory bowel disease May involve both intra and extra hepatic ducts ERCP is a sensitive investigation

Q147

Portal hypertension is defined as elevation of hepatic venous pressure gradient to *(NBE) >5 mm Hg >10 mm Hg >15 mm Hg >20 mm Hg

Sengstaken - Blakemore tubes: *(NBE) Have three lumens - gastric balloon, esophageal balloon and esophageal aspiration port Control around 90% of cases of variceal bleeding When correctly sited, should be attached to 5 kg of traction Usually have the esophageal balloon inflated before the gastric balloon

  • B. Barbiturates

  • E. Coagulopathy

  • A. Increasing transaminase
  • B. Increasing bilirubin
  • C. Increasing prothrombin time
  • D. Gram negative sepsis

  • A. Serum albumin
  • B. Serum alpha feto protein
  • C. Serum bilirubin
  • D. Factor V estimation

  • A. Prothrombin time (PT)
  • B. APTT
  • C. Decreased Albumin
  • D. Increased Arterial Ammonia

  • A. Hepatic encephalopathy
  • B. Uremic encephalopathy
  • C. Hypoxic encephalopathy
  • D. Hyperbaric encephalopathy

Flapping Tremors may be associated with all of the following, Except *(NBE)

  • B. Uremia
  • C. CO₂ Narcosis
  • D. Thyrotoxicosis

Alzheimer type II astrocyte are not seen in: *(NBE)

  • B. Alzheimer's disease
  • C. Wilson's Disease
  • D. Urea Cycle Disorders

Following liver transplantation, recurrence of primary disease in the liver most likely occurs in: *(AIIMS Nov. 05)

  • A. Wilson disease
  • B. Autoimmune hepatitis
  • C. Alpha - 1 antitrypsin deficiency
  • D. Primary biliary cirrhosis

  • B. Normal renal histology
  • C. Renal function abnormal even after liver become normal
  • D. Proteinuria < 500 mg/day
Q186

  • A. Creatinine clearance < 40 ml/min
  • B. Urinary sodium < 10mq/L
  • C. Urine osmolality lower than plasma osmolality
  • D. No sustained improvement in renal function after volume expansion.
Q187

  • A. The creatinine clearance is > 40 ml/min
  • B. The urinary sodium is less than 10 mmol/L
  • C. The urine osmolality is lower than the plasma osmolality
  • D. There is poor response to volume expansion

  • A. Hepatitis C infection
  • B. Alcoholism
  • C. Aflatoxins
  • D. Animal fat in diet

  • A. Focal nodular hyper-plasia
  • C. Hepatic adenoma
  • D. Metastasis

  • A. Jaundice
  • B. Abdominal pain
  • C. Abdominal mass
  • D. Ascitis

  • A. Hepatocellular carcinoma
  • B. Liver cell hyperplasia
  • D. Hepatitis

Increase in alpha-fetoprotein is seen in: *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. Hepatoblastoma
  • B. Neuroblastoma
  • C. Thymoma
  • D. Angiosarcoma

Which of the following statements is not true regarding α-fetoprotein: *(AI 1999)

  • A. High levels are seen in fibrolamellar hepatic carcinoma
  • B. Pre-operative high level indicates worse prognosis
  • C. High level are seen in stomach carcinoma
  • D. Levels may be increased in hepatitis

  • A. Not common in Asian
  • B. Liver biopsy is diagnostic
  • C. Raised titre of HBV and HCV antibodies
  • D. Fibrolameller type is having good prognosis

About fibrolamellar carcinoma, TRUE is: *(AIIMS May 94)

  • A. Diffuse in nature
  • B. Occurs after 60 years of age
  • C. Cirrhosis is the most common presenting feature
  • D. Has better prognosis
Q196

  • A. More common in females
  • B. Better prognosis than HCC
  • C. AFP levels always greater than 1000
  • D. Occur in younger individuals

Budd-chiari syndrome is most commonly due to: *(AI 1999)

  • A. Hepatic vein obstruction
  • B. Acute portal hypertension
  • C. Congenital portal hypertension
  • D. IVC- obstruction
Q198

What is the most common cause for Budd chiari syndrome: *(AIIMS Nov 2000)

  • A. Right ventricular failure
  • B. PNH
  • C. Valve in hepatic veins
  • D. Polycythemia vera
Q199

Commonest cause of Budd Chiary syndrome is: *(AI 1997)

  • A. Valve in the IVC
  • C. Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria
  • D. Renal cell carcinoma
Q200

Most common cause of Budd Chiari syndrome is: *(AIIMS May 97)

  • A. Idiopathic
  • B. Valves in hepatic veins
  • C. Hepatocellular carcinoma
  • D. Thrombosis of hepatic veins
Q201

In Budd Chiari syndrome, the site of venous thrombosis is: *(AI 2004)

  • A. Intrahepatic inferior vena cava
  • B. Infrarenal inferior vena cava
  • C. Hepatic veins
  • D. Portal veins
Q202

Acute Budd-Chiari syndrome: *(NBE)

  • A. Enlarged tender liver
  • B. Ascites
  • C. Jaundice
  • D. Venous collaterals
Q203

5'-Nucleotidase activity is increased in: *(AI 2005)

  • A. Bone diseases
  • B. Prostate cancer.
  • C. Chronic renal failure.
  • D. Cholestatic disorders.
Q204

Lithogenic bile has the following properties: *(AI 1996)

  • A. ↓ Bile and ↑ Cholesterol ratio
  • B. ↑ Bile and ↓ cholesterol ratio
  • C. Equal bile and cholesterol
  • D. Cholesterol only
Q205

Gall bladder stone formation in influenced by all except: *(AI 1998)

  • A. Clofibrate therapy
  • B. Hyperalimentation
  • C. Primary biliary cirrhosis
  • D. Hypercholesterolemia
Q206

Stone formation in Gall bladder is enhanced by all except: *(AI 1996)

  • B. Ilial resection
  • C. Cholestyramine therapy
  • D. Vagal stimulation
Q207

Incidence of gall stone is high in: *(AIIMS Nov 93)

  • A. Partial hepatectomy
  • B. Ileal resection
  • C. Jejunal resection
  • D. Subtotal gastrectomy
Q208

True statement about gall stones are All/Except: *(AIIMS Nov 99)

  • A. Lithogenic bile is required for stone formation
  • B. May be associated with carcinoma gall bladder
  • C. Associated with diabetes mellitus
  • D. More common in males between 30-40 years of age
Q209

Most common site for impaction of gall stones in gall stone ileus is: *(AI 1999)

  • A. 1st part of duodenum
  • B. IInd part of duodenum
  • C. Terminal ileum
  • D. Colon
Q210

The most common site of intestinal obstruction in gallstone ileus is: *(AIIMS May 05, AI 04)

  • A. Duodenum
  • B. Jejunum
  • C. Ileum
  • D. Sigmoid colon
Q211

Internal fistula is most common between gall bladder and: *(AIIMS June 93)

  • A. Colon
  • B. Duodenum Ist part
  • C. Jejunum
  • D. Transverse colon
Q212

The treatment of choice for silent stones in Gall bladder is: *(AI 1997)

  • A. Observation
  • B. Chenodeoxycholic acid
  • C. Cholecystectomy
  • D. Lithotripsy
Q213

Which of the following is not an indication for cholecystectomy: *(AIIMS May 05)

  • A. A 70 year old male with symptomatic gall stone
  • B. 20 year old male with sickle cell anemia and symptomatic gallstones
  • C. 65 year old female with a large gallbladder polyp
  • D. 55 year old with an asymptomatic gallstone
Q214

A 69 year old male patient having coronary artery disease was found to have gall bladder stones while undergoing a routine ultrasound of the abdomen. There was no history of biliary colic or jaundice at any time. What is the best treatment advice for such a patient for his gallbladder stones: *(AIIMS Nov 03, AI 03)

  • A. Open cholecystectomy
  • B. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy
  • C. No surgery for gallbladder stones
  • D. ERCP and removal of gallbladder stone is amenable
Q215

Medical treatment for gall bladder stone is amenable for: *(AI 1998)

  • A. Stones less than 15 mm in size
  • B. Radiopaque stone
  • C. Calcium bilirubinate oxalate stone
  • D. Nonfunctioning stones associated with gall bladder
Q216

All of the following are required for visualization of gall bladder in an oral cholecystogram except: *(AI 1997)

  • A. Motor mechanism of gall bladder
  • B. Patency of cystic duct
  • C. Ability to absorb water
  • D. Functioning hepatocytes
Q217

All are component of saint's triad except: *(AIIMS Nov 95)

  • A. Renal stones
  • B. Hiatus hernia
  • C. Diverticulosis coli
  • D. Gall stones
Q218

Charcot's triad includes all of the following except *(AI 1996)

  • A. Pain
  • B. Fever
  • D. Vomiting
Q219

Incorrect about Mirrizi syndrome: *(NBE)

  • A. Cystic duct obstruction
  • B. Jaundice
  • C. Cholecystocholedochal fistula
  • D. Hemobilia
Q220

Tokyo Guidelines are used for diagnosis and severity assessment of: *(NBE)

  • A. Acute Appendicitis
  • B. Acute Cholecystitis
  • C. Acute Pancreatitis
  • D. Irritable Bowel Syndrome
Q221

All of the following are known predisposing factors for cholangiocarcinoma except: *(AI 1997)

  • A. CBD stones
  • B. Clonorchis sinensis
  • C. Ulcerative colitis
  • D. Primary sclerosing cholangitis
Q222

Which of the following does not predispose to Cholangiocarcinoma *(AI 1996)

  • A. Ulcerative colitis
  • C. Choledochal cyst
  • D. Chronic pancreatitis
Q223

Risk factors for malignant change in an asymptomatic patient with a gall bladder polyp on ultrasound include all of the following, Except: *(AI 2009)

  • A. Age > 60 years
  • B. Rapid increase in size of polyp
  • C. Size of polyp > 5 mm
  • D. Associated Gall stones
Q224

All the following are features of Wilson's disease except: *(AI 1996)

  • A. Increased copper content in liver
  • B. Increased ceruloplasmin
  • C. Mental changes
  • D. Features of chronic active hepatitis
Q225

Wilson's diseases, features are all except: *(NBE)

  • A. Sunflower Cataract
  • B. Tremors
  • C. Pronounced sensory changes
  • D. Renal Calculi may be seen
Q226

All of the following statements about Wilson's disease are true, Except: *(NBE)

  • A. Autosomal dominant Inheritance
  • B. Hemolytic anemia
  • C. Low serum ceruloplasmin
  • D. Mutation on Chromosome 13
Q227

Which of the following statements about Wilson's disease is true: *(AI 2010)

  • A. Low serum ceruloplasmin and low urinary copper
  • B. Low serum ceruloplasmin and high urinary copper
  • C. High serum ceruloplasmin and low urinary copper
  • D. High serum ceruloplasmin and high urinary copper
Q228

A 12 years old girl with tremors and emotional liability has golden brown discolouration in liability has golden brown discolouration in Descement's membrane. The most likely diagnosis is- *(AI 04)

  • A. Fabry's disease
  • B. Wilson's disease
  • C. Glycogen storage disease
  • D. Acute Rheumatic fever
Q229

All of the following are features of wilson's disease, except- *(AIIMS Dec 97)

  • A. Hemolytic anemia
  • B. Testicular atrophy
  • C. Chorea
  • D. Chronic active hepatitis
Q230

Kayser-Fleischer rings (KF rings) are seen in: *(AI 2010)

  • A. Pterygium
  • B. Hematochromatosis
  • C. Wilson's disease
  • D. Menke's kinked hair syndrome
Q231

All of the following statements about Wilson's disease are true, EXCEPT- *(AIIMS May 04)

  • A. It is an autosomal recessive disorder
  • B. Serum ceruloplasmin level is < 20 mg/dl
  • C. Urinary copper excretion is <100 μg/dl
  • D. Zinc acetate is effective as maintenance therapy
Q232

A 14 year old boy presents wit recurrent episodes of hepatitis Ophthalmoscopic evalution reveals KF rings and serum ceruloplasmin levels are < 20 mg/dl. The treatment of choice for initial therapy is *(NBE)

  • A. Zinc
  • B. Pencillamine
  • C. Tetrathiomolybdate
  • D. Hepatic transplantation
Q233

Low serum copper due to ATP 7A gene is due to? *(NBE)

  • A. Dubin-Johnson's syndrome
  • B. Wilson disease
  • C. Menke disease
  • D. Gilbert's disease
Q234

A patient presents with Arthritis, hyperpigmentation of skin andhypogonadism, likely diagnosis is- *(AI 2001)

  • A. Hemochromatosis
  • B. Ectopic ACTH secreting tumour of the lung
  • D. Rheumatoid arthritis
Q235

Which of the following is least commonly involved organ system in Hemochromatosis: *(NBE)

  • A. Liver
  • B. Skin
  • C. Joints
  • D. Eye
Q236

A patient presents with fasting blood sugar of 167mg/dl, skin pigmentation and hypogonadism. His liver enzymes show SGOT as 670 and SGPT AS 690. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

  • A. Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency
  • B. Wilson's Disease
  • C. Hemochromatosis
  • D. Glycogen Storage Disease
Q237

Earliest phenotypic manifestation of Idiopathic hereditary hemochromatosis is- *(AIIMS May 07)

  • A. Post prandial increase in serum iron concentration
  • B. Elevated serum ferritin level
  • C. Slate grey pigmentation of skin
  • D. Increased transferrin saturation
Q238

The first test to become positive in patients with Hemochromatosis is: *(NBE)

  • A. Increased Serum Iron
  • B. Increased Serum Ferritin
  • C. Increased Transferin Saturation
  • D. Increased Liver Enzymes
Q239

Lallo, aged 54 years, who is a known diabetic patient develops cirrhosis. There is associated skin hyperpigmentation and restrictive cardiomyopathy which of the following is the best initial test to diagnose this case. *(AIIMS Nov 2000)

  • A. Iron binding capacity
  • B. Serum ferritin
  • C. Serum copper
  • D. Serum ceruloplasmin
Q240

Investigation of choice to confirm Hemochromatosis as the cause of cardiomyopathy is: *(NBE)

  • A. ECG
  • B. Echo
  • C. MRI
  • D. CT
Q241

All are seen in hemochromatosis except- *(NBE)

  • A. Hypogonadism
  • B. Arthropathy
  • C. Bronze diabetes
  • D. Desferrioxamine is the treatment of choice.

Hematology

500 Questions

▼
Q1

Which of the following conditions is associated with microcytic hypochromic anemia? *(AIIMS Dec 94)

  • A. Sickle cell Anemia
  • B. Thalassemia
  • C. Fanconi's anemia
  • D. Hereditary spherocytosis
Q2

All of the following cause microcytic hypochromic anaemia, except: *(AIIMS Dec 97)

  • A. Lead poisoning
  • C. Iron deficiency
  • D. Fanconi's anemia
Q3

Which of the following cause Microcytic Hypochromic anemia? *(AI 1995/AIIMS 1997)

  • B. Fanconi's Anemia
  • C. Sickle Cell Anemia
  • D. Hereditary Spherocytosis
Q4

Which of the following are not associated with microcytic hypochromic RRCs on Peripheral smear?

  • A. Iron Deficiency Anemia
  • B. Lead Poisoning
  • C. Sideroblastic anemia
  • D. Sickle Cell Anemia
Q5

Elevated serum ferritin, decreased serum iron and % transferring saturation are most consistent with the diagnosis of: *(NBE)

  • A. Iron deficiency anemia
  • C. Anemia of chronic disease
  • D. Sideroblastic anemia
Q6

Which of the following is classified as a Hyperproliferative anemia with a High Reticulocyte Count?

  • A. PNH
  • C. Thalassemia
  • D. Vitamin B12 Deficiency
Q7

RDW is an indication of which of the following: *(NBE)

  • A. Anisocytosis
  • B. Poikilocytosis
  • C. Macrocytosis
  • D. Mirocytosis
Q8

Which of the following Microcytic Anemias is associated with a High Red Cell Distribution Width?

  • B. Thalassemia Minor
  • C. Anemia of Chronic Disease
Q9

Which of the following is not expected in a case of Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia: *(NBE)

  • A. Reduced serum Iron
  • B. Reduced Total RBC distribution Width
  • C. Normal Ferritin levels
  • D. Increased TIBC
Q10

Which of the following Microcytic Anemias is associated with a Normal Free Eryhtocyte Protoporphyrin Levels? *(NBE)

  • D. Sideroblastic Anemia
Q11

Which of the following is not expected in a case of Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia *(NBE)

  • A. Reduced serum iron
  • B. Increased serum ferritin level
  • C. Decreased TIBC
  • D. Decreased Free Erythrocyte protoporphyrin
Q12

A patient presents with Microcytic Hypochromic anemia. Serum iron levels and TIBC are decreased. Likely diagnosis is: *(NBE)

  • B. Anemia of chronic disease
Q13

Anemia of Chronic disease can be differentiated from Iron deficiency anemia by: *(NBE)

  • A. ↑ TIBC and ↓ Ferritin
  • B. ↓ TIBC and ↑Ferritin
  • C. ↓ Saturation and ↓ Serum Iron
  • D. ↓ Ferritin ↑ Free Erythrocyte Protoporphyrin
Q14

A child presents with hypochromic microcytic anemia, with normal levels of free Erythrocyte Protoporphyrin The most likely diagnosis is: *(AI 92)

  • B. Lead toxicity
  • D. Anemia of chronic disease
Q15

Elevated serum ferritin, serum iron and percent transferrin saturation are most consistent with the diagnosis of : *(AI 2004)

  • D. Lead poisoning
Q16

Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia with an elevated Red Cell Count should suggest a diagnosis of:

  • C. Megaloblastic Anemia
  • D. Aplastic Anemia
Q17

Mentzer index more than 13 suggests a diagnosis of : *(NBE)

  • C. Hereditary Spherocytosis
  • D. Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia
Q18

A child has Hb-6.5 gm%, MCV-65, MCH-15 and protoporphyrin with red cell distribution width much less is most likely to be suffering from : *(AIIMS June 2000)

  • A. Thalassemia
  • B. Iron deficiency anaemia
  • C. Porphyria
  • D. Megaloblastic anaemia
Q19

A student from Punjab presents for evaluation of anemia. His RDW was observed to be 13.5 while the MCV was 62.5 fl. The likely diagnosis is *(NBE)

  • A. Hemoglobin Punjab (HB-D Trait)
  • B. Thalassemia Major
  • C. Iron Deficiency Anemia
  • D. Anemia of Chronic Disease
Q20

A 16 year old young girl presents with a history of fatiguability weakness and lethargy. Complete Blood picture (CBC) reveals Haemoglobin of 7.0, MCV of 70, MCH of 20 pg/cell and Red cell Distribution Width (RDW) of 20. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AI 2010)

  • C. Thalassemia Major
  • D. Sickle cell trait
Q21

A nine month old boy of Sindhi parents presented to you with complaints of progressive lethargy, irritability & pallor since 6 months of age. Examination revealed severe pallor. Investigation showed Hb-3.8 mg%; MCV-58 fl; MCH-19.4 pg/cell. Blood film shows anisocytosis, occasional target cells and increase in normoblasts. Osmotic fragility is normal. X-ray skull shows expansion of erythroid marrow. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? *(AI 2004)

  • B. Acute lymphoblastic anemia
  • C. Hemoglobin D disease
Q22

A 30 year old female being evaluated for anemia reveals the following indices: RBC count = 4.5 x 1012/L; MCV 55fl; TLC 8000. There is no history of blood transfusion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis: *(AIIMS Nov 2011)

  • B. Thalassemia major
  • C. Thalassemia minor
  • D. Megaloblastic anemia
Q23

All of the following are causes of iron deficiency anemia, Except: *(PGI June 01)

  • B. Celiac Sprue
  • C. Hookworms
  • D. Carcinoma colon
Q24

Erythropoetin therapy in patients with Chronic Renal Failure is associated with increased risk of

  • B. Megaloblastic Anemia
  • C. Anemia of chronic Disease
  • D. Hemolytic Anemia
Q25

A patient with anemia has the following indices. MCV = 60; Hb = 5gm%; MCHC = 20. The most likely cause of his anemia is: *(PGI Dec 2000)

  • A. Phenytoin
  • B. Blind loop syndrome
  • C. Hookworm infection
  • D. CRF
Q26

Iron absorption is increased in all except: *(PGI Dec 01, June 03)

  • A. Iron deficiency
  • B. Pregnancy
  • D. Alkaline pH of stomach
  • E. Ferrous iron salts
Q27

Signs and symptoms considered highly specific for iron deficiency INCLUDE ALL OF THE FOLLOWING, EXCEPT: *(NBE)

  • A. Pagophagia
  • B. Koilonychia
  • C. Blue Sclera
  • D. Macroglossia
Q28

Triad of Plummer Vinson Syndrome includes all of the following, except: *(NBE)

  • B. Dysphagia
  • C. Atrophic Glossitis
Q29

Which of the following is true for PlummerVinson syndrome true is- *(NBE)

  • A. Esophageal web of lower Esophagus
  • B. Megaloblastic anemia is a feature
  • C. Increases risk of squamous cell carcinoma ofEsophagus
  • D. Most common in young males
Q30

Which of the following findings is diagnostic of iron deficiency anemia : *(AI 2007)

  • A. Increased TIBC, decreased serum ferritin
  • B. Decreased TIBC, decreased serum ferritin
  • C. Increased TIBC, increased serum ferritin
  • D. Decreased TIBC, increased serum ferritin
Q31

All of the following are features of iron deficiency anemia, except: *(AIIMS MAY 2013)

  • A. Increased RDW
  • B. Decreased Serum Iron
  • D. Decreased Serum Ferritin

Which of the following is true about Iron Deficiency Anemia *(NBE)

  • A. Increased Serum Ferritin
  • B. Increased TIBC
  • C. Increased Transferrin Saturation
  • D. Macrocytic Hypochromic Anemia
Q33

Most sensitive and specific test for diagnosis of iron deficiency is: *(AI 2003, AI 2001)

  • A. Serum iron levels
  • B. Serum ferritin levels
  • C. Serum transferrin receptor population
  • D. Transferrin saturation
Q34

The earliest sign of iron deficiency anaemia: *(AIIMS Feb 1997)

  • A. Increase in iron binding capacity
  • B. Decrease in serum ferritin level
  • C. Decrease in serum iron level
  • D. All the above
Q35

Which of the following statements about iron deficiency anemia is true: *(NBE)

  • A. Decreased TIBC
  • B. Increased Ferritin levels
  • C. Bone marrow iron is decreased after serum iron is decreased
  • D. Bone marrow iron is decreased earlier than serum iron
Q36

Best marker for iron deficiency is: *(NBE 2012)

  • A. Serum iron
  • C. Total Iron Binding capacity
Q37

The earliest recognizable change of erythrocytes in iron-deficiency anemia is *(NBE)

  • B. Microcytosis
  • C. Hypochromia
  • D. Hypersegmentation
Q38

The most suitable test to assess iron stores is: *(NBE 2010)

  • B. Serum Ferritin
  • C. TIBC
Q39

How many milligrams of iron is usually present asiron stores in adult females? *(NBE)

  • A. 0- 300
  • B. 300 - 600
  • C. 600 - 900
  • D. 900 - 1200
Q40

Which is not seen in Iron deficiency anaemia: *(AI 2000)

  • A. Hyper-segmented neutrophils
  • B. Microcytosis preceeds hypochromia
  • C. MCHC < 50%
  • D. Commonest cause of anaemia in India
Q41

Which of the following best represents the sequence of events in Iron Deficiency anemia *(NBE)

  • A. Anisocytosis→Microcytosis→Hypochromia
  • B. Microcytosis→Hypochromia→Anisocytosis
  • C. Hypochromia→Microcytosis→Anisocytosis
  • D. Anisocytosis→Hypochromia→Microcytosis

All of the following statements about oral iron therapy are true, except: *(NBE)

  • A. Oral Ferrous sulfate, 325 mg three times daily may be used
  • B. Should be given with food to improve absorption
  • C. The maximum dose is 200 mg elemental iron daily
  • D. Vitamin C helps in its absorption
Q43

Which of the following is true about oral therapy for iron deficiency anemia: *(PGI Dec 05)

  • A. In 300 mg elemental iron given 100 mg get absorbed
  • B. Reticulocytosis appears in one to 2 weeks and then peaks in 3-4 weeks
  • C. Hemoglobin levels are usually corrected in six months of initiating therapy
  • D. Decrease in absorption with improvement of symptoms
  • E. Stop the Rx after normalizing the Hb

All of the following are indications of Intravenous Iron Administration, Except *(NBE)

  • A. Intermittent Gastrointestinal Blood Loss
  • B. Iron Malabsorption
  • C. Inability to Tolerate Oral Iron
  • D. Patients on Erythropoietin Therapy

Iron overload occurs in all, except : *(AIIMS Sept 96)

  • B. Myelodysplastic syndrome

Which of the following is true about Anemia of Chronic Disease: *(NBE)

  • B. Normal serum iron levels

Which of the following is seen in chronic inflammatory anemia *(NBE)

  • B. Serum iron ↓ S. ferritin ↑ and transferrin ↑

  • D. Serum iron ↓ S. ferritin ↓ and transferrin ↓

Anemia of chronic disease is characterized by all, except: *(AI 2000; AIIMS May 94)

  • A. Decreased serum iron
  • B. Increased total iron binding capacity (TIBC)
  • C. Increased serum ferritin
  • D. Increased macrophage iron in bone marrow

  • B. Anemia of Chronic Disease
  • C. Thalassemia Minor

  • C. Dialysis worsens anemia of renal failure
  • D. Anemia is proportional to the kidney disease

  • B. Iron deficiency

  • C. Pyridoxine supplementation can be used for treatment
  • D. MCV and MCHC is increased

Not present in Sideroblastic anaemia is : *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • B. Decreased transferrin saturation
  • C. Sideroblast cells in blood smear film

A patient presents with increased serum iron, decreased TIBC, increased percent saturation and increased serum ferritin. Most probable diagnosis is : *(AIIMS Nov. 2006)

  • B. Sideroblastic anemia
  • C. Iron deficiency anemia
  • D. Thalassemia minor

  • B. Hypothyroidism
  • C. Thiamine deficiency
  • D. Folic Acid Deficiency

  • A. Liver disease
  • B. Copper deficiency
  • D. Orotic aciduria

  • C. Absence of folic acid binding protein in serum

  • B. Pernicious anemia

  • C. Beta thalassemia
  • D. Iron deficiency anemia

  • C. Folic acid deficiency

All of the following cause intravascular hemolysis, Except: *(NBE)

Hemoglobinuria may be seen in all of the following conditions Except: *(NBE)

Q73

Haptoglobin levels are decreased in: *(AI 1996)

  • C. Hereditary spherocytosis
  • D. G 6 PD deficiency
Q75

All of the following are features of hemolytic anemia Except: *(PGI June 04)

  • A. Decreased RBC life span
  • B. Decreased Haptoglobin
  • C. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
  • D. Bilirubin in urine
  • E. Altered Erythroid and Myeloid ratio
Q76

All are seen in Hemolytic anemia except: *(AI 1996)

  • A. Hemosiderinuria
  • B. Reticulocytosis
  • C. Spherocytosis
  • D. Increased haptoglobin
Q77

Hemolytic anemia may be characterized by all of the following except- *(AIIMS May 05)

  • A. Hyperbilirubinemia
  • C. Hemoglobinuria
  • D. Increased plasma haptoglobin level
Q78

All are features of hemolytic anaemia except: *(AIIMS Dec 95)

  • A. Thrombocytopenia
  • B. Hemosiderinuria
  • C. Decreased haptoglobin
  • D. Raised indirect bilirubin
Q79

Features seen in hemolytic anemia are all except: *(AIIMS May 07)

  • A. Tear drop and Burr cells
  • B. ↓ Haptoglobin
  • C. Reticulocytosis
  • D. Hemoglobinuria

  • A. Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria
  • B. Following acute bleeding
  • D. Anemia in CRF

  • A. P.N.H
  • B. Hemolysis
  • C. Nutritional anemia
  • D. Dyserythropoietic syndrome

Reticulocytosis is NOT a feature of *(AIIMS Dec 94)

  • A. Antibodies attached to RBC Surface
  • B. Antibodies in the serum
  • C. Antigens attached to RBC Surface
  • D. Antigens in the serum

Direct Coomb's test detects: *(NBE)

The following protein defects can cause hereditary spherocytosis except : *(AI 07)

  • A. Ankyrin
  • B. Palladin
  • C. Glycophorin C
  • D. Anion transport protein

Features of hereditary spherocytosis include all of the following *except* : *(AI 1998) ↑ Osmotic fragility ↑ MCHC ↑ MCV Decrease surface area per unit volume

Which of the following is not seen in Hereditary Spherocytosis: *(NBE) Direct Coomb's Positive Increased Osmotic Fragility Splenomegaly Gall Stones

Q88

All of the following statement are true regarding his condition, except *(NBE)* Coomb's test should be done to exclude immunological cause Cholecystectomy may be required in future Most common mode of inheritance is Autosomal Recessive Splenectomy may be required

Q89

Usually associated with parvovirus B19 infection in those with hereditary spherocytosis: *(NBE) Mild to moderate splenomegaly Aplastic crisis Gallstones Hemolytic Crisis

Q90

Aplastic anemia in hereditary spherocytosis precipitated by- *(PGI Dec 05) Parvo virus HIV Adenovirus Influenza virus Measles virus

Q91

With regards to hereditary spherocytosis, which of the following is FALSE? *(NBE) Usually has autosomal dominant inheritance Caused by mutations in genes for proteins such as spectrin, ankrin or band 3 Red blood cells are destroyed in the spleen Aplastic crises are common

Q92

Osmotic fragility is increased in: *(PGI June 98) Sickle cell anemia Thalassemia Hereditary spherocytosis Chronic lead poisoning

Q93

Decreased osmotic fragility is seen in (select two options): *(PGI Dec 2000) Herediatry spherocytosis Sickle cell ds Autoimmune hemolytic anemia

Q94

Splenectomy is most useful in : *(AI 1998) Thrombocytopenia H.S. purpura

Q95

All of the following are true regarding Splenectomy in patients with Hereditary Spherocytosis, Except Avoid in mild cases Delay Splenectomy until at least 4 years old age Anti-pneumococcal Vaccination must be given before splenectomy Prolonged Anti-pneumococcal Antibiotic Prophylaxis must be given after splenectomy

Q96

G6PD Deficiency is associate with inability to: Oxidize NADPH Reduce NADP Oxidize NAD Reduce NAD

Q97

With regards to G6PD deficiency, which of the following is FALSE? *(NBE) Affects the pentose phosphate pathway Associated with neonatal jaundice Acute haemolysis can be precipitated by broad beans X-linked recessive disorder that does not affect heterozygous females

Q98

All of the following statements about genetics of G6PD Deficiency are true, except: *(NBE) X-linked Inheritance More severe in Men Contradicts Lyon Hypothesis May affect Heterozygous Females

Q99

Chronic Non – Spherocytic Hemolytic Anemia is seen in which class of G6PD Deficiency: *(NBE) Class I CLASS II Class III Class IV

Q100

Hemolysis in G6PD may be caused by all Expect: *(AI 2008) Primaquine Chloroquine Pyrimethamine Quinine

Q101

Which doesn't cause hemolysis in G6 PD deficiency: *(PGI Dec 99) Oestrogen Salicylates Nitrofurantoin

Q102

Primaquine may cause hemolysis in : *(AI 1998) G-6-PD deficiency NADP-deficiency Methemoglobin reductase deficiency Crabb's disease

Q103

The primary defect which leads to sickle cell anemia is- *(NBE) An abnormality in porphyrin part of hemoglobin Replacement of glutamate by valine in B-chain of HbA A nonsense mutation in the B-chain of HbA Substitution of valine by glutamate in the A chain of HbA

Q104

Which of the following is not seen in a chronic case of Sickle cell anemia : *(AI 1996) Hepatomegaly Pulmonary hypertension Cardiomegaly

Q105

All are true for sickle cell anemia, except *(AIIMS May 94) Pulmonary arterial hypertension Fish vertebra Leukopenia Increased size of heart

Q106

All of the following statements are true about sickle cell disease *except* *(AI 2004) Patient may require frequent blood transfusions Acute infection is the most common cause of mortality before 3 years of age. There is positive correlation between conc. HBS and polymerization of HBS. Patient presents early in life before 6 months of age.

Q107

All of the following are true about sickle cell disease, except: *(PGI June 2008) Mutation in α chain Symptoms ameliorated by HbF Venocclusive cries is cause of morbidity Bone pain is presenting feature

Q108

Commonest acute presentation of sickle cell anaemia is *(AIIMS Dec 98) Priapism Bone pain Fever

Q109

Heterozygous sickle cell anemia gives protection against: *(AI 2010) G6PD Malaria Dengue fever

Q110

Which of the following is not seen on hemoglobin electrophoresis in sickle cell anemia: *(AI 2001) HbA HbA2 HbF HbS

Q111

Sickle cell trait patient do not have manifestations as that of Sickle cell disease, because : *(AIIMS 50% HbS is required for occurrence of sickling HbA prevents sickling HbS is less than 50% & HbA has low affinity for HbS HbA prevents polymerization of Hbs

Q112

Crew haircut appearance in X-rays skull and Gandy gamma bodies are seen in- *(AIIMS Nov 93) Go-PD deficiency Hodgkin's lymphoma Sickle cell anaemia

Sickle cell Anemia leads to resistance towards *(DNB Dec 2006) P. Falciparum P. Ovale P. Malariae P. Vivax

Q115

Which of the following is a Quantitative defect in globin synthesis: *(NBE) Sickle Cell Hemoglobinopathy G6PD Deficiency Diamond-Blackfan Syndrome

Q116

In Beta thalassemia, there is : *(AIIMS May 01) Increase in beta chain, decrease in alpha chain Decrease in beta chain, increase in alpha chain Decrease in beta chain, decrease in alpha chain Increase in beta chain, increase in alpha chain

Q117

The most common mutations in Beta thalassemia involves: *(NBE) Intron-1 Intron-22 619 bp deletion 3.7 bp deletion

Q118

What is the most common type of mutation causing alpha thalassemia? *(NBE) Gene duplication Large deletion Point mutation Trinucleotide repeat expansion

Q119

He gives a history of similar presentation in the past which wave adequately managed by a single blood transfusion. He has not required any further blood transfusions till date. Most probable diagnosis is: *(NBE)* Thalassemia Minor Thalassemia intermedia

Q124

All are true about β - thalassemia trait Except: *(PGI June 04) ↑ HbF ↑HbA2 Microcytosis Severe anaemia

The most important diagnostic feature for beta thalassemia trait: *(NBE) raised HbF reduced MCH reduced MCV raised HbA2

Q127

True about α-thalassemia trait : *(PGI June 06) Increased HbF Increased HbA2 Severe anemia

True about aplastic anemia is all Except: *(PGI June 05) Reticulocytopenia Neutropenia

True about polycythemia rubra vera is all Except: *(PGI Dec 97) ↓ed ESR Infection

In Polycythemia vera, all the following are seen except : *(AIIMS May 01) Increased GI bleed Transient visual loss

Diagnosis of Hodgkins disease is based on all of the following, except: *(PGI Dec 1999) Reed- Sternberg cells Reactive cellular background Atypical cells in background CD 30 positivity

Most common lymph node involved in Hodgkin's lymphoma is *(NBE) Inguinal Cervical Axillary Sub-clavicular

All of the following are poor prognostic factors for Hodgkin's disease, Except: *(PGI Dec 01) Younger age Systemic manifestations Mediastinal disease Stomach involvement

The patient can be staged as having: *(AI 1997)* Stage II A Stage II B

Q253

Intermediate grade of NHL are all except : *(NBE) Diffuse small cell cleaved Diffuse large cell Mycosis fungoides Diffuse mixed

Q254

Most malignant form of NHL is : *(NBE) Small cell lymphocytic lymphoma Follicular cleavage Large cell follicular

In Burkitis lymphoma, translocation seen is chromosome *(AIIMS May 01) 12 - 14 translocation 8 - 14 translocation 4 - 8 translocation 12 - 18 translocation

  • A. Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)
  • B. Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)
  • C. T cell - ALL
  • D. Burkitt's Lymphoma
Q260

Burkitt's Lymphoma is associated with which of the following viruses *(NBE)

  • B. HTLV-1
  • C. HHV-8
  • D. Adenovirus
Q261

Burkitt's Lymphoma is associated with: *(AI 2010)

  • A. t(8;14)
  • B. t(11;14)
  • C. t(15;17)
  • D. t(14;18)
Q262

All of the following statements about Burkitt's lymphoma are true, Except: *(PGI June 02)

  • A. B cell lymphoma
  • B. t 8,14 translocation
  • C. Can present as an abdominal mass
  • D. Radiotherapy is the treatment of choice
Q263

All of the following statements about Hairy cell leukemia are true except: *(AI 2004)

  • A. Splenomegaly is conspicuous
  • B. Results from an expansion of neoplastic T lymphocytes
  • C. Cells are positive for Tartrate Resistant Acid phosphatase
  • D. The cells express CD25 consistently
Q264

'Hairy cell leukemia' is Neoplastic proliferation of: *(AI 1999)

  • A. T cells
  • B. B cells
  • C. Myeloid cells
  • D. Macrophages
Q265

CD 19 positive, CD22 positive, CD103 positive monoclonal B-cells with bright kappa positivity were found to comprise 60% of the peripheral blood lymphoid cells on flow cytometric analysis in a 55 year old man with massive splenomegaly and a total leucocyte count of 3.3 x 10⁹/L. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis? *(AIIMS Nov. 2004)

  • A. Splenic lymphoma with villous lymphocytes
  • B. Mantle cell lymphoma
  • C. B-cell prolymphocytic leukemia
  • D. Hairy cell leukemia
Q266

Which of the following is the most specific marker of Hairy Cell Leukemia in fixed tissue specimens

  • A. TRAP
  • B. CD5
  • C. CD11c
  • D. AnnexinA1
Q267

Which of the following is the most common clinical examination finding in Hairy Cell Leukemia

  • A. Lymphadenopathy
  • B. Splenomegaly
  • C. Hepatomegaly
  • D. Mediastinal Mass
Q268

Which of the following is NOT used in treatment of hairy cell leukemia: *(AIIMS Feb 97)

  • A. Steroid
  • B. Pentostatin
  • C. Splenectomy
  • D. Alpha-interferon
Q269

Treatment of choice in hairy cell leukemia is: *(NBE)

  • B. Cladribine
  • D. Rituximab
Q270

Mantle Cell Lymphoma is a lymphoma of *(NBE)

  • A. Precursor B Cells
  • B. Mature B cells
  • C. Precursor T Cells
  • D. Mature T Cells
Q271

Characteristic translocation in mantle cell lymphoma is: *(NBE)

  • A. t (8; 14)
  • B. t (14; 18)
  • C. t (11; 18)
  • D. t (11; 14)
Q272

Lymphomatosis polyposis in the large intestine is common in *(NBE)

  • A. Mantle Cell Lymphoma
  • B. Hairy Cell Leukemia
  • C. Burkitt's Lymphoma
  • D. Follicular Cell Lymphoma
Q273

A 63 year old man presents with splenomegaly and lymphadenopathy. Immunophenotype was positive for CD5, CD19, CD79b and FMC7. The most likely diagnosis is: *(AIIMS May 01)

  • A. Hairy cell leukemia
  • B. Mantle cell Lymphoma (MCL)
  • C. Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL)
  • D. Follicular Lymphoma
Q274

Mantle cell lymphomas are positive for all of the following, except *(NBE)

  • A. CD 23
  • B. CD 19
  • C. CD 5
  • D. CD 79B
Q275

A 63-year old man presented with massive splenomegaly, lymphadenopathy and a total leucocyte count of 17000 per mm³. The flow cytometry showed CD 19 positive, CD 5 positive, CD 23 negative, monoclonal B-cells with bright kappa positivity comprising 80% of the peripheral blood lymphoid cells. The most likely diagnosis is: *(NBE)

Q276

Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) is associated with: *(AI 2003)

  • A. Individuals > 50 years of age
  • B. Mature small lymphocytes in peripheral smear
  • C. Hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy
Q277

80 year old, asymptomatic man present with a Total Leucocyte Count of 1 lakh, with 80% lymphocytes and 20% PMC's. What is the most probable diagnosis? *(AI 2005, 2007)

  • A. HIV
  • B. CML
  • C. CLL
  • D. TB
Q278

All of the following are true about Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia, Except: *(PGI 2008)

  • A. Diagnosed on routine blood tests
  • B. Leukocytosis is prominent
  • C. T lymphocyte CLL is more common
  • D. Can present as acute leukemia
Q279

A 48 years of woman was admitted with a history of weakness for two months. On examination cervical lymph nodes were found enlarged and spleen was palpable 2 cm below the costal margin. Her hemoglobin was 10.5g/dl. Platelet count 237x10⁹/L, and total leucocyte count 40x10⁹/L, which include 80% mature lymphoid cell with coarse clumped chromatin. Bone marrow revealed a nodular lymphoid infiltrate. The peripheral blood lymphoid cells were positive for CD19, CD5, CD20 and were negative for CD79B and FMC(-7). Which one of the following statements is not true about this disease? *(AI 2005, 2007)

  • A. Trisomy 12 correlates an aggressive clinical course
  • B. Abnormalities of 13q 14 are associated with long term survival
  • C. Case with t(11;14) translocation is present in most of the cases
  • D. t(11;14) translocation is present in most of the cases
Q280

A 48 year old women was admitted with a history of weakness for two months. On examination, hemoglobin was 10.5 g/dl, platelet count 2.7x10⁹/L and total leucocyte count 40x10⁹/L, which included 80% mature lymphoid cells with coarse clumped chromatin. Bone marrow revealed nodular lymphoid infiltrate. The peripheral blood lymphoid cells were positive for CD 19, CD 5, CD20 and CD23 and were negative for CD79B and FMC-7. The histopathological examination of the lymph node in this patient will most likely exhibit effacement of lymph node architecture by *(AI 2005)

  • A. A pseudofollicular pattern
  • B. A monomorphic lymphoid proliferation with a nodular pattern.
  • C. A predominantly follicular pattern
  • D. A diffuse proliferation of medium to large lymphoid cells with high mitotic rate.
Q281

A 48 year old woman was admitted with a history of weakness for two months. On examination, cervical lymph nodes were found enlarged and spleen was palpable 2 cm platelet count 237 X 109/L and total leucocyte count 40 x coarse clumped chromatin. Bone marrow revealed a nodular lymphoid infiltrate. The peripheral blood lymphoid were negative for CD 19, CD5, CD 20 and CD 23 and were negative for CD 79 B and FMC - 7. What is the most likely diagnosis? *(AIIMS Nov. 05)

  • A. T-cell rich B - cell lymphoma with leukemic spill over in blood.
  • B. Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
  • C. Mantle cell lymphoma
  • D. A definite diagnosis can not be made in this patient without lymph node biopsy
Q282

In Ritcher's transformation CLL transforms into: *(NBE)

  • A. Large B cell Lymphoma
  • B. Anaplastic carcinoma
  • C. Burkitt lymphoma
  • D. Lymphoproliferative lymphoma
Q283

All of the following statements about treatment of typical CLL are true, except : *(AIIMS May 2013)

  • A. Treatment should be initiated as soon as diagnosis is established
  • B. Chlorambucil and Fludarabine are the most commonly used agents
  • C. Fludarabine is preferred in the young and Chlorambucil is preferred in the elderly
  • D. Young patients are candidates for Bone marrow transplantation
Q284

All of the following statements about Philadelphia Chromosome in CML are true, except: *(AIIMS Nov 2013)

  • A. Reciprocal translocation between long arm of chromosome 9 and long arm of chromosome 22
  • B. The breakpoint cluster region (BCR) is located on the long arm of chromosome 22
  • C. The ABL gene is located on chromosome 9
  • D. The Philadelphia chromosome with the oncogenic BCR-ABL gene is found on chromosome 9
Q285

Peripheral smear with increased neutrophils, basophils, and platelets with normal red cell morphology is highly suggestive of- *(AIIMS May 06)

  • A. Acute myeloid leukemia
  • B. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia
  • C. Chronic myelogenous leukemia
  • D. Myelodysplastic syndrome
Q286

A 60 year old man presented with fatigue, weight loss and heaviness in left hypochondrium for 6 months. The hemogram showed Hb. 10gm/dL, TLC 5 lakhs/mm³, platelet count 4 lakhs/mm³, DLC: neutrophil 55%, lymphocytes 4%, monocytes 2%, eosinophils 6%, metamyelocytes 10%, myelocytes 18%, promyelocytes 2% and blast 3%. The most likely cytogenetic abnormality in this case is: *(AIIMS May 03)

  • A. t (1; 21)
  • B. t (9; 22)
  • C. t (15; 17)
  • D. Trisomy 21
Q287

In a patient suffering from chronic myeloid leukemia, Hb falls from 11g% to 4g% in a short span of time, and splenomegaly occurs. The cause could be (select two options)- *(PGI June 02)

  • A. CML IN Chronic Phase
  • B. CML in blast crisis
  • C. Ineffective erythropoiesis
  • D. Myelofibrosis
Q288

Which one of the following is not a criterion for making a diagnosis of chronic myeloid leukemia in accelerated phase : *(AIIMS Nov 2004)

  • A. Blasts 10-19% of WBC's in peripheral blood
  • B. Basophils 10-19% of WBC'S in peripheral blood
  • C. Increasing spleen size unresponsive to therapy
  • D. Persistent thrombocytosis (>1000 x 10⁹/L) unresponsive to therapy
Q289

Leukocyte alkaline phosphate is increased in all, except *(AIIMS May 94)

  • A. Polycythemia vera
  • C. Myelofibrosis
  • D. Myeloid metaplasia
Q290

Which of these is true regarding CML : *(AIIMS 1999)

  • A. Size of splenomegaly indicates prognosis
  • B. Phagocytic activity of WBC is reduced
  • C. Sudan black stain is specific for myeloblast
  • D. Myeloblast, granuloblast and lymphoblast become PH chromosome +ve. -ve following remission
Q291

All of the following are features of juvenile CML except: *(AI 94)

  • B. Fetal Hb is increased
  • C. Philadelphia chromosome is positive
  • D. Lymphadenopathy
Q292

Haemoglobin F is raised in: *(AIIMS Dec 97)

  • A. Juvenile chronic myeloid leukemia
  • B. Hereditary spherocytosis
  • C. Congenital red cell aplasia
  • D. Myasthenia gravis
Q293

Which of the following conditions is associated with raised levels of FoetalHaemoglobin (HBF):

  • A. Juvenile CML
  • B. Hereditary Spherocytosis
  • D. Myasthenia Gravis
Q294

Initial treatment recommended for newly diagnosed patient with CML is: *(NBE)

  • A. Allogenic Bone Marrow Transplantation
  • B. Imatinib Mesylate Therapy
  • C. TNF-α
  • D. IFN-α
Q295

Chemotherapeutic Agent of Choice for the treatment of CML is: *(NBE)

  • A. Imatinib
  • B. Vincristine
  • C. Cyclophosphamide
  • D. Methotrexate
Q296

Drug of choice for chronic myeloid Leukemia (CML) is: *(AI 2008)

  • A. Hydroxyurea
  • B. Imatinib
  • C. Infliximab
  • D. IFN
Q297

Drug of choice for treatment of CML is: *(NBE)

  • B. Hydroxyurea
  • C. IFN-Alfa
  • D. Infliximab
Q298

Best Rx for CML is: *(PGI Dec 98)

  • A. Autologous BMT
  • B. Allogenic BMT
  • C. Alpha Interferon
  • D. Hydroxyurea
Q299

Treatment of chronic phase of CML in pregnantwomen with hyper-leukocytosis in First trimesteris-

  • B. Leukapheresis
  • C. Interferon Alpha
Q300

Which of the following is not compatible with a diagnosis of chronic myelomonocytic leukemia?

  • A. Peripheral blood monocytosis more than 1x10⁹/L
  • B. Absence of Philadelphia chromosome
  • C. More than 20% blasts in blood or bone marrow
  • D. Absent or minimal dysplasia in myeloid lieages.
Q301

Mycosis Fungoides is a *(NBE)

  • A. Cutaneous B Cell Lymphoma
  • B. Cutaneous T Cell Lymphoma
  • C. Peripheral B Cell Lymphoma
  • D. Peripheral T Cell Lymphoma
Q302

Peripheral B Cell Lymphoma *(NBE)

  • A. Sarcoidosis
  • C. Mycosis fungoides
  • D. Pityriasis Lichenoides Chronica
Q303

Clinical stages of Classical mycosis fungoides include all of the following except *(NBE)

  • A. Patch
  • B. Nodular
  • C. Plaque
  • D. Tumor
Q304

Mycosis fungoides which is not true *(AI 2007)

  • A. It is the most common form of cutaneous lymphoma
  • B. Pautriers microabscess
  • C. Indolent course and easily amenable to treatment
  • D. Erythroderma seen and spreads to peripheral circulation
Q305

Which of the following is the most common Hematological Malignancy in Childhood *(NBE)

  • A. ALL
  • B. AML
  • D. CML
Q306

The most common type of ALL is: *(NBE)

  • A. Pre B cell ALL
  • B. T cell ALL
  • C. B cell ALL
  • D. Biphenotypic ALL
Q307

Which ONE of these is least likely to be considered in the differential diagnosis of acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL) in a child? *(NBE)

  • A. Aplastic anaemia
  • B. Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia
  • C. Neuroblastoma
  • D. Acute myeloid leukaemia
Q308

Which ONE of these is the most accurate description of the pattern of incidence of acute lymphoblastic leukaemia? *(NBE)

  • A. Peak in first year of life, secondary rise after age of 10
  • B. Peak in first 2-5 years of life and gradual rise after age 50 years
  • C. Peak at age 5-10 years and secondary peak at age 60 years
  • D. Peak in adolescence and then gradual decline
Q309

Which ONE of these is the most likely clinical presentation of a child with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia? *(NBE)

  • A. Respiratory distress
  • B. Abdominal pain resulting from swollen spleen
  • C. Lymphadenopathy
  • D. Recent history of tiredness and bruising
Q310

Which of the following presents as mediastinal enlargement: *(NBE)

  • A. Promyelocytic Leukemia
  • C. ALL
  • D. Diffuse histiocytic lymphoma
Q311

A17-year-old boy presented with TLC of 138x10⁹/L with 80% blasts on the peripheral smear. Chest X- ray demonstrated a large mediastinal mass. Immunophenotyping of this patient's blasts would most likely demonstrate: *(AIIMS May 2006)

  • A. No surface antigens (null phenotype)
  • B. An immature T cell phenotype (Tdt/CD34/CD7 positive)
  • C. Myeloid markers, such as CD13,CD33 and Cdi5
  • D. B cell markers, such as CD19,CD20 and CD22

  • A. Hyperdiploidy
  • B. Female sex
  • C. Pre B cell ALL
  • D. t (12; 21) translocation

Good prognostic Factors for ALL include all of the following, Except: *(NBE)

Q314

  • A. Total Leukocyte count 4000-100,000
  • B. Age < 2 years
  • C. Testicular involvement
  • D. Blasts in peripheral smear
Q315

Which of the following is a good prognostic factor in ALL *(PGI 2008)

  • A. High WBC count
  • B. Male sex
  • C. Age < 2 years
  • D. Hyperdiploidy

Lasparaginase is particularly used in which type of leukemia- *(AI 1996)

  • A. AML
  • D. CLL

Treatment of choice in intracranial ALL is: *(PGI June 99)

  • B. Vincristine and predisolone

In which of the following types of leukemia is methotrexate administered for CNS prophylaxis: *(PGI June 02)

  • A. Methotrexate
  • B. All - trans retinoic acid
  • C. Prednisolone
  • D. L-Asparaginase
  • E. Vincristine

  • A. Asparaginase
  • B. Amylase
  • C. Lipase
  • D. Transaminase
Q321

Most common type of acute myeloid leukemia: *(NBE)

  • A. M2
  • B. M3
  • C. M4
  • D. M5
Q322

Which of the following is a poor prognostic factor in Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) *(AIIMS Nov

  • A. Monosomy
  • B. Deletion of X or Y chromosome
  • C. t (8; 21) translocation
  • D. Nucleophosmin mutation

Auer Rods are most frequently seen in which subtype of AML *(NBE)

  • A. M0
  • C. M5
  • D. M7
Q324

Auer Rods are typically not seen in: *(NBE)

  • A. AML - M0
  • B. AML - M2
  • C. AML - M3
  • D. AML - M1

All of the following are poor prognostic factors for acute myeloid leukemias, except: *(AI 2003)

  • A. Age more than 60 years
  • B. Leucocytes count more than 1,00,000/μl
  • C. Secondary leukemias
  • D. Presence of t(8;21)
